- How to MAKE FRIENDS as an ADULT: the best way to make new friends: Kind: captions Language: en I'm Dr Orion taraban and this is cycax Better Living Through psychology and the topic of today's short talk is how to make friends as an adult so this is an extremely important skill the ability to make friends and it gets harder to do as you age it's not always easy to make friends in high school and college but it will never be easier to make friends than it is in high school and college and this is because you are more or less forced to spend a great deal of time with people your own age and you don't have a lot of other things to do or a lot of other responsibilities and so your free time defaults to your cohort and For Better or For Worse these folks that you become friends with in high school or college tend to be your foundational friends and you may stay friends with them for the rest of your lives as they say you can't make new old friends the issue is not so much that these friends have falling outs though that can happen but that they slowly become less important as their lives diverge and this is particularly true for men maintaining their friendships is something that women generally do better than men not only because they seem to enjoy emotional connection for its own sake more than men do but because they kind of play 4D chess with their social relationships as well like a woman will take a girlfriend out to lunch because she thinks that if she allows her to vent about her husband in two months she'll be more likely to accept an invitation to her baby shower something like that women see how putting the time in now might come in handy later and they enjoy spending time unless goal-oriented contexts don't get me wrong I'm not saying that men don't value their friends the platonic relationship between two male friends might be one of the strongest and purest relationships on this planet it's more like men are socially incentivized and rewarded for prioritizing other things above their friendships for instance my best friend lives in the same metropolitan area that I do but I see him like twice a year and that's not because I'm not making the effort to get together his story is that he spends half of his Waking Life working and the other half of his Waking Life with his wife and kids fortunately he seems mostly content with his life however this is the story of millions of men over time their friends kind of become the 10th most important thing in their lives which functionally reduces them to insignificance and I suppose this is fine as long as the man is happy and as long as the situation works what can happen however is that a guy can run into financial trouble maybe he gets hurt maybe he loses his job and that in turn destabilizes his relationship which can lead to divorce and just like that the two pillars of his life to which he has devoted the last 20 years of his life work and marriage can topple possibly even through no fault of his own and because his entire social network was his colleagues and his wife's friends these men can find themselves desperate and alone and this is one of the primary reasons why suicide rates in men are so high following a divorce the protective factor of a support system is one of the things that tends to make women more resilient in the face of setbacks for instance the demographic with the lowest suicide rates historically has been black women and this is not because these folks tend to suffer fewer hardships than others the general consensus here is that their tight-knit social networks can help them through their darker moments in any case it's important for men to actively maintain their friendships throughout their lifespan but it can be hard if you only depend on your old friends friends who may have moved away and are now busy with their careers and their families so what do you do now before I give you the answer if you're liking what you're hearing please consider sending this episode to someone who might benefit from its message because it's Word of Mouth referrals like this that really help to make the channel grow and you can also tip me by hitting the super thanks buttons the three little dots in the lower right hand corner uh you can tip me in proportion to the value that you believe that you've received from this message because it's your support that makes all of this happen and I really appreciate your help now I have a Surefire way to make friends as an adult if you follow this advice I can almost guarantee that you will have a few close friends in three months time which is actually pretty incredible here's the hack to making friends as an adult the way to make friends as an adult is to enroll in some kind of training or certification program this could be a 200 hour yoga teacher training program this could be a cordon bleu pastry chef certification this could be a Wilderness survival program it can really be anything as long as it's connected to some form of achievement let me break down how this works first of all the fastest way to build relationships is to undergo a shared trial that's why the closest bestest buddies in the world are war buddies it's the guys that stand shoulder to shoulder with you in the trenches with whom you form deep and abiding friendships undergoing a shared trial brings people together as it functionally forces them to lean on each other in order to overcome the trial so by entering into a period of apprenticeship where you will be asked to perform a great deal in a short amount of time you will be undergoing a shared difficulty with your fellow aspirins what's more these programs tend to be very intensive and this intensity works in your favor here to make a friend you actually need to spend time with the other person let's say you try to make a buddy by going to the Wednesday night Jiu Jitsu class at the local Dojo every week in a year you could have spent around 50 hours together not bad but if you were to enroll in some kind of training camp you might be able to spend a couple hundred hours together in the span of a few months thus you could accomplish in four months what might otherwise have taken you four years these programs offer a concentrated opportunity to bond with other people undergoing a shared trial in a shorter amount of time allowing you to leave with some close friends in a matter of weeks instead of years and because it's tied to some kind of achievement or certification you're not going to encounter the kind of flakiness or non-investment that plagues casual classes when there is an enrollment cost and a personally relevant goal at stake people tend to be much more committed they show up regularly which is important as you can't really build a relationship with someone who comes and goes and because there's a goal involved these programs often attract a higher caliber of person than those you might find just hanging around at the local pub after all these are people who like you are interested in some form of self-development which is why they have invested in a particular certification program so that's the easiest way to make friends as an adult enroll in some kind of intensive time limited program connected to a form of achievement and ideally this program should concern itself with something you actually enjoy like if you don't care about yoga don't sign up for the yoga teacher training program this is especially true for men who tend to bond side by side as opposed to face to face male bonding often requires at least the pretext of an activity even if it's just watching the game programs create a reason for men to get together and connect so have fun what do you think does this fit with your own experience let me know in the comments below and if you've gotten this far you might as well like this episode And subscribe to this channel you may also become consider becoming a channel member with perks like the priority review of comments or booking a paid consultation as always thank you for listening

  14. @leonelleonardis9564: Making friends as an adult is challenging, especially for men, because societal incentives often prioritize work and family over maintaining friendships (1:06). Dr. Orion Taraban explains that while high school and college naturally foster friendships due to proximity and shared experiences (0:20-0:48), these foundational relationships can weaken as lives diverge (0:52). Men, in particular, may neglect friendships, which can lead to isolation during crises like divorce, increasing vulnerability to mental health issues (2:49-3:20).

Dr. Taraban proposes a "surefire" method for adults to make new friends quickly: enrolling in an intensive training or certification program (4:56). This approach works because:

Shared Trial: Undergoing a difficult experience together, similar to "war buddies," creates deep bonds and forces people to rely on each other (5:23).
Concentrated Time: These programs are intensive, allowing individuals to spend hundreds of hours together in a few months, accelerating the bonding process that might otherwise take years (6:02).
Commitment and Quality: The enrollment cost and personal goal involved ensure participants are committed and show up regularly, fostering consistent interaction (6:50). Such programs also attract individuals interested in self-development, potentially leading to higher-quality connections (7:18).
This method is particularly effective for men, as it provides an activity-based pretext for bonding, which aligns with how men often form relationships (7:56).

- I Visited My Second Home To Rent It Out And Found My Sister Living There With Her 3 Kids...: Kind: captions Language: en I stared at the ruins of my sanctuary. 20 minutes ago, this was a climate controlled restoration gallery in downtown Chicago, a space of silence and precision, where I brought 17th century oil paintings back to life. Now, it looked like a landfill. Cardboard boxes stamped with the logo of a cheap supplement company were stacked 6 feet high, blocking the fire exits. Packing peanuts covered the floor like toxic snow. And there, sitting on a $50,000 Persian rug I had spent three months restoring, was my older sister, Nicole. She was eating a meatball sub, and a glob of tomato sauce was currently dripping onto the silk fibers. My name is Lauren, and I deal with fragile things for a living. But as I watched Nicole wipe her greasy hands on her jeans and smirk at me, I knew nothing in this room was safe. "Mom gave me permission," Nicole said, her mouth full. She said I needed a warehouse for my business and you weren't using the space anyway, so we moved in. If you don't like it, call the police. She actually laughed then, a small, arrogant sound that echoed off the high ceilings. She thought she was untouchable. She thought the word family was a magic spell that granted her immunity from consequences. She was wrong. Drop a comment and let me know where you're listening from and what time it is for you right now. I'd love to know who's part of our community. Chapter 2. The architect of silence. I didn't scream. I didn't throw a fit. I didn't give her the satisfaction of seeing my pulse rise, even a single beat. My profession requires a steady hand. One tremor can destroy a masterpiece. And right now, my life was the canvas I needed to protect. I am an art restorer. My world is built on chemistry, patience, and absolute control. I work with solvents that can strip paint or burn skin if mishandled. I understand that some damage is reversible, but some rot must be cut out entirely. Nicole was the rot. She was waiting for the explosion. That's how narcissists operate. They feed on your reaction. They want you to cry, to yell, to beg because it validates their power over you. If I shouted, she would play the victim. She would call our parents and sob about how her mean little sister was bullying her. She wanted fuel. I decided to give her nothing. I looked past her at the chaos she had created. My eyes scanned the room, cataloging the damage like an insurance adjuster, the blocked exits, the food waste near archival materials, the unauthorized commercial activity. It wasn't a family dispute anymore. It was a series of lease violations and safety hazards. In psychology, there is a technique called the grey rock method. You become as uninteresting and unyielding as a stone. You offer no emotional surface for the toxic person to latch on to. You don't argue. You observe. You don't defend. You document. I looked Nicole dead in the eyes. Her smirk faltered for a second when she didn't see the rage she expected. She saw nothing but a mirror reflecting her own mess. "Okay," I said. My voice was flat, devoid of any inflection. "Okay," she repeated, blinking. "That's it. That's it," I said. I turned around and walked out the door. I didn't slam it. I closed it with a soft, decisive click. I could hear her laughing behind me, probably texting our mother that I had rolled over, that I was weak. She had no idea. She thought I was walking away to cry. I was walking away to burn her world to the ground legally, methodically, and without a single tear. Chapter 3. The investment of blood. To understand why I walked away, you have to understand the dinner that happened two weeks ago. It was a Sunday roast at my parents house in the suburbs. The kind of obligatory family gathering that feels less like a meal and more like a hostage negotiation. I had come with good news. I had just secured a contract with a museum in London to restore a collection of water damaged Victorian portraits. It was the break of a career. It required a $300,000 investment to set up a satellite lab. But I had a plan. I would rent out my Chicago loft. It was a unique property, industrial chic and high security, easily commanding $18,000 a month on the corporate rental market. I laid the numbers out on the table between the pot roast and the mashed potatoes. I was proud. I thought for a split second that they might be proud, too. Then Nicole spoke up. She didn't congratulate me. She looked at our mother and said, "Perfect. That solves my warehouse problem." Nicole sells supplements, not the kind you buy in a pharmacy, but the kind you buy from a friend on Facebook who promises they will cure your anxiety and make you rich. She calls herself a CEO. I call it a pyramid scheme. She had boxes of inventory rotting in her garage, and she decided my loft was the upgrade her brand needed. I can move the inventory in next week, Nicole said, reaching for a roll. It'll save me storage fees, and the address will look great on my business cards. I laughed, thinking it was a joke. It wasn't. My mother set down her fork. That's a wonderful idea, Nicole. Lauren, you're going to be in London anyway. Your sister needs a leg up. It's the Christian thing to do. I looked at them. I looked at the $18,000 a month I needed to fund my dream. And I looked at my parents demanding I set it on fire to keep Nicole warm. There is a concept in economics called the sunk cost fallacy or the gamblers's fallacy. It is when a gambler keeps betting on a losing hand because they have already lost so much that they cannot bear to walk away. They think if they just put in one more chip, the luck will turn. My parents are gamblers. Nicole is their bad bet. They have poured decades of money, emotional labor, and excuses into her. She has failed at college, failed at three marriages, and failed at five businesses. Admitting she is a failure would mean admitting their parenting failed. They can't do that. So instead, they look at me. the solvent child, the one who worked and saved and built, and they see me as a fresh stack of chips. They don't love Nicole more. They are just more terrified of her failing. I am the collateral they are willing to burn to prove they were right about her potential. No, I said at the table, the loft is a business asset. I have tenants lined up. I am not turning a multi-million dollar property into a storage unit. My father slammed his hand on the table. You are selfish, Lauren. Your sister is drowning and you are building a yacht. I left that dinner early. I thought that was the end of it. I thought saying no was enough. I forgot that to a gambler. A no was just a bluff they haven't called yet. Chapter 4. The solvent hazard. The timeline brings us back to this morning. 20 minutes before I found Nicole eating a meatball sub on my rug. I stood in the hallway outside my loft adjusting my blazer. Next to me was Logan, a private security specialist who managed the building, and the Morose, a husband and wife executive team relocating from Paris. They were the ideal tenants. They had impeccable credit. They appreciated art, and they had already wired a deposit based on the photos alone. The light in the main gallery is north, I told Madame Maro. Perfect for consistent illumination without UV damage, and the climate control is hospital grade. I was selling them a dream of precision and elegance. I put my key in the lock. It didn't turn. That's odd, I muttered. I tried again. The mechanism felt jammed, sticky. I apologized and knocked. There was no answer, just the muffled sound of a television and thumping. Logan stepped forward. Let me try the master override. He keyed in the code. The lock disengaged with a heavy clunk and the door swung open. The smell hit us first. It wasn't the clean scent of linseed oil and varnish I was used to. It was the smell of stale fast food, unwashed bodies, and cloying cheap perfume. Then the visual shock. My breath hitched in my throat. The loft was a sweat shop. Nicole's three children, ages 6 to 10, were sitting on the floor in a circle, stuffing plastic bottles into cardboard mailers. They looked miserable. But what stopped my heart wasn't the child labor. It was where they were sitting. They were 3 ft away from my chemical storage rack. Art restoration involves dangerous substances, acetone, tuane, industrial solvents. I kept them in sealed fireproof cabinets, but the cabinet doors were wide open. A bottle of solvent was sitting on the floor next to a juice box. One spark, one spill, and this entire building could go up in toxic fumes. Montier, Madame Maro whispered. She pressed a handkerchief to her nose, looking at the scene with absolute horror. This is Msieur Maro began, then shook his head. This is not as advertised. I watched them back away toward the elevator. I didn't try to stop them. What could I say? Ignore the unauthorized commercial operation and the fire hazards. They were gone. That was $432,000 of rental income walking out the door. Logan looked at me, his face pale. Lauren, is that is that acetone next to the kid's lunch? Yes, I said. That was the moment the switch flipped. This wasn't a family squabble. This was negligence. This was endangerment. This was a liability nightmare that could bankrupt me and send me to prison if a child ingested those chemicals. Nicole looked up from the couch, then wiping tomato sauce from her lip. She didn't look guilty. She looked annoyed that we had interrupted her show. "Oh, you're here," she said. You should have called first. Chapter 5. The 911 protocol. The elevator ride down to the lobby was the longest 30 seconds of my life. I stood next to Logan, my hands trembling slightly, not from fear, but from the adrenaline of finally irrevocably choosing myself. My phone buzzed in my pocket like an angry hornet. I didn't look at it, but I knew who it was. My mother texting me that I was selfish. texting me that Nicole was just trying to build a future for her children. Texting me that I had upset the delicate ecosystem of enabling that our family had cultivated for 30 years. "Are you going to call your parents?" Logan asked quietly as the doors opened. I stepped into the lobby. It was cool, quiet, and sane. "No," I said. "There is no point. They will just tell me to apologize." I pulled out my phone. 17 missed calls from my mother, five from my father, and a notification from Tik Tok. Nicole had posted a video. I clicked on it. It was a tour of my loft. "Check out my new office, guys," she chirped, spinning the camera around to show the view of the skyline. "Big things coming for my brand. Manifesting abundance works. She was taking credit for my success. She was using my sanctuary as a backdrop for her delusions. And she was doing it while my mother cheered her on from the sidelines. I closed the app. I thought about the solvents. I thought about the art. I thought about the absolute disrespect of my boundaries. She told me to call the police, I said to Logan. What? She told me, I repeated, my voice steady. If I didn't like it, I should call the police. She thinks I won't. She thinks I'm still the little sister who keeps the piece. My thumb hovered over the keypad, then pressed the sequence. 91 1 911. What is your emergency? I need to report a trespassing in progress at 404 West Hubard Street, I said. My voice was calm, precise, professional. The intruders have refused to leave. There are also hazardous chemicals on the premises, and there are unsupervised minors in close proximity to them. I am concerned for their safety. I didn't say my sister. I didn't say my nieces and nephews. I used legal terms. Trespassing, child endangerment, chemical hazard. I was gay rocking the emergency operator. When the police arrived, two officers looking serious, I met them at the door. I showed them my deed. I showed them my ID. I explained the situation briefly and factually. Is the individual inside a tenant? One officer asked. No, I said. She is a squatter who broke in. We went up. When the officers walked into the loft, Nicole screamed. I live here, she yelled, jumping up from the couch. Ask my mom. She gave me permission. The officer looked at me. I looked at the officer. Does your mother own this property? The officer asked Nicole. No, but then her permission is irrelevant, he said. I watched Nicole's face crumble. For the first time in her life, she was encountering a boundary that couldn't be manipulated with tears or guilt. "The law didn't care about her brand. It didn't care about her struggles. It only cared about who held the deed." Chapter 6. the marketplace betrayal and the bill. As the officers were escorting a sobbing Nicole toward the elevator, Logan tapped me on the shoulder. He was holding his tablet, looking at something with a frown. Lauren, he said, "Where are the Louis the 14th chairs?" I looked at the corner of the room. It was empty. Two antique chairs upholstered in original 17th century velvet were gone. They were worth $12,000 a pair. Maybe she moved them to the bedroom, I suggested, though a cold knot was forming in my stomach. I don't think so, Logan said. He turned the tablet toward me. It was a Facebook Marketplace listing. Posted one hour ago. Antique chairs, great condition. Must go today. $500 for the pair. The seller was Nicole. The knot in my stomach tightened into a hard, heavy stone. She hadn't just invaded my home. She hadn't just endangered my livelihood. she had stolen from me. She had sold heirloom antiques for the price of a used sofa to pay for her inventory. "Officer," I called out. My voice was ice. The officer stopped. Nicole looked back at me, her mascara running down her cheeks. "I need to add a charge," I said. I held up the tablet. "Grand lararseny." Nicole's eyes went wide. "I was just borrowing them. I was going to pay you back when my business took off. That's not how theft works," the officer said. He reached for his handcuffs. This wasn't a family dispute anymore. It was a felony. By the time my parents arrived at the precinct to pick up the children, Nicole was being processed. They burst into the waiting area. My mother's face red and blotchy. My father looking like he wanted to hit someone. Drop the charges. My mother screamed, rushing at me. She's your sister. How could you do this? I didn't flinch. I reached into my bag and pulled out a document I had drafted on my phone while waiting. It was an invoice. This is a bill, I said, handing it to my father. For the professional cleaning of the loft, for the disposal of the hazardous waste she brought in, for the restoration of the rug she ruined, and for the market value of the chairs she sold. My father stared at the paper, his hands shaking. "You, you are dead to us." "Good," I said. "That means you can't ask me for money anymore." I turned my back on them. I walked out of the station into the cool Chicago night. I was alone. I had no family. And I had never felt more free. Chapter 7. The architecture of peace. It has been 6 months since I walked out of that police station. I am currently sitting in a small cafe in Kensington, London, watching the rain wash the cobblestones clean. My phone is on the table, face up. It is quiet. The Chicago loft was rented out 3 weeks after the incident. I had to hire a specialized cleaning crew, the kind that usually deals with crime scenes or hoarding situations, to fully sanitize the space. They stripped the carpets, repainted the walls, and removed every trace of the chaos Nicole had brought into my world. It cost me $5,000, but it was worth every penny to exercise the ghosts of obligation. The Marose, the French couple who had fled in horror, actually came back. Logan explained the situation to them that the squatter had been arrested and the security codes had been changed. They signed a two-year lease, paying the full $18,000 a month upfront. That capital allowed me to open my London studio ahead of schedule. I don't talk to my parents. I don't talk to Nicole, but I do talk to my aunt Sarah. She called me yesterday with an update. Nicole pleaded guilty to grand lararseny to avoid jail time. She is currently serving two years of probation and has a permanent criminal record. No reputable landlord will rent to her and her brand collapsed the moment the mugsh shot went public. She is living in my parents' basement along with her three children. My parents are broke. They drained their retirement savings to pay for Nicole's legal defense. The sunk cost fallacy finally caught up with them. They kept betting on a losing hand until the house took everything. Aunt Sarah told me they spend their dinners complaining about how ungrateful I am, how I abandoned the family. I listened to her and I felt nothing. No anger, no guilt, no desire to call them and explain myself. I used to think that sanctuary was a physical place. I thought it was white leather sofas, climate controlled galleries, and expensive locks. I thought if I built a perfect enough space, I would finally feel safe. But I was wrong. Sanctuary isn't a place. It is a boundary. It is the ability to say no without explaining yourself. It is the refusal to set yourself on fire to keep others warm. It is the realization that blood is biology, but family is behavior. I looked at the rain falling on the London streets. I am alone here, but I am not lonely. I have my work. I have my self-respect. And most importantly, I have the keys to my own life. And I am never making copies for anyone else again. If you have ever had to become a grey rock to survive a toxic family member, hit that like button and share this story with someone who needs to know that walking away isn't quitting, it is Earning.

From a lending standpoint, you're going to tell the lender what your intention is with the property. In this case, he was going to use this as a vacation rental. And this is key, which is why that video that he saw actually had him reach out. But in his case, he wanted to do a minimum down payment type of loan. and he was thinking he wanted to do a second home property classification, but he'd already been pre-qualified with another lender. And what I know about second homes that a lot of lenders don't share, including the lender he already spoke with, was that there is a writer that accompanies the deed, the security instrument, the final loan documents that a borrower signs when they purchase a second home. and that writer specifically dictates what you can do with the property. Well, he was not aware of this. And what it says specifically is that it's going to require that the property remain under the owner's control. And so, in short-term rental situations, that's not always the case. When you are getting a management company involved, which he was, he wanted to have a management company actually manage the property. and his intention was to use it a couple times a year as a second home, but the majority of the time he was going to have a management company do short-term rental management for the property. Yeah, a rate in the sevens, at least based on this point in time. This the second scenario though is a 15% down investment property and it's at the same rate. So there's no anybody dictating whether it can be a short-term rental, whether he can have a property manager manage the rental, whether it can be a long-term rental. On an investment property, it is open game. As an investor, you can do what you want with the property and however you want it. So his mind was just kind of blown of like, "Oh my gosh, I didn't even know this existed." home equity home equity is the value of your home which is determined by your loan balance subtracted from your home's value so let's say your home's value is about $400,000 and let's say you have a mortgage balance of $300,000 $400,000 minus $300,000 leaves you with $100,000 of home equity understanding your home equity is critical for a variety of reasons number one your home equity is established with your down payment so if you put down 20K or 20% that starts off your home's equity and the growth now your home's Equity is built in two different ways primarily the first way is home appreciation if your home values going up every year you're building home equity the second way you build home equity is by paying down your balance every single month we call that principal reduction so every month you make a mortgage payment a portion of that payment goes towards your home equity some of it goes towards interest and taxes but a portion of it goes towards your Equity so every month you're chipping away at your loan balance ideally if home value is appreciate every single year you build equity up and you build equity down and that Equity can be leveraged to help you make other Investments so I'm sure you've heard this before but the the number one way people build wealth and store wealth is in real estate and specifically that refers to the home equity in people's real estate holdings some people have one home some people have three homes some people have big apartment buildings or an entire portfolio so that being said your home's Equity changes every single month every month you're making a mortgage payment and every month home values can go up or down now your home's Equity can be determined in two ways it can be determined by an appraisal which is a snapshot on where the market is now or it can be determined if you actually go and list your home for sale and you negotiate a price that which someone is willing to pay it's not cash you can access in the bank tomorrow but there are ways to tap into it and to build your wealth so we've touched on what home equity is and how it's determined let's talk about the various ways you can access your home equity the first way is a home equity loan you're essentially getting a mortgage for a portion of the equity it's a second mortgage it's a loan and you receive a lump sum of cash from there you make monthly mortgage payments to pay down that balance every single month just like you do on your primary mortgage for example let's say you wanted to borrow $60,000 you would apply for a home equity loan you would get that $60,000 after the underwriting and mortgage process which takes a few weeks once you close you get that money it's yours to do whatever you want and you pay it down every single month just like you do on your first mortgage I have this magical thing called a home equity loan now the second way is a home equity line of credit which is also known as a HELOC this is a little bit different you're not getting a lump sum at closing you're getting an open line of credit so let's use the same $60,000 after you go through the underwriting process and get approved for your HELOC you have a $60,000 line that you can tap into whenever you want so initially you may not need to use the funds let's say you want to do some repairs you get a quote it takes 30 days to get a bid and now you know that you need to spend $110,000 on a new garage well you can borrow $10,000 of that $660,000 line and then every month you only pay on that $10,000 the idea here is to not get all the money up front to have access to it and you pay as you use it a really easy way to understand this is to think about a HELOC as a credit card you use what you need and you pay down your balance every month but you don't get it all in one lump sum now the third way to access your home equity is through a Cash out refinance this is a little bit different than the first two ways because now you have one new loan so here's how it works let's say your mortgage balance is $300,000 and you want to take out $60,000 for Home Improvements instead of getting a second mortgage for $60,000 and having two loans at two different rates you do one new loan for $360,000 000 and the difference between what you owe and what your new loan is is what you get to keep in one lump sum now you have one mortgage payment it's based off the $360,000 loan it keeps your finances simple and it houses all of the debt under one interest rate for Simplicity now here's the fourth and last way to access your home's equity and it's kind of straightforward it's basically selling your home now I'm not saying you need to go out and sell your home but at some point you might be ready to make a move ready to downsize or ready to relocate when you sell your home a realtor is going to help you determine your home's value you're going to look at your mortgage St to determine what you owe and the difference is your home equity so all in all what you'll walk away with after you sell your home is the sale price minus the real estate transaction fees and minus your actual mortgage balance and any taxes owed at closing so now we understand what home equity is and how you can access it let's talk about some of the common ways that you can use your homes Equity to your benefit so the first and most common way people use their homes Equity is to buy a new primary residence in other words you sell the home you're living in now you take the money you made which is your home equity and you apply that towards a down payment on your new home which you're going to reside in the most common example of this is when a young family outgrows the space they have and they need a bigger home with a yard and a basement now the second way to use your home's Equity is to buy an investment property and this is one of my favorite strategies to make more money what you do is you take a home equity line of credit or home equity loan and you use that money to purchase another property it might be a 2 unit it might be a 4 unit it might even be a single family home you get some renters in that property and now you start having some cash flow which you can use for other Investments you can use it to pay down your current mortgage the idea here is to build another stream of income and now to have a second property that's building more Equity that contributes to your total net worth now if you're paying attention here you'll start to notice some patterns as a mortgage lender this is the most common way we help people buy their first investment property by using the equity in their primary residence and over time what happens is you start to build more equity in every property that you own and you can tap into that Equity to purchase another investment property and to build your real estate portfolio over time the third way to use your home Equity is to pay off high interest rate debt like a credit card or an auto loan as I record this video credit card rates are at an all-time high between 25 to 30% mortgage rates are still around 7% by eliminating High interest rate credit card debt or even a high interest rate auto loan it could drastically lower your total monthly overhead for example let's say you took out $440,000 of home equity to pay off all your credit card debt and to pay off a high payment auto loan that $440,000 in monthly payments might cost you $300 a month but by eliminating all the debt from your credit cards and your auto loan you could save over $11,000 a month for a net Savings of about $700 per month and the last way to leverage your home equity is to establish an emergency Reserve fund now a lot of people sleep on this and many firsttime and second time buyers don't even consider it but here's a deal there might come a time where you need a major repair where you need to do some maintenance where you come across a business opportunity or an investment opportunity having that open line of credit gives you the ability to tap into that Equity immediately and to use it for whatever reason or whatever purpose you need in your life at that time again it could be an emergency it could be an investment it could be some major repair maintenance on your home the punchline is it's important to have access to reserve funds or to investment funds when you need them in a pinch so look we've talked about all the exciting ways you can leverage your home's Equity how to access that cash to make more money but I'd be remissed if I didn't share some of the things to be mindful of and some of the downsides of borrowing too much money no we're not borrowing money so the first thing to consider obviously is as you borrow more money money against your home it reduces your home's Equity let's say in a couple years you want to go sell your home but real estate values have dropped if your loan balance is here and real estate values come down to here you have no equity to make when you sell your home and you might even need to bring some money to the closing table to get rid of that property the second thing to be mindful of is your debt to income ratio this is what lenders look at which is basically how much debt you have compared to how much income you bring in so when you get that money we're looking to make sure you can make those monthly payments but for example let's say a year or two passes and you lose your job or the financial circumstances in your household change you still got to make that payment every month and it might be challenging so just be very mindful of what those new payments are going to be when you take that equity and think Down the Road 2 3 years so you don't commit to a financial situation that puts a strain on you and your family so now that we have a better understanding of your home's equity and its use I want to give you a simple example of how to use your home's Equity to buy another home so let's say you've been in your current home for the Last 5 Years and let's assume your home's value is $400,000 and your home balance is $300,000 so you got about $100,000 worth of home equity now because your interest rate on that home that you own right now is still very low you don't want to sell it you want to rent it out and make some money but you still need that equity for the down payment on your new home so let's say you want to buy a $500,000 home and you want to put 10% down that's $50,000 so your goal here is to take $50,000 out of your current home's equity and apply it towards a down payment on the new home so what you would do is you would apply for a home equity loan and you would get that $50,000 in the form of a lump sum based on today's interest rates that $50,000 will cost you anywhere from $300 to $400 a month so you got to take that into consideration and you got to look at your current mortgage payment plus the $3 to $400 a month and you got to make sure that the rental income from the property you're moving out of meets or covers that monthly amount that way you don't have to worry about paying additional to use that $50,000 now when you go to buy your new property the mortgage lender you're working with will take into consideration that $50,000 as your down payment is it's completely allowable and when you go to make an offer this is the key here you don't need what's called a home sale contingency that means you can buy the new home without having to sell your previous home because the rental income that you're going to get covers the mortgage payment and your home equity payment in total so now your income is freed up to go buy and qualify for your new $500,000 property so you find that $500,000 property you go through the mortgage process and that $50,000 home equity loan is totally used to cover your entire down payment so if you made it to the end of this video I hope you picked up a couple nuggets and I hope it got - How Do Volunteers Make Chicago Summer Festivals Happen? - What To Know About Chicago: Kind: captions Language: en [Music] How do volunteers make Chicago summer festivals happen? Imagine a city alive with music, food, art, and laughter, all brought to life by the energy of volunteers. These dedicated individuals are the heart of Chicago's summer festival scene. From June through August, thousands step up to help make these events happen. Without their help, many festivals could not run smoothly or at the scale residents and visitors enjoy. Volunteers take on many roles during festivals. They work at beverage tents, serving drinks to keep everyone refreshed. They help manage the lines at entry gates, ensuring safety and flow. They assist with setting up stages, booths, and decorations before the event begins. After the festival, they help with cleanup to leave parks and streets tidy. Their work keeps everything running smoothly so visitors can focus on enjoying the experience. Some volunteers support specific community causes. For example, at the Chicago Canine Rescue Festival, volunteers serve beer at tents and all tips go directly to helping animals. Starting in 2024, volunteers also help with gate entry, showing how their roles grow to meet festival needs. This flexibility makes volunteering a great option for many people. Volunteering isn't just about big events. Many organizations focus on youth programs during the summer. Chicago Light Summer Day offers classes, arts, and field trips for students in grades 1 through nine. Volunteers help with reading, writing, math, and creative activities like dance and music. They also support career prep classes, helping students write resumes and explore jobs. These roles give volunteers a chance to make a real difference in young lives. For those who want to help but have busy schedules, many festivals offer flexible options. Shifts can last from 1 and 1/2 hours to 6 hours with most around 2 to 3 hours. This means working professionals, students, and retirees can find a way to contribute. The Chicago Park District also offers one-day opportunities like park cleanups, Earth Day events, and special Olympic support. These short-term options make volunteering accessible for everyone. Organizations recognize volunteers, efforts with rewards. For example, the Chicago International Film Festival gives volunteers festival t-shirts, raffle entries, and certificates for hours worked. Volunteers who contribute more hours can earn memberships or other perks. These incentives show appreciation and encourage ongoing participation. Volunteers help make arts and cultural events accessible across Chicago neighborhoods. Many festivals are free or lowcost thanks to volunteer support, making sure everyone can enjoy summer activities regardless of income. This helps build stronger communities and brings people together through shared experiences. Festival groups also provide training and guidance. Volunteers at Chicago Lights receive lesson plans and support from teachers. Technical volunteers at theater festivals work under professionals to learn set design and stage management. Clear communication channels like dedicated email addresses and volunteer coordinators make it easy for new volunteers to sign up and get involved. The summer festival season is busy, but volunteer opportunities exist all year round. Spring is for preparations. Summer is for the main events. And fall features film festivals and food celebrations. Volunteers are the backbone of this cycle, helping Chicago shine during its warmest months and beyond. In short, volunteers make Chicago summer festivals happen by bringing enthusiasm, skills, and teamwork. They support everything from setting up stages to teaching kids, ensuring these lively events can grow and thrive. Their efforts turn parks and streets into vibrant cultural hubs, making Chicago's summer season unforgettable for everyone.

- Skills for Healthy Romantic Relationships | Joanne Davila | TEDxSBU: Kind: captions Language: en Reviewer: Queenie Lee Intimacy, security, respect, good communication, a sense of being valued. These are some of the things that most people would agree make for healthy relationships. And researchers would agree, too. There is a large body of literature on romantic relationships that has identified the features of healthy relationships, and the list I just provided contains many of them. Researchers also agree on what makes for unhealthy relationships - things like fighting so much that you just can't work things out; not being able to go to your partner for support when you need it; contempt, criticism, hostility, violence. When these problems happen in relationships, they can cause significant unhappiness. They can lead to the end of relationships and divorce, and they can literally make people physically and emotionally sick. This is why it is so critical that people have healthy relationships. But there is a problem: how many people know, I mean, really know what to do on a day-to-day basis, to create healthy relationships? My point is this: we may know what a healthy relationship looks like, but most people have no idea how to get one, and no one teaches us how to do so. We need to teach people how to have healthy relationships. Now, you know when we typically do so? After it's too late. It is called couples therapy. I do couples therapy, and it can be a wonderful thing. But many people come to couples therapy with so many ingrained problems and patterns that they just can't change. It's too late. You know when else we try to teach people how to have healthy relationships? Right before they get married. It's call premarital education. And this is a good idea: teach people how to have a good relationship while they are still happy, presumably. And it can work. But in my opinion, it's still too late. Why? Because people have already selected the person they want to commit their life to. What if they selected poorly? No amount of premarital education can make up for a bad partner choice. So the ways we have tried to teach people how to have healthy relationships have been limited, because they fail to address three important things: genuinely knowing what you want and need in a partner and a relationship, selecting the right person, and developing and using skills right from the beginning. I don't mean the beginning of any particular relationship. I mean the beginning-beginning, like as soon as possible. We need to teach people, especially young people, Now, towards this end, my colleagues and I have developed a skills based model of relationship functioning that we believe can help people create the things that lead to healthy relationships and reduce the behaviors that lead to unhealthy ones. We've identified three skills - insight, mutuality, and emotion regulation - that form the basis for what we call romantic competence. Romantic competence is the ability to function adaptively across all areas or all aspects of the relationship process, from figuring out what you need, to finding the right person, to building a healthy relationship, and to getting out of relationships that are unhealthy. I'll tell you more about the skills in a minute, but first, let me say that we didn't just make this up out of the blue. We identified the skills based on a thorough review of theory and research. And the skills really represent the commonalities across the major theories and research findings on healthy relationships. And because they represent the commonalities, we think they really can help people with all the different parts and with all different people - whether people in a relationship or not. So let me tell you about the skills. The first one is insight. Insight is about awareness, and understanding, and learning. So with insight, you'll have a better idea of who you are, what you need, what you want, why you do the things you do. So let's say you are being really snappy to your partner. With insight, you might notice or realize that it's not that your partner is doing anything, but actually you're really stressed out at work. What you really need is to relax a little bit, so it doesn't bleed out over into your relationship. Insight will also let you know your partner better. Let's say your partner shows up late for a date. With insight, you'll know why. For example, maybe your partner is late for everything. It's nothing about you or the relationship. That's just who your partner is. With insight, you'll be able to anticipate the positive and negative consequences of your behavior. For example, you'll know that if you send that nasty text, it is not going to go well. Maybe you'd better make a phone call instead. With insight, you will be able to learn from your mistakes in ways that allow you to behave differently in the future. So maybe you'll recognize that you're the kind of person who tends to jump in really quickly - you get wrapped up in the romance of things - and then things don't go well. So you might be able to say, "Well, you know what the next time I'm just going to take things a little more slowly and not repeat the same mistake. And with insight, you'll have a better understanding about what's really right for you in a relationship. Maybe you're the kind of person who really needs a monogamous relationship. You are not OK with your partner seeing other people. Or maybe you'll realize it's just the opposite, that you're not ready to settle down, and you need a partner who is OK with that. So that's insight. The second skill is mutuality. Mutuality is about knowing that both people have needs, and that both sets of needs matter. With mutuality you'll be able to convey your own needs in a clear direct fashion that increases the likelihood that you'll get them met. Let's say you have to go to a really stressful family event, and you'd like your partner to be there with you. You might say directly: "You know this is going to be stressful for me. I'd really love for you to be there; you'll be a really good buffer for me. Is there any way you can clear your schedule to come with me?" With mutuality, you'll be willing to meet your partner's needs as well. Let's say you know that your partner really likes to go to the gym first thing in the morning, it makes your partner feel better the rest of the day. Mutuality will let you be willing to support your partner in this, even though you'd really rather have your partner stay home, in bed with you. And mutuality also lets you factor both people's needs into decisions that you make about your relationship. So let's say you get a great job offer that you'd like to take, but you know it means you will to have to work more, and you know how important it is for both you and your partner to spend time together. With a mutual approach, you might say, "You know, I'd really like to take this job, it's really important to me, but I also am concerned about us spending time together. If I promise to protect some time for us, will you be OK with me taking this job?" That's a mutual approach to relationships. The third skill is emotion regulation. And emotion regulation is about regulating your feelings in response to things that happen in your relationship. With emotion regulation, you'll be able to ... keep your emotions calm and keep things that happen in your relationship in perspective. So, you might think: "Oh, my goodness. This is a disaster! This is the worst thing ever! How am I going to handle this?" With emotion regulation, you'll think: "You know what, I can handle this. This is going to be all right. There is a way to deal with this. I'm going to figure this out. Everything is going to be OK." you'll be able to tolerate uncomfortable feelings and not act out on them impulsively, so you'll to be able to think through your decisions more clearly. So let's say your waiting for your partner to text you back. That text isn't coming; you're getting really anxious; you're checking your phone every two seconds. you'll be able to tell yourself, "You know what? Calm down. The text is going to come. I don't need to check my phone every second; I'm just going to put it away and focus on the task at hand." And with emotion regulation, you'll be able to maintain a sense of self-respect and commitment to your needs, even when bad things happen in your relationship. So let's say you have a breakup. You're feeling really depressed; you're really missing your partner. you'll be able to let yourself know that it is OK; that, yeah, you're going to feel depressed, but you're going to get over it and get through this. If you beg and plead to get back together, you're not going to feel good about yourself, and you don't even want to be in a relationship that wasn't good for you. So insight, mutuality, and emotion regulation. I believe it's people's ability to use the skills on a day-to-day basis that lets them have healthy relationships. So let me give you an example of how this works. The other day I was talking to someone, and she said that when her partner asked her what she wanted for her birthday, she told him she didn't want anything. So guess what? She didn't get anything. And she got really angry, and they had a big fight. Because she really did want a present, she just didn't want to tell him; she just wanted him to somehow know. It is called mind reading. It is a terrible idea; it never works. Had she been using the skills, insight would have let her know herself well enough to realize that she really did want something, and if she didn't get it, she was going to be mad. Insight also would have let her know that her partner was the kind of guy who was just going to take what she said literally. Mutuality would have let her really ask for what she wanted, directly and clearly. And emotion regulation would have let her deal with any feelings she was having that were getting in the way of doing that. So maybe she was feeling kind of anxious: What would he think if I asked for what I needed? Or maybe she was feeling guilty, you know. She knows they are saving for a big trip, and she maybe thought that he would think that she was kind of greedy or something. So if she had used the skills, she would have been able to say, "You know what? I know we are saving for that trip, but I really like that necklace that we saw the other day, and it wasn't that expensive." He would have gotten it for her. She would have felt respected and valued. He would have been happy. They would have felt more intimate. This whole birthday gift thing would have gone well, instead of ending in a fight that could really damage their relationship. Now, this was just an anecdote. We have data to support this as well. I've been studying romantic competence, the ability for people to use insight, mutuality, and emotion regulation, among young people. In one of our studies, we looked at 13- and 14-year-old girls, early adolescent girls, and we found that girls who were more romantically competent felt more secure in their relationships. They felt comfortable being close to people, they could trust people, they weren't worried about being rejected. Girls who are more romantically competent reported fewer depressive symptoms, they had better mental health. They also were more positive about their expectations about marriage in the future; they were more optimistic that it could go well. Girls with greater romantic competence were engaging in more typical romantic activities for their age, things that were normative, like dating and flirting and affectionate behaviors like hugging and kissing. And girls who were more romantically competent were engaging in fewer ... atypical, sexual activities, like sexual intercourse, which can be considered pretty risky for a 13- and 14-year-old girl. So, even at an early age, 13 and 14 years old, when these girls mostly were not even in relationships, the more romantically competent they were, the more adaptive relational functioning they were showing, and the better mental health they were showing. We see the same things among young adults, 18 to 25 years old: More romantically competent men and women feel more secure in relationships. They also report making better decisions, they can see the warning signs when things aren't going well and make conscious decisions with confidence. They're also better at seeking and providing support to their partners. So, they are more willing to ask for what they need and use what their partners give them. And they are better at providing helpful support when needed. And this isn't just what they told us, we actually observed them doing this in our laboratory, where we asked them to talk with one another about a personal problem. Young people who were more romantically competent also were more satisfied in their relationships, they were happier. And again, they reported fewer depressive symptoms and also fewer anxiety symptoms. So overall, being romantically competent at a young age is associated with greater, more adaptive relationship functioning and greater individual well being. And this brings me back to my point that we need to be teaching people So, like I said earlier on, we may know what a healthy relationship looks like, but most people have no idea how to get one, And this is a problem. We need to help people genuinely know what they want and need in a relationship. We need to help them select the right partner. We need to help them make good decisions and deal with the challenges that relationships bring. And we need to help them build and use skills This is what the notion of romantic competence is all about. It's all about using insight, mutuality, and emotion regulation to reduce the behaviors that lead to unhealthy relationships, like fighting, and poor support, and hostility, and criticism, and contempt, and violence. And create the things that lead to healthy relationships, like intimacy, security, respect, and a sense of being valued. And wouldn't all of our relationships benefit from this? I think they would. Thank you. (Applause)

- 3 rules for better work-life balance | The Way We Work, a TED series: Kind: captions Language: en Transcriber: Leslie Gauthier For so many of us, myself included, our days feel filled with a million small interruptions. And this is true even of our days off. Maybe you've taken a call at the beach, texted your boss from the grocery store or emailed a colleague while on a picnic with your family. We’ve convinced ourselves that these behaviors are no big deal. It's just one email. But there's a real cost to these interruptions, and there are smart strategies we can all take to better protect our time. (Music) [The Way We Work] These moments seem so small at the time, and yet research suggests they add up to a tremendous loss. The constant creep of work into our personal lives can increase our stress and undermine our happiness. So just what is the cost? In one study, researchers recruited parents who were visiting a science museum with their kids. Some parents were told to check their phone as much as possible; others were told to check their phone as little as possible. After the visit, parents who used their phones reported that the experience was significantly less meaningful. They also felt much lonelier. In another study, tourists who were asked to have their phones out while visiting an iconic church remembered fewer details a week later. And in my research, employees who were paid for their performance spent increasingly less time interacting with friends and family, and more and more time interacting with colleagues and clients. These constant interruptions come at a cost to organizations, too. Companies lose 32 days of productivity each year to employee depression, which is often caused by the stress and burnout of our always-on culture. Despite knowing better, I, too, have found myself focusing on “urgent work distractions” over important life moments. Most recently, I found myself texting a client while in the middle of my first child’s first ultrasound ... happy client, guilty mom-to-be. When you add up all of these moments, the sum total is a life shortchanged on meaning, joy, connection and even memory. As we remake our models of work in the wake of the pandemic, now is our opportunity to create a new culture that respects time. And the way to make this really big change is through small steps that we can take right now. The first step that we need to take is to reframe rest. Reflect for a moment about what you think about when you hear the word “rest.” Sounds amazing, right? But in my mind, I immediately worry about not being productive enough or letting down my colleagues. When we do have time off, we need to find ways in which we can enjoy the present moment and savor the leisure time that we have available, as opposed to seeing it as an unproductive barrier to our work. One specific strategy we can take is to treat our upcoming weekend like a vacation. On Friday afternoon, jot down how you would act and behave as if you were on a holiday. Maybe you and your partner will buy a bottle of wine and watch online clips of the Eiffel Tower. Maybe you’ll visit a local café and listen to some live music. Or maybe you'll go for a long walk in the middle of the day with no phone and no agenda. The plan doesn't have to be expensive or extravagant. Another strategy you can take is to create clear boundaries for your time off. Instead of saying, "I'm out of the office. Feel free to Slack me whenever,” say, "I'll be offline. Call me only if it's urgent." To uphold these personal goals, work together as a team. Set team goals for personal time. Do it publicly, collect data and hold each other accountable. These goals could sound like, “I will not check email between 6:00 and 8:00pm;” “I will have dinner with my family four nights a week;” or "I will go for a jog midday." Check in on your team’s progress and see how everyone's doing. If you or your teammates are unsuccessful, work together to help accomplish personal goals. Lastly, you can negotiate for more time to prevent work from creeping into your personal life. In business school, I teach students to negotiate for salary but realize I spoke almost nothing about negotiating for more time. What does this look like in practice? You can ask for more time on adjustable deadlines at work. If your client asks for a deliverable Monday morning, ask for an extension until Tuesday afternoon so you don’t find yourself working on your well-deserved weekend. And don't worry too much about reputation. Quality truly is the metric that matters most. In my data, employees who proactively asked for more time reported lower levels of stress and burnout, and were seen as more committed and professional by their colleagues. These are small but powerful changes to not only reframe rest, but to reclaim it. Once you discover the profound impact that these changes can have, you’ll feel empowered to demand that others respect and accommodate your approach to time. Maybe they’ll even feel inspired to piece together the fractured moments of their lives, too.

- The Three Requirements of a Good Relationship: Kind: captions Language: en-GB Many people, after they’ve been in a couple for&nbsp; some time, will privately admit that they are - in&nbsp;&nbsp; many ways - frustrated and disappointed by the&nbsp; person they’ve chosen to share their lives with. &nbsp; If pressed for details, they will have&nbsp; no difficulty coming up with a list:&nbsp;&nbsp; their partner, they might complain: Is too loyal to their irritating family&nbsp; doesn’t share their views on&nbsp; the layout of the living room&nbsp; Never wants to go on camping holidays Plays tennis every Wednesday evening,&nbsp;&nbsp; no matter what Doesn’t like Moroccan food&nbsp; Doesn’t share their enthusiasm&nbsp; for 19th century Russian novels&nbsp; Has a habit of adding ‘actually’ to every&nbsp; second sentence, when it’s actually redundant As the list gets longer, they sigh; they&nbsp; still love their partner and long to be happy&nbsp;&nbsp; together, it’s just that it seems impossibly&nbsp; complicated to make this relationship work. What’s driving the frustration isn’t that&nbsp; they’ve sadly fallen for an idiot as a mate;&nbsp;&nbsp; it’s rather that we have all&nbsp; inherited needlessly complicated ideas&nbsp;&nbsp; of what a relationship is supposed to be for. We&nbsp; are told that love is meant to involve the almost&nbsp;&nbsp; total merger of two lives: we expect that a&nbsp; loving couple must live in the same house,&nbsp;&nbsp; eat the same meals together every night, share the&nbsp; same bed, go to sleep and get up at the same time;&nbsp;&nbsp; only ever have sex with (or even sexual&nbsp; thoughts about) each other, regularly see&nbsp;&nbsp; each others’ families, have all their friends&nbsp; in common - and pretty much think the same&nbsp;&nbsp; thoughts on every topic at every moment. It’s a beautiful vision, but a hellish one too,&nbsp;&nbsp; for it places an impossibly punitive burden of&nbsp; expectation on another human. We feel the partner&nbsp;&nbsp; must be right for us in every way, and if they’re&nbsp; not, has to be prodded and cajoled into reform. But there’s another perspective: relationships&nbsp; don't have to be so complicated and ambitious&nbsp;&nbsp; if we keep in view what in the end actually&nbsp; makes them fulfilling. If we boil matters down,&nbsp;&nbsp; there might really just be three essential&nbsp; things we want from one another: Kindness: a partner who is gentle&nbsp; with our imperfections and can&nbsp;&nbsp; good-humouredly tolerate us as we are. Shared vulnerability: someone with whom&nbsp;&nbsp; we can be open about our anxieties, worries&nbsp; and the problems that throw us off balance:&nbsp;&nbsp; someone we don’t have to put on a good front for;&nbsp; someone around whom we can be weak, vulnerable and&nbsp;&nbsp; honest - and who will be the same around us. Understanding: someone who is interested in,&nbsp;&nbsp; and can make sense of, certain obscure features&nbsp; of our minds: our obsessions, preoccupations&nbsp;&nbsp; and ways of seeing the world. And whom&nbsp; we are excited to understand in turn. If we have these three critical ingredients to&nbsp; hand, we will feel loved and essentially satisfied&nbsp;&nbsp; whatever differences then crop up in a hundred&nbsp; other areas. Perhaps our partner’s friends or&nbsp;&nbsp; routines won’t be a delight, but we will be&nbsp; content. Just as if we lack these emotional&nbsp;&nbsp; goods, and yet agree on every detail of European&nbsp; literature, interior design and social existence,&nbsp;&nbsp; we are still likely to feel lonely and bereft. By limiting what we expect a relationship to&nbsp;&nbsp; be about, we can overcome the tyranny and&nbsp; bad temper that bedevils so many lovers.&nbsp;&nbsp; A good, simpler - yet very fulfilling -&nbsp; relationship could end up in a minimal state:&nbsp;&nbsp; we might not socialise much together. We might&nbsp; hardly ever encounter each other’s families.&nbsp;&nbsp; Our finances might overlap only at a few points.&nbsp; We could be living in different places and only&nbsp;&nbsp; meet up twice a week. Conceivably we might not&nbsp; even ask too many questions about each other’s&nbsp;&nbsp; sex life. But when we would be together it&nbsp; would be profoundly gratifying, because we&nbsp;&nbsp; would be in the presence of someone who knew&nbsp; how to be kind, vulnerable and understanding. A bond between two people can be deep and&nbsp; important precisely because it is not played out&nbsp;&nbsp; across all practical details of existence.&nbsp; By simplifying - and clarifying - what a&nbsp;&nbsp; relationship is for we release ourselves&nbsp; from overly complicated conflicts - and&nbsp;&nbsp; can focus on our urgent underlying needs to&nbsp; be sympathised with, seen and understood.

And they did so only rarely, usually during extraordinary times, no longer. YouTube and X and other platforms are instantly available to virtually everyone. Why? The president of the United States started his own social media platform so that he could say anything he wanted without any accountability. And in recent years, government departments and agencies, including the police, have resorted to issuing vague statements in lieu of real answers to specific questions from journalists. Ironically, such statements sometimes seem designed to withhold information rather than provide it. As social media continues to reshape the way we communicate, unilateral statements should be received with healthy scepticism. Connecting with close friends on social media didn't make them any less lonely. Researchers in the U.S. looked at national health data from more than 111,000 adults over several decades. The study, published in the BMJ Journal, found that people who did a greater variety of physical activities had a lower risk of dying early compared to those who stuck to fewer types of exercise. In fact, those who mixed up their routines the most switching among workouts like walking, running, tennis and weight training had about a 19% lower risk of death. Individual cardio activities, especially walking and running, showed some of the strongest benefits, while racquet sports d weight training were also linked to better health, experts say different types of movement may help the body in different ways, including heart health, strength and overall fitness. - How to Make Friends While Traveling (even if you're shy): Kind: captions Language: en Making friends is hard at the best of times, but making friends while traveling seems impossible. Gus and I each left home to travel as dorky, anxious teenagers. We learned through a lot of trial and error how to make friends while traveling. And we're going to teach you how with realistic step-by-step instructions. Step one is to book a social hostel. If you're a drinker, a hostel that organizes welcome drinks or a pub crawl is the best option. Loved host in Asia are a great example. They do a get to know you hour every day for everyone that checked in that day with a free cocktail. And a little bit of liquid courage makes everything easier. If you don't drink, don't worry. Look for a hostel with family style dinners or organized group activities. They'll have photos like this or this on their website. Yes, I know bunk beds suck for everyone, but social interaction is the name of the game. Chances are anyone already in the room will say hi once you walk in, and you can choose whether or not to engage. Oh, good day, mate. Hey, what's your name? My name is Gus. How are you? My pro tip is to look for bunk beds built into the wall and make sure they have curtains for privacy. For girls, book a female dorm if one is available because girls are so much more likely to be social when their guard is down. Step three is to join activities. Go ahead and sign up for every single tour that your hostel offers. If there's a game night, put your name down. If there's a temple tour, cooking class, a beach trip, sign up. Most people you meet at these tours are already staying at the host, so you already have something in common. Strike up a conversation about the food or the beds or the hostel itself. Okay, here's a hot tip. If you haven't met anyone in your dorm yet, go to the grocery store, buy yourself some noodles, and then cook those noodles in the kitchen. There are a million ways to strike up a conversation in a cramped kitchen. For example, how's the how's the food here? Have you had lunch yet? Do you want to join me? Do any of these knives not suck? Do you know if they have a microwave in the kitchen? Step four is smile and say hi. Backpacking life isn't like real life. People are more relaxed and open. That being said, people traveling in large groups aren't usually open to meeting people. The goal is to find other solo travelers or pairs of travelers. Individuals are always easier to approach, but sometimes I'll walk into the common room and everyone is already grouped up. But just remember that even though a group might seem like an established friend group, usually they met 30 minutes ago and they're probably more than happy to add you into their group. So step five is the conversation part. The hardest part about meeting people is starting the conversation. Please don't start with where are you from? It's the most common opening line and tends to shut down conversation or at least reduce it to the stereotypes of each country. Hey, where are you from? Um, I'm American. Cool. I'm Canadian. Oh, my uncle's Canadian. Oh, where is that conversation going to go? Instead, here are some opening lines that are more likely to kickstart an actual conversation. Do you know where the waterfall trip starts? The bathrooms here are awful, aren't they? Are you going to do the trivia tonight? How's the food here? Have you ordered any of it yet? It's important to keep the subject matter in neutral ground. Something that you both know something about and can both comment on. People get bored of talking about somewhere they've already been. You're somewhere right now. Talk about where you are right now. It is important to read the room, though. If they're not responding easily, just drop it. Excuse yourself. Don't force it. Hey guys, do you want to go? You guys want to go do something today? Step six is inviting people to go and do something. Ask people to go for lunch, join your trivia team, go out and do that city tour. Simply say, "Can I come with to the beach?" Have you been to the beach yet? Uh, not yet. I might go this afternoon, though. Do you mind if I join? Oh my gosh. Yeah. Yeah, totally. Sure. We'd love that. Look for these signs that people don't want to hang out with you. Most people won't just straight up say no, you can't come. Hey, do you mind if I join you guys at the beach? Um, yeah. Yeah. Um, maybe if it's okay, I'll just uh let you know later. I have to kind of talk to my friends and see what the plan is. Just remember, if you feel embarrassed about a conversation or an interaction you had, just pack your bags and move to the next city. you will never have to see these people again. Step seven is to ask for their socials or their phone number. So, you had a promising chat, but you're both busy at the moment. You should ask to connect on social media. Instagram is the best option because you may see something on their profile that starts a conversation. WhatsApp is another good option for anyone who doesn't want social media. Always reach out ASAP to reconfirm what you talked about in person. If the person you were talking to earlier doesn't follow you back, then they're not interested in doing anything. It's an easy way to figure out if someone's down to hang or not. But if they are, you can easily set plans in stone for later. I hope you feel inspired now to travel solo. But if you're still intimidated by our DIY approach, you can sign up for a group tour. For example, you could start your Vietnam trip with the Hajjang Loop tour, or you could start a South America trip with the Sunb Blast Islands tour. Not having anyone to travel with is no excuse. If this is the life you want, go for it. We'll see you next time for an episode on how to travel when you're poor, a subject I unfortunately know too much about. Subscribe for more travel guides without the fluff.

  9. @Floridamainiac [1 likes]: Man I have a crazy solo traveling story: While solo traveling through Uruguay, I took what I thought was a local bus to Punta del Diablo, but I must’ve dozed off because I woke up in a tiny coastal village I couldn’t find on any map. The place felt like a dream—old fishermen nodded knowingly at me, kids offered me sweet maté without a word, and someone handed me a flower crown like I was meant to be there. That night, they led me to the beach for a “guardian celebration.” I followed the music, half-curious, half-weirded out, until I saw a massive papier-mâché fish being pushed into the ocean, lit from within like it had swallowed the stars.

At midnight, it exploded—literally exploded—in a flash of light and sea spray, and hundreds of glowing jellyfish washed ashore. No one screamed. No one ran. They just smiled, scooped them into jars, and held them like sacred candles. I swear I saw one of the jellyfish wink at me. The next morning, the village was back to normal, no one remembered a thing, and they laughed when I asked about the festival. But I still have the jar. It’s dimmer now… but sometimes, in the dark, it still pulses.

And while many of them have come and gone, some of them got married, some of them moved town, one of them became a priest, actually. (crowd laughing) But anyways. So I think it's really important to surround yourself with the right people, to form your tribe with intention. You wanna surround yourself with people who make you a better person. And for the most part those types of relationships are gonna be friendships, but you don't get quality friendships by following a step-by-step action plan or something like that. Some of the best relationships I've ever had in my life seem to have just randomly happened when I wasn't really expecting it whatsoever. But I do wanna talk about some really useful things that I've learned throughout my life, that have really helped me make friends easier and make higher quality friends.
