- Could 'degrowth' save the world? | BBC News: Kind: captions Language: en [Music] So essentially it's how do we live well on this planet without destroying it. It's a simple question. We are saturated no of all this information and of talking of the problem. How can we have spaces to look towards the solution? We've all heard it before. To create a sustainable world, we must move away from fossil fuels and embrace green technology. But what if technology alone isn't enough? People are dying and all you can talk about is money and fairy tales of eternal economic growth. How dare you? What if solving the issues of climate change demands a more profound systemic change? A group of activists and academics argue that infinite economic growth on our finite planet is a dangerous solution. Their bold solution, degrowth. We are trying to teach people the importance of building social, economic and environmental links with people around you. So moving to this project gave me a lot of stability. I know that I'm going to have here 40 people that will be there for me. It takes two kinds of work now. It takes social, political, organization work, but also it takes a cultural shift. Is it time to embrace more radical solutions or are we risking too much? All of our experience uh historically has been that if the economy shrinks for a period of time that results in a big increases in poverty. I think degrowth uh underestimates the power of innovation. Degrowth underestimates the need for investment and degrowth underestimates the social feasibility of what they propose. There's no necessary contradiction between social and ecological objectives, but it does require a pretty radical transformation of the way we think about the economy and structure it. In 1972, a group of researchers published a book titled The Limits to Growth. Their first conclusion was the following. If the present growth trends in world population, industrialization, pollution, food production, and resource depletion continue unchanged, the limits to growth on this planet will be reached sometime within the next 100 years. The book sparked a heated debate at the time, but also inspired a generation of scholars who started questioning the benefits of an everexpanding economy. Professor John Martinez Cier is considered one of the godfathers of degrowth and the founder of the Barcelona School of Ecological Economics. He began writing about the links between the economy and the environment in the 80s. Economic theory was based just on prices and markets and not on the real substance of the economy which is the material flows and the energy flows. So my book was doing a kind of history of these critiques of the economy and it was the first book with the title ecological economics because the growth comes partly from ecological economics. Then in 2008 they had the idea of having in France a meeting in English about degrowth. The degrowth movement which began in France as dequasans and was inspired by thinkers like Serge Luch is now spreading worldwide becoming popular in countries like Japan, the UK and Spain. In Barcelona it is being embraced by academics, grassroots movements and activists. degrowth is a is a radical proposal to let's say pull the emergency brake on a train that it's going to the precipice. So it's a it's a call to stop this pursuit of endless compound geometric growth for growth's sake. What isn't it isn't like a call to destroy the economy. It's not a call for permanent recession. It's a call for doing things differently and in a way that human needs come first. Human and ecosystem needs. Welcome to Labriuga Puval. This is our uh guest house and environmental teaching space. Here we have a guest house business. We have a retreat center. We have our own family home. We have a field there where we're doing regenerative agricultural experiments. And we have all all of this forest where where people come to to reconnect with nature. So we're really transparent about how we run this place on the principles of degrowth and the principles of permaculture. We like to convey the idea that an abundant life doesn't necessarily have to be one that's full of consumerism and and and loads of stuff. So it does demand a lifestyle change, but it in theory shouldn't be one that bothers many people. Um but it's getting people out of what is basically a trap. And one of the main traps we are in now, degrowthists argue, is actually the very way we measure our economies using GDP or gross domestic product. Yeah, GDP is interesting. It's um it's a really bad measure of of an economy. It counts the market value of everything that we produce, but it does not count the social and ecological costs of production. Because right now the idea is effectively let's just chase GDP growth everinccreasing levels of production and hope that this magically solves our social and ecological crisis which it never does. We need a more rational approach to how we manage the economy. It's like being on an airplane that simply in order to stay in the air has to continue to accelerate. Uh I mean you don't want to be on that machine. It's extremely dangerous. For me, when we talk about degrowth with people who are actually unacquainted, my starter is always how would you make your life a little bit more meaningful? And meaningfulness is very much obtained through our kind of nourishing empathy, helping others. Many groups don't necessarily tag themselves as degrowth, but you would see that their agenda, their way of being and their kind of overall framework of reference is very much the one of the growth. Today it was the first day of the uh forum for the ecosocial transition. We tried uh to think how the future would look like in 2040. We try to advocate for what would be a deg scenario but also being aware that we live in the global north and a deg scenario would mean that we would have to change a lot of our lifestyle. Advocates of degrowth believe that rich countries use more resources which hurts the environment. The waste from these resources usually ends up in poorer countries. So even though it seems that rich countries are doing better in reality they are just passing the problems to others. The solution lies, the growthers say, in dramatically reducing material and energy consumption in the global north while still focusing on people's well-being. So, Catalonia, it's a place where there's a story of the existence of cooperatives, which are economic enterprises that are not based uh on profit making for the accumulation of benefit, but actually that try to do their activity with the goal of uh satisfying need. We see this as a growing tendency. A clear example is the housing sector. Here for example, it's quite a new tendency and it's growing a lot. Definitely showing also that alternatives are possible. Proponents of degrowth argue for a democratization of the economy and for collectively managing key resources like housing. They propose scaling up existing solutions like housing cooperatives. I'm going to show you. Yeah, my place. There we are. So basically when you get in you already have kind of the living room together with the kitchen. So all the apartments have an open kitchen uh the bedroom uh with a lot of light too. So basically yeah you have the right to use it for 75 years. And the good thing is that it's kind of protected. So in the sense that since I am not the owner, I won't be able to sell my apartment like in a couple of years so that we cannot speculate um with housing. So this is one of the biggest common spaces that we have in the ground floor. So basically here we do assemblies, we do um birthday parties, we do dinners. Apart from reducing the impact of energy and material wise, we are also bonding community strengths, we are basically being more autonomous and working for getting that autonomy which allow us to be less in the market system. So basically the architectural firm is a cooperative also the construction company that has built this building and the way that this works is that you are basically only paying for the material price and the salary price. There is no industrial benefit on that because it's for nonprofit. That's what we call the library of things. So we have many kinds of different things that we use from time to time. Uh so we share them here. We have them here. We need them. and we take them and we put them back. Normally in conventional apartments um in Barcelona what you get, you know, is kind of a a narrow corridors while here, you know, that's where you have like the um entrance doors to all the different apartments. So, it's really a place where kind of um life in community happens a lot. But what exactly are degrowers proposing? The main thing is we have to uh scale down less necessary forms of production, right? So let's have an open democratic conversation about what kinds of production we really do need uh to increase, right? Say things like solar panels and public transit uh you know regenerative food supply etc. I mean we need these things and then what and what forms of production are clearly destructive and actually not necessary for our society, right? So SUV production, private jet production, uh industrial beef, you know, fast fashion, advertising, the military-industrial complex. I mean, there's huge portions of our productive system that are mobilized around goods and services that are actually not necessary for society and mostly exist to enrich elites, right? Spending less on the fence would of course prove controversial. And this approach would cost jobs. Jobs like the one Mike left behind. This is this is something that we scare our guests with. Um, in the supermarket, the mushrooms, the these kinds of oyster mushrooms that you'd get there are almost always grown in grow bags. And we can make this happen completely naturally. You know, I can't tell you how happy people are even if they manage to grow one thing. You know, it's mind-blowing for people. I remember what that was like. I was one of those people 25 years ago. I worked for almost 20 years um as an urban designer. I wasn't blind to the fact that I was blatantly working within the real estate system even though I was you know a sustainability consultant. Yeah. You realize there's a materials blindness in what we in what we ignore when we're studying sustainability, at least in the in the early 21st century, because none of these technologies that we as designers were being encouraged by their manufacturers to fit into our designs can be made without fossil fuels. I felt cheated. Um I this was not something I was aware of. We are fighting against the the idea that green growth is possible. We are trying to highlight that a bigger change than perhaps most people are comfortable with immediately uh is what's required. What the economy call externalities and they say that we could put a price in the externalities and and close the economic circle. This is just imagination. It's not like this physically and the calculations that we have is that only 10% of materials or less are recycled. 90% of what comes into the economy becomes waste. It is time I think to think about scaling back and reducing our energy use and satisfying that with renewable energies in a decentralized in a planned in a careful way. This maybe could be possible. But thinking of the current gigantic system 10 times bigger and serviced by renewables starts getting in the sphere of fantastic. I think the growth economics champions social justice arguing that market demands and social inequalities fuel the issues around climate change. It also argues that relentless global growth in production and consumption is not just unsustainable but economically disastrous especially for the most vulnerable. So I teach people in the masters at IKA and the degrowth masters and I see that young people of you know 20s um are much more aware of the tight linkages between what's happening in terms of environmental um degradation in terms of climate change and social inequalities and injustices at global and local levels. So I think that awareness has increased tremendously and that's why these fields now are becoming more prominent and people want to study and learn more. You know there's a mix of excitement and stress and chaos and passion and and a lot of passion a lot of passion. Right now we are very very close to opening the exhibition which is our final project of the master in dig. We have been working on creating an exhibition that is based on social transformations. How do we engage more people into joining actively or emotionally with social transformations into a more uh just and and equal world? I did an investigation about people's opinions around the topics of degrowth. I found that that many people were also very very in in support of degrowth and these ideas. But I also found that maybe many people don't really grasp how much of a change that would entail and and also what their own tools are of of like uh changing something. Look at what we have. Wow. We think that one thing that's hindering social transformations is a lot that people are afraid of many things. Afraid of change, afraid of destabilizing their lives and we always asked them one question is do you feel secure? And that always ended up opening a conversation about what causes a form of security or insecurity and always what they related that to. Historically, cities have been divided by class, by race, by ethnicity. But what we see now is that this move towards building resilient cities, green cities is accompanied by these, let's say, pervasive green divide that continues because of dynamics of what we call green gentrification. Barca is part of a larger study that we conducted of 28 cities where we found that in 17 of those cities, greening is a contributor to gentrification that is to the displacement of socially vulnerable residents due to different processes of real estate speculation, competitive urbanism. One of the conclusions of our study is the need to make public spaces really public again and being an amenity for everyone in the city. That is it actually very closely connected to degrowth and to the role of the commons in people's lives. Doing more equitable greening would be also involving people in the decisions taken about where to implement what. So being also very much about participatory democracy and um decision making involving the most vulnerable people and so on. Critics of degrowth argue that it would cause huge changes to society and that it would make people worse off. that we need in new technology and in new uh solutions and degrowth underestimates the social sort of uh feasibility of what they propose. So growth has very successfully over many decades lifted large numbers of people out of poverty. That in and of itself is one of the reasons why you get into the way of economic growth at your peril. In the UK since 1990, we get three times amount the economic prosperity out of a ton of CO2 than we did in 1990. So that's a big decoupling. Uh the speed at which that has happened hasn't been sufficient. Um but we sort of are in the scurve in the accelerating part of those technological changes so things can speed up. policies which would result in a smaller economy unless staggeringly carefully implemented would result in imiseration of large numbers um of people. So uh you certainly need to accompany it with a you know a dramatic experiment. Now there may be ways of doing that but that will be a very very risky experiment indeed in my view. reducing your own material consumption, not throwing uh good food away. And these kind of things are very much ingrained in for everyone kind of but but that that this might need systemic change that needs to be politically activated or that needs stronger political participation that was not so so often in people's minds. The growth is not an idea of how things should be and pushing everyone like a like a flock of birds or sheep into the same direction. No, but it's just building conversations upon how can we make an alternative future happen. We do get a fair number of people who come here and go look I know that only 9% of my recycling gets recycled. So what can I do? It's helping those people see that small actions can cause behavioral contagion, but also possibly helping them be a little bit more radical in their choices and helping them feel like they're not trapped in the system as much as they might think they are. It's very clear that our political class is unable right now to resolve the contradictions of our economic system and all sorts of competing ideas are rushing in to fill the void. It's urgent that we prioritize a sort of transition that is going to be just and ecological, not one that is going to be deeply inequitable and deeply destructive. In May 2023, the EU Parliament hosted the Beyond Growth Conference in Brussels, emphasizing the need to change the current economic system. This event was viewed by many as a sign of mainstream support for some of the ideas of degrowth, shifting the movement from the periphery towards the center. But can degrowth really change the world? Or would it greatly damage society? What is clear is time is running out and urgent changes are needed if we want to safeguard the future of humanity.

- UK’s New Passport Rules for Dual Citizens Under 2026 ETA Rollout | Sky 522 | DM News English: Kind: captions Language: en [music] Hello and welcome. I'm Sunnia and you are watching DM News English. Millions of British citizens live overseas, including more than a million people who hold dual nationality. For many of them, returning to the UK is about to become more complicated as new passport rules come into force alongside the government's roll out of the electronic travel authorization or ETA. Let's take a look at this report. From 25th February 2026, British citizens who hold dual nationality will face new requirements when traveling to the United Kingdom following changes linked to the government's electronic travel authorization or ETA system. Under the new rules, dual nationals will no longer be permitted to enter the UK using only a non-British passport, even if that passport is from a visa exempt country, such as an EU member, state, Canada, or Australia. Instead, affected travelers must present a valid British passport, a valid Irish passport, or a foreign passport containing a certificate of entitlement confirming the right of abode in the UK. The change marks a shift from previous practice. Until now, UK law did not strictly require British citizens with another nationality to enter the country on a British passport, and many routinely traveled using their foreign one without issue. The move is tied directly to the expansion of the UK's ETA system, a digital pre-travel authorization being introduced for visitors who do not require a visa. While most foreign nationals from visa-free countries will need an ETA to enter the UK, British citizens are exempt, but they are also not eligible to apply for one. This has created a practical problem for airlines and ferry operators who are required to verify passengers permission to travel before departure. If a dual citizen presents only a foreign passport, carrier systems may flag them as needing an ETA they cannot obtain. To avoid penalties for transporting ineligible passengers, carriers will now be required to check for proof of British or Irish status before boarding. For many dual nationals, the most straightforward solution will be renewing or applying for a British passport. However, those who choose not to do so may instead apply for a certificate of entitlement, an official endorsement placed in a foreign passport confirming the holder's right to live and enter the UK without restriction. That certificate is significantly more expensive than a passport and must be renewed each time the passport expires. The UK government has stressed that the changes do not affect nationality law. Dual citizenship remains legal and unchanged. The update relates solely to how citizenship or right of abode must be evidenced at the border rather than removing any rights. Irish citizens are unaffected by the new rules. Those traveling on an Irish passport will continue to be exempt from both the ETA requirement and the additional documentation checks, reflecting long-standing arrangements between the UK and Ireland. Officials are advising dual nationals to check their travel documents well in advance, warning that passengers who arrive at an airport or port without the correct paperwork may be denied boarding before they reach the UK border. The new measures are part of wider efforts to digitize and tighten border controls with the government arguing they will improve security while streamlining entry for eligible travelers provided they carry the correct documents. >> That's all for now. I'm Sunnia with DM News English. Stay tuned for more updates.
