- Identity Crisis: Are We Israel? Are We Gentiles?: Kind: captions Language: en Back in the 2nd century, the early church father Irenaeus used a great analogy about the false teachers of his day. He said we can think of Scripture as a mosaic of tiles designed to express a certain image, let's say, for example a king. We can think of each of those little tiles as a passage or statement in the Bible that has been connected and arranged in such a way that collectively, they form an image, for example this king. What false teachers do, Irenaeus said, is take those exact same tiles, those same passages and statements in the Bible, and rearrange and redefine them, which ultimately causes the image to change into something different and distorted. Something that Scripture never intended. And this is so true of Hebrew Roots teachers like the one we’re going to hear from today. They talk about real historical events, real people, and actual passages from the Bible. So at first glance, if we’re not paying attention, it all kind of sounds right and true. But because of their precommitment to keeping the Law of Moses, these teachers rearrange and redefine the tiles in a way that ultimately creates a distorted image of the real thing. And unfortunately, the difference between the pictures isn’t immediately obvious if we’re not paying attention. We can look at both of these pictures and say, “Hey that’s a king!” But if we’re not very familiar with the genuine king, we may not realize that the false king has a distorted face, and the pose is different, and his clothes are different. It's easy to spot false teachers who turn an image of a king into a fish or a dog. When differences are that glaring it’s obvious something’s wrong. But with Hebrew Roots teachers, the changes are more subtle and many of us don’t notice the differences. And that makes it all the more dangerous. Especially since most of our Hebrew Roots friends truly believe what they’re saying, and don’t recognize it as a false image. And that just may be the case with the teaching we’re going to look at today which has to do with understanding our identity as followers of Jesus. In what sense are we grafted into Israel? Are we no longer Gentiles? Are we now Israelites? This identity issue is pervasive in Torahism because it’s used to lay the groundwork for the theological claim that Christians are required to keep the Law of Moses. And by the way, the term "Torahism" just describes any theology that teaches that Christians are required to keep the Torah, meaning the Mosaic rituals—eating kosher, and the Torah feasts, the weekly Shabbat, and so on. You might know it as Hebrew roots, or Torah-observant Christianity, or something like that. And those who subscribe to this theology believe that not keeping those rituals is sinful and disobedient. And while the Torah is a beautiful and fundamental part of the Christian faith, Torah-ism is a dangerous misapplication of the commands of God. And as I mentioned, this identity issue is persistent in Torahism. There’s a lot of confusion stirred up by Hebrew Roots teachers who promote these ideas. And we are really going to get into this today, because it really needs getting into. And we’re going to do so by looking at a specific teaching by a popular teacher of Torahism, Jim Staley. In a video called “Identity Crisis,” he presents a fairly sophisticated version of a theory that’s more properly known as Two-House theology, although he doesn’t use that term in this video. And we’re going to methodically dismantle this theology, piece by piece, in a fair and respectful and truthful way. We are going to compare Mr. Staley’s teaching to Scripture. Or to use our analogy, we’re going to compare his image to the image the Bible teaches, and we’ll see how and where it misses the mark. We’re going to hear some clips from Mr. Staley in his own words, and talk about what Two House theology is and demonstrate exactly why it’s so unbiblical, and why so many people fall for it, and what the Bible really says about our identity in reference to Israel and being grafted in. And then we’ll wrap it up with a look at the three biggest dangers of Two-House Theology. The good news is that as sophisticated as Two-House theology is, disproving it is fairly simple. We won’t even need to get into all the contours of the argument and examine all the “evidence” that Mr. Staley presents. Why? Because the foundational claims on which this theology is built are easily tested against the Bible and shown to be false. In other words, we don’t need to fully understand how Mr. Staley (and other Hebrew Roots teachers) arrived at their particular arrangement of tiles, because we can demonstrate that the image they’ve created is distorted and inaccurate. A number of our viewers have asked about Mr. Staley’s “Identity Crisis” video. And, to be fair, the video we’re going to be looking at is a few years old, so it’s possible Mr. Staley’s views have changed since he taught this. But, unfortunately, the Internet never forgets. And whether or not he still holds this exact position, this is the teaching that’s out there and influencing people. And it’s a teaching he has given multiple times. So that’s what we’re going to address. And I’ll include a link to his full teaching below so you can review it in its entirety. And let me say this up front: because I know many of you are going to comment on it: Yes, I am aware of the legal issues that Mr. Staley has been through, but I’m not going to get into that here in this video, or in the comments below, because it’s irrelevant to the issue at hand. Mr. Staley doesn’t use this term in his video, but what he is teaching is known as “Two House Theology.” And in a nutshell, this theory is based on the splitting of the ancient kingdom of Israel. So let’s do a quick Bible history refresher here to make sure we are all on the same page. Remember after King Solomon, in about 975 BC, the 12 tribes of Israel suffered from infighting and split into two different kingdoms or houses. There were 10 tribes in the north, and the northern kingdom was called Israel, or the House of Israel. And the other two tribes made up the southern kingdom called Judah, or the House of Judah. So in 722 BC, the northern kingdom was conquered by Assyria and hauled off to other lands. And then about 130 years later, around 586 BC, the southern kingdom of Judah was captured by Babylon and sent into exile. Now, after 70 years, the Israelites in Babylon were allowed to return to the land and they began to rebuild Jerusalem and the temple. But the Bible never explicitly tells us that the northern 10 tribes ever came back. So Two-House Theology says that the people we refer to as Jews today are solely descended from the southern kingdom of Judah. Hence the term “Jews.” But the northern kingdom was assimilated into the nations and, according to this theory, became Gentiles. Therefore, Mr. Staley says, many of the people we call Gentiles—especially those who are drawn to a Torah-observant lifestyle—are actually descendants of the 10 tribes of the northern kingdom of Israel. So those are the two houses: the house of Israel, which according to Two House theology turned into Gentiles, and the house of Judah. And the idea behind this teaching is it’s important for we Gentile believers in Jesus to receive the truth of our identity so that we can begin living and acting as the people we actually are: because we are Israel. Two-house theology says that Gentiles who come to faith in Jesus are no longer Gentiles, they are now Israel or Israelites. And therefore, according to Mr. Staley, understanding the distinction between the two houses is critical. Here’s how he puts it: “This is really important, why I told you earlier you’ve got to understand the difference between the house of Israel in the north and the house of Judah in the south. There is not a time in the Bible where Israel (all 12 tribes) are called the house of Israel. It is the house of Israel in the north, and the house of Judah in the south.” And in this video, he’ll go on to walk us through Scripture as he lays out his case. But let’s pause here because he said something we can easily test. And I love this kind of stuff because you don’t need to be a Bible expert to test some of these kinds of claims. He has defined the phrase “House of Israel” as the ten northern tribes. He said there’s not a time in the Bible where all 12 tribes are called the house of Israel. So we can just go to our favorite Bible website—Blue Letter Bible, Bible Gateway, Bible Hub—and do a search on the phrase “house of Israel.” And what we’ll find is that Mr. Staley’s statement is false. All 12 tribes are referred to as the “house of Israel” many times. For example, in Exodus 40 when all 12 tribes were wandering through the wilderness together, we read this: “For the cloud of the Lord was on the tabernacle by day, and fire was in it by night, in the sight of all the house of Israel.” And all 12 tribes are referred to as the “house of Israel” five more times in Leviticus, and again in Numbers and Joshua and Ruth. These passages all occur long before the kingdom of Israel ever split. And as we’ll see in a bit, the phrase “house of Israel” is used to refer to all 12 tribes in the New Testament. So, contrary to what Mr. Staley stated, there are a number of passages where the Bible does apply the phrase “house of Israel” to all 12 tribes. And that’s an important fact to remember as we make our way through his teaching. Now, despite the occasional factual error, Mr. Staley is a gifted speaker and very persuasive, and he presents his theology in a fairly compelling and sophisticated way. And he wisely spends the first 30 minutes of this video setting up a framework in which Two House theology doesn’t come across as quite so farfetched. And even then, he asks his audience to indulge him as he unpacks some of his statements because he understands how odd they sound. And, by the way, I not only watched his entire three-hour video on this topic, but I also outlined it and took copious notes to make sure that I really understood what he was teaching. (I hope you know I do these things for you guys because I love you.) So, yes, he is a good speaker. But as we’ve just seen, not all of his claims are true. And his factual errors are going to compound. So let's not let his enthusiasm and persuasiveness cause us to leave his statements untested. Our ultimate standard of truth isn’t what Mr. Staley says, and it’s certainly not me. It’s Scripture. And here’s the thing. Two-House theology is not taught in the Bible. As I’m going to demonstrate, it’s not a position we arrive at by letting the text of Scripture speak for itself. Rather, it’s a theology that has been constructed to support and reinforce the preconceived notion that Christians are required to keep the law of Moses. And as we’ll see, in order for this theology to work, Mr. Staley is going to change the definition of words, insert theories that aren’t supported by the text, and re-interpret commonly understood passages. We’ll see examples all of these things. So let’s start our examination of Two-House Theology by looking at this idea of the lost 10 tribes of Israel. The foundation of Two-House Theology is the claim that when the 10 tribes of the northern kingdom of Israel were conquered by Assyria they were scattered and lost. Mr. Staley explains it in the dramatic opening of his video: “The northern kingdom continually broke the law of God and they, too, were taken into captivity into Assyria. From here, just as the Bible foretold, they assimilated into the nations, would lose their identity as the northern house of Israel. They would be pushed into all four corners of the then known world, which would eventually be the four corners of the Earth today. The Asherites, the Reubenites, the Danites, all the other tribes, where are they? They don’t exist today because they never came back. God said at the end of time the lost tribes would be found amongst the Gentiles and their identity would be revealed.” And I want us to notice not only what he’s saying here, but what the application is. The idea is that the 10 northern tribes of Israel were lost and scattered among the Gentiles. And they remained that way for thousands of years. And now, today, our identity as Gentiles is being revealed: we are descendants of those lost tribes! Contrary to the last 4,000 years of Judeo-Christian thought and study, it turns out that Gentile believers in Jesus—like you and me and Mr. Staley—we’re actually Israelites! And you can see how this creates a belief system in which the keeping of the law of Moses can be seen as natural and even our duty. Now, I completely agree with Mr. Staley regarding the historic divided kingdom of Israel, and the defeat of the 10 northern tribes by Assyria, and that those tribes were largely assimilated into other cultures. That’s historical and biblical fact. The question is: What does the Bible tell us about those other 10 tribes after they were scattered? The phrase “lost tribes of Israel” isn’t found in Scripture. And this issue is really popular with those who are… let’s say predisposed to conspiracy theories. This idea has actually been around for centuries in various forms and it feels like every claim under the sun has been made about it. British Israelism claims that the lost tribes of Israel ended up in the British Isles. So the British colonists who came to America are their descendants, and therefore Britain and/or America are the new Israel. On the other hand, Mormons believe that several hundred years after His resurrection, Christ appeared and taught in the Americas in order to minister to, you guessed it, descendants of the lost 10tribes. Back in the 1600’s, a Hebrew scholar from Amsterdam named Manasseh ben Israel claimed he had discovered the lost 10 tribes in Ecuador. And there’s literature in Finland from the 1700s where the Finns, Lapps, and Estonians are identified as coming from the lost tribes of Israel. The Ahmidiyya movement in Islam believes that after surviving the crucifixion (because they believe Jesus wasn’t really killed on the cross), Jesus went to the descendants of the 10 tribes who were in Afghanistan and Kashmir. And the modern Black Hebrew Israelite movement believes that it is blacks from Africa, Hispanics from South America, and Native Americans today who are the true Israelites and that white people or “conventionally accepted” Jews aren’t members of the tribes at all. And ironically, on the opposite end of the race spectrum, the white supremacist group Christian Identity Movement claims that it’s white, Anglo-Saxon people who are the rightful descendants of the “lost tribes” of Israel. And then, of course, we have modern-day Hebrew Roots teachers like Mr. Staley who claim that it is Gentile believers in Jesus who are descendants of the lost tribes. So everyone’s trying to get in on the game. There are a lot of claims out there and there is zero historical evidence in support of any of it. These are conspiracy theories that grow on the shadowy edges of documented history. Because everyone wants to be God’s chosen people. So what does the Bible actually tell us about those ten lost tribes? Scripture is actually very quiet on the issue. In fact, it is in this silence that conspiracy theorists try to insert their ideas. However, based on the text of the New Testament, which was written 700 years after the northern kingdom was scattered, the idea of 10 lost tribes doesn’t seem to exist in the time of Jesus. For example, Luke introduces us to a woman named Anna who was from the tribe of Asher. Asher was one of the 10 northern tribes that Mr. Staley said never came back. If those tribes were fully lost, how did Luke know what tribe she was from? And in Acts 26, when the apostle Paul made his defense before Agrippa he said, “And now I stand here on trial because of my hope in the promise made by God to our fathers, to which our twelve tribes hope” (notice the present tense: hope) “to attain, as they earnestly worship” (present tense) “night and day.” Paul is speaking of the 12 tribes of Israel as if they were very much alive and known and still worshipping God in his day. And James, the brother of Jesus, addressed his epistle “To the twelve tribes in the dispersion.” So James not only thought the 12 tribes still existed, but he actually wrote a letter to them, and that letter part of Scripture. Now, Mr. Staley knows his Bible quite well and I’m sure he understands the big hole in his theory that I am demonstrating. So presumably as a way to account for these New Testament references to the allegedly lost tribes of Israel, he introduces this idea. “By the New Testament times…These next couple slides are so critical, listen to me. By New Testament times, most still knew which tribe they were from and lived across the Euphrates River, exactly what Josephus says, He says they lived by the millions across the Euphrates River. In the New Testament, even the Jews knew the northern 10 tribes, that by the millions lived They were not allowed to be part of Israel. And I’ll explain that a little bit later. This is really important theology to understand. But over the past 2,000 years they would completely lose their identity." So the claim is that at the time the New Testament was written, the northern Jews still knew which tribe they were from. But after that, over time, they completely lost their identity. And it’s important to notice that Mr. Staley offers no Scriptural evidence in support of his theory. This is all apparently based on the writings of the famous first-century historian Josephus. And if this information is true, it’s not corroborated in the Bible. The New Testament doesn’t say anything about the northern 10 tribes—not that they were living across the Euphrates by the millions, nor that they weren’t allowed to be part of Israel, nor that the southern tribes said they can’t come back. All of those statements are extra-biblical conjecture that actually seems to contradict New Testament accounts. For example, Anna from the northern tribe of Asher? Luke goes on to tells us, “She did not depart from the temple, worshiping with fasting and prayer night and day.” So here we have a member of a lost northern tribe worshiping in the temple (which was located in southern Israel, 500 miles from the Euphrates River), and she was openly doing so among all the other Jews who were there. Yet Mr. Staley says the 10 northern tribes weren’t allowed back or to be part of Israel. Or better yet, how about Acts 2, where Jews “from every nation under heaven” had gathered together in Jerusalem (again hundreds of miles beyond the Euphrates River) for the feast of Shavuot. I thought they weren’t allowed to be part And this event is amazing. Remember, this is Pentecost when the Holy Spirit fell on the disciples and they began speaking in tongues, and the Jews from every nation were astonished because they could each hear their own language being spoken. And they thought the apostles were drunk so Peter got up and spoke to them. He began by addressing them as, “Men of Israel.” And then look at the concluding statement of his sermon, in verse 36. “Let all the house of Israel therefore know for certain that God has made him both Lord and Christ (or Messiah), this Jesus whom you crucified.” So here Mr. Staley’s theory runs into a big problem. Because if Peter used the phrase “house of Israel” to explicitly refer to the lost tribes of the northern kingdom (which is what Mr. Staley said that phrase always means), what were they all doing in Jerusalem keeping Shavuot at the temple? I thought they were across the Euphrates and not allowed to come back or be part of Israel. On the other hand, if, in this instance, Peter used the phrase “house of Israel” to refer to all the Jewish people from all tribes, then those 10 northern tribes were obviously not lost, and they weren’t estranged from the southern kingdom. So this single event in Acts 2 renders the entire premise of Two House theology false. In fact, some scholars suggest that Pentecost—with the falling of the Holy Spirit and the 3,000 Jews that were saved that day—was the fulfillment of the Old Testament prophecies about the regathering of the tribes of Israel. The idea being that Jews from every nation under heaven had gathered in Jerusalem, representing all twelve tribes. And when they came to faith in Christ, they were all gathered together under one King, King Jesus the Messiah. And as these new Jewish believers returned home to their nations after the feast ended, the Word of the Lord, the Gospel, flowed forth from Jerusalem to the ends of the Earth. Which is a really interesting way of looking at it. But this idea that the southern kingdom wouldn’t allow the northern tribes back into Israel just doesn’t align with Scripture. As we saw in Acts 26, Paul refers to the 12 tribes collectively as one people who were worshiping God night and day. And when James addressed his letter to the 12 tribes, he said nothing of two houses or a rift between them. In fact, after addressing the 12 tribes, his letter begins, “Count it all joy, my brothers, when you meet trials of various kinds…” He refers to all 12 tribes as his brothers. So the idea that the 10 northern tribes were lost or estranged at the time of Jesus just doesn’t seem to be the case. And it makes you wonder how an articulate and intelligent man like Mr. Staley could arrive at such an unbiblical conclusion. I think it has to do with his definitions. A red flag we need to watch for when it comes to Bible teachers is when they want to change the commonly accepted definition of words. I’m not talking about adding clarity or nuance to what certain words mean, but actual changes. Because the definitions of biblical words are determined by how the Biblical authors used those words, not by what we think they mean today. We just saw an example of this when Mr. Staley unsuccessfully suggested a new definition for the phrase “house of Israel.” He said it only refers to the 10 northern tribes, but the Bible disagreed with him. So let’s look at two more words that he needs to redefine in order for this Two-House Theology to work. The first word is "Jew" or "Jews." And here I want you to notice two things that Mr. Staley does in this next clip. First of all, he’s going to suggest that we need to go back to the Bible for the proper definition of the word. And of course he’s right! It’s critical that we understand how the Bible defines this word. But you’ll notice that immediately after saying that we need to go to the Bible, he actually goes to Wikipedia for his definition. “Now, if you are Jewish today, I understand that just the question that I’m asking is offensive. I’m asking you to put your emotions aside for just a moment, and let’s let the Bible define itself. Let’s take us out from modern-day theology and religious circles and go back to the Bible during the time, when those definitions were given and let the Bible define itself. According to Wikipedia, the origin of the word, Jew is this: after the splitting of the united kingdom of Israel and Judah, the name Yehudi was used for the southern kingdom of Judah. It thus ultimately originates in the biblical Hebrew word, Yehudi, meaning from the tribe of Judah, from the kingdom of Judah, or Jew. So, in other words, the word "Jew" comes from the southern kingdom.” And the second thing we need to notice is that Mr. Staley didn’t actually provide a definition for the word Jew. What he cited from Wikipedia was the etymology or historical roots of where that word came from. And in English, where a word comes from doesn’t necessarily tell us what it means. For example, the English word “horse” is a noun, an animal. But if you trace its etymology, it comes from the Latin verb “currere” which means "to run." And we can all see the connection here, horses run, right? But the English word "horse" does not mean "to run." So where a word comes from historically does not necessarily tell us what it means today. So when we see the word Jew, sure, we can all agree that it comes from But how is that word used today? And for the commonly understood meaning of an English word, all we need is a dictionary. “Jew: One of a scattered group of people that traces its descent from the Biblical Hebrews or from postexilic adherents of Judaism; Israelite.” And interestingly, if we go to the same Wikipedia entry that Mr. Staley quoted from, we find this definition sitting there in plain sight. “Jews: An ethnoreligious group, nation or ethnos originating from the ancient Israelites and Hebrews of historical Israel and Judah. So in common parlance, the word Jew doesn’t carry the idea of being limited to the southern tribes. But how does the Bible define that word? Let’s take a look. Easton’s Bible Dictionary says: “Jew: The name derived from the patriarch Judah, at first given to one belonging to the tribe of Judah or to the separate kingdom of Judah” And then it points us to the biblical data behind this usage in 2nd Kings and Jeremiah. And it goes on to say: “in contradistinction from those belonging to the kingdom of the ten tribes, who were called Israelites.” Okay so this is Mr. Staley’s definition. But it doesn’t end there. The text goes on to say... “During the Captivity, and after the Restoration, the name” (meaning the name Jew), “however, was extended to all the Hebrew nation without distinction.” And then it references the biblical data behind this usage in Esther and Daniel and Ezra Okay, so that’s a very important distinction. It tells us that, in the Bible, the meaning of the word "Jew" changed over time. So when we come across the word "Jew" in the Bible, it’s vital that we understand where we are along the timeline. Because that word means something different in 2nd Kings than it does in Daniel. The International Standard Bible Encyclopaedia agrees with Easton. Their entry for Jew says, “Jew denotes originally an inhabitant of Judah…” And here they refer to 2nd Kings, where it, “applies to the two tribes of the Southern Kingdom. But later the meaning was extended to embrace all descendants of Abraham.” And it lists several appearances of the word in the Old Testament. And it goes on to say, “Jews” (always plural) is the familiar term for Israelites in the Gospels.” And it gives the biblical data behind that usage in John and Acts and Ephesians. And for good measure, let’s also look at the Lexham Bible Dictionary’s entry called “Jews in the New Testament.” It says this: “In general, the New Testament uses the term “the Jews” to refer to the biological descendants of Abraham, but the term was also used to characterize specific groups of Jewish people who rejected Jesus as the Messiah.” So ever since the return of Israel after their exile in Babylon, the word "Jews" has been used either to refer to the biological descendants of Abraham (regardless of tribal affiliation) or the specific group of Jewish people who rejected Jesus, which we find in passages like John 5:18, which says “This was why the Jews were seeking all the more to kill him…” John obviously doesn’t mean every biological descendent of Abraham wanted to kill Jesus. Especially since Jesus and his disciples (and most of His early followers) were all descendants of Abraham themselves! So the biblical definition of the word “Jew” at the time of Jesus was the same definition we use today. It wasn’t limited to the southern kingdom of Judah. The term “Jews” in the New Testament refers to all descendants of Abraham, also known as Israelites. In fact, Paul uses the terms “Israelite” and “Jew” interchangeably when he’s describing himself. For example, “For I myself am an Israelite, a descendant of Abraham, a member of the tribe of Benjamin.” “We ourselves are Jews by birth and not Gentile sinners.” “Are they Hebrews? So am I. Are they Israelites? So am I. Are they offspring of Abraham? So am I." “In order somehow to make my fellow Jews jealous, and thus save some of them.” So despite the origin of the word “Jew,” when we read that word in the Bible, it's really important to know the context. And in the New Testament, it never refers solely to the southern kingdom of Judah. The other important word to look at is "Gentiles." And here Mr. Staley is a little ambiguous when he uses this term. He never makes a clear statement about the definition of a Gentile. So let’s piece together what he says in his own words. Now, sometimes he will use the word “Gentiles” in the traditional, plain sense of referring to people who are not Jewish. For example, in talking about Amos 9:9, Mr. Staley says this: “Because it’s the northern house of Israel that was prophesied to be scattered into the four corners of the Earth, not the southern kingdom. Some of them were scattered, but it was the north that was prophesied to be amongst all of the nations. So he says I will sift the house of Israel among the Gentiles, telling us that they’re going to be found among the Gentiles.” So the northern 10 tribes were scattered among the Gentiles, the non-Jews. But they were still distinct from them. But that starts to change: “Listen to this: The 10 tribes were taken into captivity and dispersed across all of Assyria. Over time they assimilated into the nations. And they became as goyim, Gentiles. They knew their identity, this is important. They had not lost their identity at this point. they knew their identity the entire time that they were amongst the Gentiles. They knew their genealogy, they knew that they were from the tribe of Dan, ‘I’m a…you know, I’m from Naphtali, I’m from Asher, I’m Gad.’ But over time they became indistinguishable in word and deed. In other words, they may have knew their family genealogy, but they acted and talked, dressed, looked just like the rest of the world.” So he says the 10 northern tribes assimilated into the Gentiles and started to act like Gentiles but still knew they were actually Israelites. And then Mr. Staley says that somewhere along the way, those tribes began to be seen as Gentiles. “People had no idea which one was a tribe of Dan, which one was this person, that person. From a Greek’s perspective, ‘This is my friend, he’s a Greek.’ They don’t even know! Now that person knows. They know themselves where they come from, but most likely no one else did because they assimilated into the culture.” It’s important to note that most of this is speculation on the part of Mr. Staley. The Bible doesn’t actually speak to the process he lays out one way or the other. And as we just saw with the day of Pentecost in Acts 2, Jews from every nation under heaven gathered in Jerusalem for the feast of Shavuot. So these were certainly not Jews who acted like the rest of the world or would’ve been mistaken for Greeks. These were people who packed up their families, shut down their houses, asked their neighbors to grab their mail, and traveled to Jerusalem to celebrate a Jewish feast at the temple. Everyone knew they were Jewish. Okay, so now we get to the point where Mr. Staley floats a controversial theory about the Gentiles. “Have you ever wondered why pagan Gentiles who hated the Jews–antisemitic as they come –would so readily accept a Jewish messiah? So a rabbi, Paul, (Rav Sha’ul in his Hebrew tongue), is going around to different cities and he’s supposubly preaching to pagans, and 100,000 people convert to Christ in just the city of Ephesus. All of these are polytheistic pagan gods, Sungod worshipers, they got 1000 different gods, they don’t even need another God, much less a Jewish god, of which they can’t stand to begin with! Why would they accept a Jewish Messiah? I’m going to suggest you something: Because many of these Gentiles were not pagan Gentiles, they were Israelite Gentiles.” Okay, now he’s teaching that there are two types of Gentiles. Pagan Gentiles and Israelite Gentiles. And there are a few things we need to address in this clip. First, this charge of antisemitism among the Gentiles is an anachronistic error. Mr. Staley is importing modern racial theory into the first century, where it did not exist. Even Jewish historians agree that antisemitism did not exist at the time of Jesus. I wrote about that in my book Divergence, and I’ll put a link below to a podcast episode where we look at racial relations in the early church. Secondly, Mr. Staley’s statement also reveals a misunderstanding of polytheistic pagan culture in the Roman Empire. He says, “They don’t even need another God, much less a Jewish god.” But first-century pagans had no problem worshiping multiple gods; adding one more was no big deal. Remember in Acts 17 when Paul stood up at the Areopagus and addressed the pagan philosophers in Athens? He had no problem introducing them to one more God; the real and living Creator God of the Bible. No, the problem that pagans had with Judeo-Christianity, was its insistence there is only one true God, and every other god is a false god. Thirdly, and much more important to the point of our discussion today, is Mr. Staley’s suggestion that the reason so many Gentiles came to faith in Jesus is that many of them were “Israelite Gentiles,” and were, therefore, predisposed to believe in a Jewish Messiah. And, of course, he teaches this in an attempt to lend more credibility to his bizarre theory about “Israelite Gentiles.” But there is literally zero evidence or support for this theory in the Bible. None. And more than that, this idea undermines the saving power of Jesus. As if the large number of Gentile converts in Scripture were a byproduct of being predisposed to a Jewish Messiah, rather than the moving of the Holy Spirit and the beauty and saving power of the Gospel of Jesus. And more than that, his category of “Israelite Gentiles” reveals the absurdity of the definitions. But before we get into that, let’s first establish the definition of the word “Gentiles.” And just for fun, let’s first see how Wikipedia defines it: “Gentile is a word that usually means "someone who is not a Jew.” And Dictionary.com agrees: When used as an adjective it means, “of or relating to any people not Jewish. Or a Christian, as distinguished from Jewish.” When it’s used as a noun it means “a person who is not Jewish, especially a Christian.” So the commonly accepted meaning of the word “Gentile” is anyone who is not a Jew. So let’s see what the Bible says that word means, and we’ll start with Easton’s Bible dictionary: “Gentiles—meaning in general all nations except the Jews." The Lexham Bible Dictionary says, “Gentiles: A word of Latin origin meaning, “belonging to a people.” The Hebrew and Greek words translated as Gentiles mean “people” or “nations.” Bible translations selectively use Gentiles to designate non-Jews." The Logos Factbook simply says “Anyone who is not Jewish.” And it references several passages, including Romans 3:29, “Or is God the God of Jews only? Is he not the God of Gentiles also? Yes, of Gentiles also” So there’s a clear contrast between Jews and Gentiles in Scripture. The biblical authors used the word “Gentiles” to mean the same thing we mean by that word today. A Gentile simply means “anyone who is not Jewish.” In Scripture, there’s no sense in which that word is used to refer to the lost northern tribes. In fact, Gentiles are specifically people of non-Jewish descent. In other words, they didn’t descend from any of the 12 tribes of Israel. So if we accept Mr. Staley’s definitions, it would render the New Testament nonsensical. For example, when Jesus was sending out His twelve apostles He said, “Go nowhere among the Gentiles and enter no town of the Samaritans, but go rather to the lost sheep of the house of Israel.” According to Mr. Staley’s definition, at the time Jesus uttered these words, the house of Israel referred to the ten northern tribes who were located among the Gentiles. So how could Jesus tell them not to go among the Gentiles but instead to the house of Israel? They were the same thing according to Mr. Staley. No, the only way Jesus’s statement to His disciples makes sense is if we understand that the “House of Israel” refers to different people in a different place than the Gentiles. Jesus wanted his Jewish disciples to go to the Jewish villages and towns, not the Gentile areas. And this is why Mr. Staley’s statement in the clip we just saw is so surprising. “Many of these Gentiles were not pagan Gentiles, Based on the biblical definitions we’ve just established, the term “Israelite Gentiles” is meaningless. It’s the logical equivalent of describing someone as a married bachelor. It’s a contradiction. There is no such thing in Scripture as an “Israelite Gentile.” In fact it actually contradicts what the Bible says. And yet Mr. Staley not only repeats this false idea several times in this video, he even uses it to redefine the apostle Paul’s mission. “Many of the people whom Paul preached to were from the Northern House of Israel. No questions asked, that the majority, I believe, of the people that Paul is actually preaching to when he says “I am the apostle to the Gentiles,” of course there are pagan Gentiles, or heathen Gentiles, and Gentiles that are real Gentiles that were never Israelites to begin with. But I’m here to tell you as the sky is blue that our Bible tells us (in the language that Paul is using) what he actually means by “apostle to the Gentiles.” And who he is really talking to is the scattered Northern House of Israel, that the Southern house of Israel called wild dogs and Gentiles.” Now by the time Mr. Staley gets to this particular statement, he’s already an hour and a half into his teaching. So he’s laid a lot of groundwork before he starts making these kinds of dubious comments. But look at what he’s saying! He says when Paul calls himself an apostle to the Gentiles, what he actually means is that he’s an apostle to the scattered northern tribes of Israel. Mr. Staley has twisted Scripture a full 180° to mean the opposite of what it actually says. The Bible says Paul was an apostle to the non-Jewish people, but Mr. Staley says Paul was an apostle to the Jewish people of the northern kingdom. This is a prime example of how the teachings of Torahism can rearrange the tiles and distort the true image. And from this position, Mr. Staley goes on to recast the language in the Bible that talks about Jews and Gentiles. Rather than understanding the New Testament authors to be talking about the distinction between the Jewish people (the descendants of Abraham), and the non-Jewish people (the nations), he reframes many passages as if they are actually speaking to the distinction between the northern and southern kingdoms. For example, he begins replacing the word “Jews” in the biblical text with the phrase “southern kingdom” when he reads Scripture. “Romans 9:24 says this: “Even us whom he called not of the southern kingdom only, but he called of the Gentiles.” Wow. And look at what he does to Romans 11 where Paul is talking about the mystery of the salvation of Israel: “That blindness (we’re back in Romans 11) in part, has happened to Israel (speaking of the Israel that existed in the first century, because that was the definition in the first century to the Jews, was that they were the only Israel that existed) until the fullness of the Gentiles come in.” So it says that blindness in part has happened to the southern kingdom until the fullness of the Gentiles have come in.” That’s another example of these definitions twisting Scripture 180°. And how about the passage in Ephesians 2 about Jesus breaking down the wall between who? Jew and Gentile, right? Not according to Mr. Staley. No, he reinterprets that passage to be speaking about the two houses, as well: “For he himself is our peace, who has been both made both one, and has broken down the middle wall of separation. What’s the middle wall of separation? The southern kingdom of Judah. Because of the divorce of the northern kingdom created a hostility wall of separation between the Jew and the house of Israel, Gentiles, that were living among the Gentiles, saying that you can never be part of the commonwealth of Israel because you guys were divorced.” Okay, I think you get the picture. I just wanted to present enough of Mr. Staley’s own words so that you understand I’m not making this stuff up. He is messing with the storyline of Scripture and inserting ideas that aren’t taught in the text. And that’s scary stuff. Here’s where things start getting really dangerous with Mr. Staley’s theology. He is now going to use these erroneous definitions to establish the reason Jesus came. Watch how he structures his argument. He starts by reminding us that the northern kingdom was still lost in the first century. “You guys assimilated into the nations, and you never came back, and God divorced you for it. You can’t come back. It doesn’t matter how bad you want to come back. and by the way, we’ll let you come back, but you’ve got to convert to the southern kingdom.” So there’s that idea again that if the northern tribes wanted to return to Israel, they would have to convert to the southern kingdom first. What does he mean by “convert to the southern kingdom”? He never actually explains that in this video. It's possible he means the northern tribes who assimilated among the Gentiles were no longer worshipping the God of Israel and had become pagans. And so they needed to convert back to Judaism in order to be let back into Israel. That would make sense of his statements here. But it would make a mess of the Bible. Because, again, we saw Jews from every nation under heaven gathered in Jerusalem keeping the feast of Shavuot together. They were already worshiping the God of Israel. What would they convert to? Nevertheless, this is what Mr. Staley is teaching. And he goes on to tell us that those northern tribes were never going to convert. “No way was a Israelite from the northern kingdom going to convert to Judah. Not going to happen. They have their own tribal identity. Very few did this. More of them were pagan Gentiles that were not Israelites that actually converted to Judaism, than ever an Israelite from the northern kingdom. So we have a major problem. Because this becomes the mystery that Paul talks about. There’s several mysteries, but the large mystery is how does God solve this?” Okay, so he’s repeating the claim that we disproved earlier that there was ongoing hostility between the tribes in the first century. And he’s now presented us with the mystery of how God is going to reconcile the two divided kingdoms of Judah and Israel. Which isn’t a mystery that is really posed in the Bible. Rather, in the storyline of Scripture, what we see is that after the Israelites returned from exile in Babylon, the whole issue of tribal affiliation sort of fades away. It’s just not talked about. And as we saw earlier, by the time we get to the New Testament, there is no lingering mystery about the tribes. The two houses aren’t even mentioned. Instead, the story of Jesus and the spreading of the Gospel and the growth of the early church proceeds through the pages of the New Testament as if the tribes aren’t lost and the two houses aren’t at odds with one another. And while Scripture is silent on this issue, it’s a central tenet of Mr. Staley’s theology. And here is another example of rearranging the tiles into the shape of a distorted king. And next he goes on to eloquently speak about the bride of Christ. And he says a lot of true things here. And the Bride of Christ, of course, is a metaphor in Scripture that illustrates the relationship between Jesus and His Church. Ephesians 5 talks about how Jesus serves as the bridegroom of the Church, and loves His bride by offering Himself as a sacrifice for her. In response, the Church submits to Jesus and remains faithful to Him as His bride. And this metaphor comes right out of the Old Testament image of Israel as the bride of Yahweh. Isaiah wrote, “As the bridegroom rejoices over the bride, so shall your God rejoice over you.” A common theme in the Old Testament is faithfulness and loyalty in the marriage relationship between God and His covenant people. God always remains faithful, but His people rarely do, and their unfaithfulness is compared to adultery and prostitution. In fact, this is how Israel broke the Mosaic Covenant given at Sinai, and why a new covenant was promised. The prophet Jeremiah wrote, “Behold, the days are coming, declares the Lord, when I will make a new covenant with the house of Israel and the house of Judah”—And remember this prophecy was written more than 600 years before Jesus—“not like the covenant that I made with their fathers on the day when I took them by the hand to bring them out of the land of Egypt, my covenant that they broke, though I was their husband, declares the Lord.” And Mr. Staley teaches most of this beautifully. And his audience responds appropriately by applauding when he declares that Christ died for his bride. Because that is an amazing biblical truth. And he then asks a really interesting theological question. After talking about why Christ died, he now asks why He was resurrected? After all, it was the blood that atoned for our sin, and the Old Testament blood sacrifices never were resurrected. So why did Jesus come back to life? And this is where Mr. Staley’s teaching drifts seamlessly out of the truth of Scripture and into foreign ideas. And in this next clip, he’s going to say a number of things that should cause our antennae to go up. And I’m not sure if he realizes how dangerous his statements are here. Listen carefully as he tells us why Jesus rose from the dead and what that means. “He rises from the dead because he’s creating an eligible bachelor for his bride to marry. That is why he rises from the dead. He says, ‘Here, here, pick me! Choose me! Don’t go amongst the nations anymore. I’ve broken the curse.’ The curse for the northern house of Israel is they could never come back. And the Jew knew that. So when Paul goes to the elect, to the ones scattered in Galatia, in Ephesia, and all across that known world of the Mediterranean, and he walks into the cities, where do you think he’s going to go? First he goes to the Jew in the synagogue. And then he goes out to the Gentiles. He goes out to the Northern House of Israel into their streets and says, ‘Gather around all of you Danites, Naphtalites, all you Gentiles that have lost your identity and don’t even care. I got some good news for you. You can come back.’” So let’s unpack this clip a bit and let me show you exactly how Mr. Staley rearranges the proverbial tiles. And remember in our analogy with the image of the kings, the individual tiles represent passages and texts and truth of Scripture. And the false teacher rearranges and redefines those tiles to fashion their own narrative. So let’s look at four tiles that Mr. Staley reconfigures. Tile #1: The Nations - Mr. Staley has the resurrected Jesus saying “Choose me! Don’t go amongst the nations anymore.” And this is because he believes that when someone comes to faith in Jesus, they are no longer Gentiles, they are no longer of the nations. (And we’ll hear him say that in his own words in a bit.) But what did the resurrected Jesus say about the nations? In what is commonly referred to as the Great Commission, Jesus said, “Go therefore and make disciples of all nations, baptizing them in the name of the Father and of the Son and of the Holy Spirit.” And the Greek word there behind the phrase “all nations” is ἔθνος, which is the same word behind the term Gentiles elsewhere in the New Testament. Jesus said the nations, the Gentiles, were all invited into His kingdom. We read the same thing in Acts chapter 1, in the last recorded statement of the resurrected Jesus before He ascended into heaven, He told His apostles, “But you will receive power when the Holy Spirit has come upon you,”—and he’s talking here about Pentecost, which we read about in Acts 2— “and you will be my witnesses” (where?) “in Jerusalem and in all Judea and Samaria, and to the end of the earth.” Tile #2: Jesus is the bridegroom - Is Jesus the bridegroom? Absolutely! And did He rise from the dead to create an eligible bachelor for His bride to marry? No, that’s not in Scripture. His resurrection accomplished a lot of crucial things, but creating an eligible bachelor isn’t one of them. Rather, the resurrection of Jesus demonstrated the power of God over death, and it validated that Jesus was who He claimed to be, and it fulfilled the Old Testament prophecies that foretold of the Messiah’s suffering and resurrection, and it validated His own prophecies that He would be raised on the third day. Jesus was the firstfruits of those who have fallen asleep, so His resurrection paved the way for us. Our Messiah is someone who transcended death and promised that those who follow Him will do the same. The sinless Son of God didn’t need to make himself eligible for his bride. No, it’s the other way around. He died to wash His sinful bride clean, to make her eligible for the Bridegroom. Tile #3: Jesus broke the curse - Did Jesus break the curse? Of course! And did the curse He broke have to do with the northern house of Israel and how they could never come back? No, that’s not in the Bible. The curses we read about in Scripture (and they’re listed in Deuteronomy 28) are the punishment for breaking the Sinai covenant by disobeying the Law of Moses. Remember God said if Israel would obey His laws they would be blessed, and if they disobeyed they would be cursed. And Deuteronomy 28:15 says, “But if you will not obey the voice of the Lord your God or be careful to do all his commandments and his statutes that I command you today, then all these curses shall come upon you and overtake you.” And then the text in that chapter goes on to list the curses, which by the way, applied to all 12 tribes, long before Israel was divided. And the curse attached to the law of Moses is the only curse that the New Testament speaks of Jesus breaking. We read about it in Galatians 3. “For all who rely on works of the law are under a curse; for it is written, ‘Cursed be everyone who does not abide by all things written in the Book of the Law, and do them.’” And that is a quote from Deuteronomy 27. And the text goes on to say, “Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us—for it is written, “Cursed is everyone who is hanged on a tree”—so that in Christ Jesus the blessing of Abraham might come to the Gentiles, so that we might receive the promised Spirit through faith.” The curse Jesus redeemed us from was the curse of the law; it was the curse established for those Israelites (of all 12 tribes) who did not keep the law. And Paul, of course, goes on to say that before Jesus came, we were captive under the law, but now that Jesus has come, we’re no under the law. There is nothing in the New Testament about Jesus breaking a curse for the northern tribes to allow them to “come back.” I’m sorry, but that's biblcal fiction. In fact, Galatians 3 says that “Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law” Why? “So that in Christ Jesus the blessing of Abraham might come to the…Gentiles!” And as we’ve already established, whenever we read the word “Gentiles” in the New Testament, it refers to non-Jews. Tile #4: Paul went to the Gentiles - Was Paul the apostle to the Gentiles? And, of course, we’ve already looked at the ways in which Mr. Staley redefines this particular tile. And in the clip we just saw, he repeats the idea that we disproved earlier that when we read the word “Gentiles” in the New Testament, it is actually referring to the lost tribes. He’s compounding that error here. Instead of Paul going out to the Gentiles, the non-Jews, and preaching the gospel of Jesus, as the New Testament describes, and as God promised, and as Jesus commanded us in the Great Commission, Mr. Staley has Paul going out to the lost northern tribes of Israel and telling them they can come back. Which is more biblical fiction. It is an idea that we do not read about anywhere So why does Mr. Stanley spend so much time and energy promoting this false narrative about the Gentiles being the lost tribes of Israel? It is all part of his quest to redefine the identity of followers of Jesus. So where is all this leading? What does Mr. Staley want us to conclude from all of this? Let’s let him tell us in his words. “With whom is the new covenant made? Jeremiah 31, verse 31 says ‘behold the days are coming, says the Lord, when I will make a new covenant with the house Israel and the house of Judah.’ You’ll notice there is no house of Gentiles….If you haven’t figured it out by now ladies and gentlemen, as a follower of Jesus, you’re part of Israel. You’re part of Israel. You’re not separate from Israel. It is not Israel the Gentiles, and the New Covenant with the house of Israel, House of Judah, the New Covenant’s with the Gentiles… our own theology doesn’t work! It says that the new covenant is only with the house of Israel and the house of Judah. You’ve been grafted into the commonwealth of Israel, and have been made partakers in the covenants of promise.” And to his credit, Mr. Staley goes on to clarify that he is talking about a spiritual Israel, not about the physical 12 tribes. And this clip here makes a ton of sense. Paul refers to believers in Jesus as the “Israel of God.” And the New Testament talks about how Gentiles who come to faith in Jesus are grafted in, we’re adopted as sons and daughters into the pre-existing family of God. Ephesians 2 says Gentiles “are no longer foreigners and strangers, but fellow citizens with God’s people and also members of his household.” Galatians 3 says, “And if you are Christ's, then you are Abraham's seed, heirs according to promise.” Anyone who has come to faith in Jesus, whether Jew or Gentile, is an heir of the promise that God gave to Abraham. And in so far as Mr. Staley's statement agrees with these passages of Scripture, I think we can all give him a big amen! And there is one issue I want to clarify, which is this idea that the new covenant was only made with the House of Israel and the House of Judah. And we need to remember that this prophecy was given 600 years before Jesus, and at that time the kingdom was divided. So explicitly mentioning the House of Israel and the House of Judah communicated a couple things to Jeremiah’s audience. First of all, it indicated in no uncertain terms that God was talking about the entirety of his people, which, at that time, did not include the Gentiles. So we would never expect there to be a “house of Gentiles.” In fact, that idea reveals a complete misunderstanding of what the phrase “house of” means in Hebrew. When we see “house of” before a name, it refers to the descendants, the family, the household of that person. And remember after wrestling with God, Jacob’s name was changed to Israel. So all who descended from this man named Israel, which would be all 12 tribes, are referred to in Hebrew as בֵּ֨ית יִשְׂרָאֵ֜ל, the house of Israel. And since the term Gentiles refers to people descended from many nations, the very concept of a “house of” Gentiles, is just silly. As if they were all descendants of someone named "Gentile"? So anyway, Jeremiah’s prophecy of a new covenant that would be made with the House of Israel and the House of Judah, that prophecy was specifically given to the southern kingdom, because that’s all that existed at that time. That prophecy came after the northern kingdom had been scattered. And so it would’ve been a clear signal of hope to the Israelites who heard it. The southern kingdom was sitting in the ruins of the once great kingdom of Israel, after God had allowed them to be severely punished for breaking the Mosaic covenant. And the prophet Jeremiah was pointing them to a hopeful future in which the entirety of God’s people would be reunited and restored under a new covenant. And under the New Covenant that Jesus brought us, the definition of God’s People was expanded to include Gentiles who have placed their faith in Jesus. So Mr. Staley's statement about Gentiles being adopted into Israel in a spiritual sense is very biblical. And this is exactly why his teachings are so dangerous. Because he gets so many things right. He’s an intelligent man and knows his Bible. Which tends to make us want to accept everything he says as equally true. And as we’ve seen, that’s simply not the case. This means we need to be extra careful and lean in and test things against Scripture. Because he goes on to conclude this: “So when someone comes into Israel and follows the God of Israel, they’re no longer a Gentile, they’re a Gentile Israelite, they’re Gentile Hebrew. They’re part of the commonwealth of Israel, and by default, they’re part of that inheritance.” Okay, so we’ve already established that a “Gentile Israelite” is a logical contradiction on par with a “married bachelor.” It's nonsensical. So Mr. Staley says once we come to faith in Christ, we’re no longer Gentiles. But the apostle Paul says that Yahweh is the God of both Jews and Gentiles, and so His kingdom is comprised of both Jews and Gentiles. In fact, 1 Corinthians 7 says that if we were Jewish when we came to faith, we’re to remain Jewish. And if we were Gentile, to remain Gentile. It says, “Only let each person lead the life that the Lord has assigned to him, and to which God has called him.” Our Jewishness or Gentile-ness is something that was assigned to us by God. “This is my rule in all the churches. Was anyone at the time of his call already circumcised?” Was he Jewish? “Let him not seek to remove the marks of circumcision. Was anyone at the time of his call uncircumcised?” Was he a Gentile? “Let him not seek circumcision.” He doesn’t need to become Jewish. So the body of Christ in Scripture is made up of both Jews and Gentiles dwelling together in unity. This is what we see in God’s kingdom in the final days. Revelation 7 paints a beautiful picture of all 12 tribes of Israel listed by name plus, “a great multitude that no one could number, from every nation, from all tribes and peoples and languages, standing before the throne and before the Lamb, clothed in white robes, with palm branches in their hands.” This is the fulfillment of God’s promise to Abraham that in him—meaning through his descendants, the Jewish people—all the families of the nations will be blessed, both Jews and Gentiles. And Yahweh will be the God of them all. Yet Mr. Staley preaches the end of the Gentiles. He says, “So when someone comes into Israel and follows the God of Israel, they’re no longer a Gentile, they’re a Gentile Israelite.” He has rearranged the tiles and distorted the body of Christ, which, in turn, reduces the scope of the Gospel. Mr. Staley has invented a third category of people not found in the Bible; the mythical "Israelite Gentile." And even worse, he teaches that the biblical category of Gentiles is ending. And yet five separate times in the book of Revelation we’re told that in God’s ultimate kingdom, there will be people from every tribe and language and people and nation. So if every believer gets grafted into Israel and becomes part of Israel and is assigned to a tribe in Israel and is no longer a Gentile, who are all these people from every tribe and nation? Okay let’s do a quick recap of what we’ve just discussed and look at the three primary reasons that the errors and distortions we’ve seen from Mr. Staley (and from Two House theology in general) are dangerous. First of all, this theology unbiblical. We looked at a number of statements from Mr. Staley and compared them to Scripture and found they just don’t match. There are many aspects of two-house theology, and Torahism as a whole, that directly contradict what Scripture says. They distort the image made by all those tiles. Secondly, and more specifically, as we saw, Two-House theology undermines God’s promise to Abraham (that in him all the families of the Earth would be blessed), and it reduces the Body of Christ into one homogenous people group. Yahweh is not the God of Israel alone or of the Gentiles alone. Revelation 7 gives us a clear picture of His kingdom; and it includes—to use the New Testament idiom “Jews and Greeks”—it includes everyone who has placed their faith in Jesus. It is multicultural and multi-ethnic, and we find every people group joined together in unity worshipping God. How beautiful is that? There is a dangerous theology called Supersessionism, also known as Replacement Theology, which teaches that the New Testament Church has superseded or taken the place of Israel in God’s plans. And this is a terribly unbiblical concept that undermines that beautiful picture of heaven by rejecting Israel. And this two-house theology taught by Mr. Staley and other Hebrew Roots teachers makes the same exact error in the opposite direction. It’s reverse Replacement Theology! Or we might call it Subsumptionism because it teaches that the Gentile identity will be subsumed or absorbed into Israel. And both Supersessionism and Subsumptionism are dead wrong for the exact same reason. They each teach that when all is said and done, there is just one people group in God’s Kingdom. Supersessionism says there are no more Jews. Subsumptionism says there are no more Gentiles. But the Bible clearly and consistently teaches that God’s kingdom, the body of Christ, will contain both. And lastly, two-house theology is dangerous because it ultimately leads to Gentile believers taking on the false burden of keeping the Law, specifically the Mosaic rituals. The chain of logic used in Torahism says this; Because you are no longer Gentiles, but have been grafted into Israel, you are now an Israelite and bound by the laws that God gave to Israel. And of course, the gigantic error in this logic is that, while Gentiles have been adopted into God’s pre-existing family—that much is true—because of the work of Jesus, the family rules have changed… for everybody! The New Testament unequivocally teaches that spiritual Israel—which is made up of Jewish and Gentile believers in Jesus—is not governed by the Law of Moses. Galatians 3 says, “Now before faith came, (meaning before Jesus arrived) we were held captive under the law, imprisoned until the coming faith would be revealed. So then, the law was our guardian until Christ came, in order that we might be justified by faith. But now that faith has come, we are no longer under a guardian,” no longer under the law. And by the way, we unpack this passage in great detail in part 4 of our Bible study on Galatians. And I’ll link to that video below. So keeping things like the Torah feasts, or the weekly Sabbath, or a kosher diet are certainly allowed for followers of Jesus. Those things were never forbidden. We are free in Christ to keep them if we choose. And I think doing so is especially appropriate for Jewish believers in Jesus, for whom these things are ancient traditions and boundary markers of their Jewish identity. But contrary to what Mr. Staley and other Hebrew Roots teachers want you to believe, keeping these things is not required of followers of Jesus in any sense. They are not a requirement of salvation, or righteousness, or obedience. There is not a single thing any of us could do, no ritual or works of the law, that could possibly add even a microgram to the righteousness that is ours through faith in Jesus alone. And what happens when we begin to keep those rituals—and I’ve seen it time and again—we begin to feel like those rituals make us a little more righteous, a little more spiritual, and make God a little more pleased with us. And that leads to the biggest danger of all: which is pulling our focus away from Jesus and the Gospel and placing it on ourselves and our works. Don’t get me wrong: the Torah, the law, Moses, Israel, the prophets… these are all foundational parts of the Christian faith. And every one of those things was given to point us to Christ. Jesus Himself taught, “You search the Scriptures because you think that in them you have eternal life,” and the Scriptures he’s referring to here are the Jewish Bible, the Tanakh, what we call the Old Testament today. He says, “You search those Scriptures because you think that in them you have eternal life, and it is they that bear witness about me, yet you refuse to come to me that you may have life…For if you believed Moses, you would believe me; for he wrote of me.” The entire history of God’s people—from the Garden, to the nation of Israel, to the prophets—were all pointed in one direction: toward Jesus. And any theology or teaching that tries to pull our focus away from Christ, is a dangerous distortion of Scripture. Thank you so much for watching, I hope you found this video helpful. Let me know your thoughts in the comments below. Shalom.

- The HARSH TRUTH About IDENTITY CRISIS After 60 (Carl Jung’s Wisdom): Kind: captions Language: en The house is quiet, too quiet. Your children are gone and you feel empty. Like you don't even know who you are anymore. For 20, maybe 30 years, you were mom or dad. That was your identity, your purpose, your entire world. But now, now you're staring at yourself in the mirror asking, "Who am I when I'm not needed anymore?" If you felt this crushing identity crisis, what I'm about to reveal will shock you. Because Carl Young discovered something about empty nest syndrome that most therapists won't tell you and it changes everything. The shocking truth about emptity crisis. Here's what no one tells you about emptiness syndrome. It's not a disorder. It's not weakness. It's actually a psychological necessity. Carl Jung studied this phenomenon for decades. He discovered that parents who sacrificed their entire identity for their children inevitably face this crisis. And here's the harsh truth. You were supposed to feel lost because you became so fused with the role of parent that you forgot the person underneath. One mother told me, "I spent 25 years being everything to my children." When they left, I realized I didn't even know what I like to eat for breakfast. This isn't failure. This is the natural consequence of complete self-sacrifice. But Young understood something revolutionary. This crisis isn't your ending. It's your awakening. Why most empty nesters stay stuck. Most parents facing empty nest syndrome make the same mistake. They try to fill the void by becoming even more involved in their adult children's lives. calling constantly, giving unsolicited advice, planning visits they're not invited to. This is psychological desperation masquerading as love. Jung called this unconscious projection. You're projecting your need to feel needed onto children who are trying to become independent adults. And here's the brutal truth. The more you chase them, the further they pull away. Your adult children don't need another parent. They need you to become a whole person again. Because when you're lost, you can't guide anyone, including yourself. Jung's revolutionary discovery. Carl Young discovered that life has two distinct phases. The first half, building external identities, career, family, social roles. The second half, discovering who you actually are beneath all those roles. Most people get stuck in the first half. They become so identified with their roles that when those roles change or end, they feel like they're dying. But Jung realized you're not dying. You're being reborn. The empty nest isn't a tragedy. It's an invitation to individuation. The process of becoming your authentic self. For the first time in decades, you have the space to ask, "What do I want? What brings me alive? Who am I when no one else is watching?" This is terrifying. And it's supposed to be the four stages every empty neester must face. Young identified four stages of this transformation. Stage one, the grief. Most people skip this. You must mourn the parent you were. That version of you served their purpose. Honor them. Thank them. Then let them go. Stage two, the silence. This scares most people. In the quiet house, listen. What parts of yourself have been buried under decades of caregiving. Stage three, the exploration. Most people judge this as selfish. Start small. Take that art class. Join that hiking group. Travel somewhere you've always wanted to go. This isn't selfish, it's essential. Stage four, the integration. This is where magic happens. You don't stop being a parent. You become a parent and a whole person. The hidden gift no one talks about. Here's what shocked me most about Jung's research. Parents who successfully navigate empty nest syndrome often have better relationships with their adult children because when you stop being just their parent and become a complete person, they can finally see you as a human being. Your children don't want you to be lost without them. They want you to be proud of who you are. When you model wholeness, you give them permission to live their own lives without guilt. This is the greatest gift you can give them, your own fulfillment. Loneliness after 60 isn't a disease to be cured. It's a wake-up call to be answered. Carl Young understood what most people never learn. The relationship you have with yourself determines the quality of every other relationship in your life. If you can't stand being alone with yourself, no amount of other people will ever be enough. But if you learn to be at peace with who you are, really at peace, then solitude becomes a choice, not a sentence. Your loneliness isn't telling you that you need more people. It's telling you that you need to finally meet yourself. The question isn't, how do I find more connections? The question is, who am I when no one else is watching? And am I brave enough to find out? If this video opened your eyes to the truth about emptiness syndrome and loneliness after 60, know that you're not alone. Subscribe now. I'm here to reveal the hidden psychology of aging that few will ever tell you. And if you're ready to go deeper into Carl Jung's insights, just watch the video appearing on your screen. Your second half of life is waiting. Are you ready to claim it?

So,&nbsp;&nbsp; it could not have happened in the first century&nbsp; or Judaism itself would not even be having this&nbsp;&nbsp; conversation because Rabbi Eliezer says in&nbsp; response, “As this day just as this day darkens&nbsp;&nbsp; and then becomes light again so the ten tribes&nbsp; even as it went dark for them, so it will become&nbsp;&nbsp; light for them.” So, the point is that we’ve&nbsp; got written record of two rabbis going at it,&nbsp;&nbsp; saying, “I don’t believe they’re going to come&nbsp; back.” “I do believe they’re going to come back.”&nbsp;&nbsp; So, what by default can we deduct&nbsp; from both of these? They’re not back.&nbsp;&nbsp; Even the Israel Ministry of Foreign Affairs –&nbsp; the IMFA says this, “The exile to Babylonia,&nbsp;&nbsp; which followed the destruction&nbsp; of the First Temple (in 586 BC)&nbsp;&nbsp; marked the beginning of the Jewish Diaspora.”&nbsp; Do you realize how important this is for the&nbsp;&nbsp; IMFA to make this statement because the northern&nbsp; kingdom was exiled 130 years earlier? By default,&nbsp;&nbsp; they’re admitting that the first dispersion was&nbsp; not the Jewish people but the House of Israel.&nbsp;&nbsp; For you Gentiles, this may not be an important&nbsp; message, but I know for my Jewish brethren,&nbsp;&nbsp; this is an important slide&nbsp; because you have all been taught&nbsp;&nbsp; generically that the Jewish people were&nbsp; dispersed and that was all twelve tribes.&nbsp;&nbsp; But even your own organizations, again, at the&nbsp; highest levels of academia admit that the first&nbsp;&nbsp; dispersion of the Jewish people was 586 BC going&nbsp; to Babylon because that’s the House of Judah.&nbsp;&nbsp; Let’s get in to the New Testament a little&nbsp; bit and let me ask this paramount question,&nbsp;&nbsp; “Have you ever wondered why pagan gentiles&nbsp; who hated the Jews, anti-Semitic as they come,&nbsp;&nbsp; would so readily accept a Jewish Messiah?” So,&nbsp; a Rabbi Paul, Rav Shaul, in his Hebrew tongue,&nbsp;&nbsp; is going around to different cites, and he&nbsp; is supposedly preaching to pagans, and a&nbsp;&nbsp; hundred thousand people converted to Christ&nbsp; in just the city of Ephesus. All of these are&nbsp;&nbsp; polytheistic pagan gods, sun god worshippers –&nbsp; they got a thousand different gods. They don’t&nbsp;&nbsp; even need another god, much less a Jewish&nbsp; god of which they can’t stand to begin with.&nbsp;&nbsp; Why would they accept a Jewish Messiah?&nbsp; I’m going to suggest to you something:&nbsp;&nbsp; because many of these Gentiles were not&nbsp; pagan Gentiles. They were Israelite Gentiles.&nbsp;&nbsp; Let’s dig into this and find out if this crazy&nbsp; theory is true. Peter says this in verse 1 -- now&nbsp;&nbsp; you have some definitions from the Tanakh,&nbsp; the Old Testament, we can actually unravel&nbsp;&nbsp; some incredible mysteries in the New Testament.&nbsp; (1 Peter 1:1) “Peter, an apostle of Jesus Christ,&nbsp;&nbsp; to the strangers scattered throughout Pontus,&nbsp; Galatia, Cappadocia, Asia, and Bithynia, elect&nbsp;&nbsp; according to the foreknowledge of God&nbsp; the Father…” Right off the bat, where&nbsp;&nbsp; Peter is using terminology that he’s not making&nbsp; up. Every one of his Jewish brethren knows that&nbsp;&nbsp; the word “scattered” comes from the prophecies&nbsp; of scattering the northern House of Israel.&nbsp;&nbsp; There’s only one people in the entire&nbsp; Bible that are called “the elect,”&nbsp;&nbsp; and that is Israel of the Old Testament.&nbsp;&nbsp; Now, some of you, it might not bother you,&nbsp; but let me finish what I’m going to say.&nbsp;&nbsp; We’ve been talking for hundreds of years now&nbsp; trying to figure out this whole doctrine of&nbsp;&nbsp; election. Books have been written, Calvinism,&nbsp; Armenianism. You know, once saved always saved,&nbsp;&nbsp; all of these things. The doctrine of election is&nbsp; very simple, and pre-destination is very simple.&nbsp;&nbsp; God elected His people, scattered them,&nbsp; pre-destined for the elect to come back.&nbsp;&nbsp; That is the definition of pre-destination right&nbsp; there in a nutshell. You can’t look at it from&nbsp;&nbsp; your 21st century Western Greco-Roman&nbsp; post-Catholic backgrounds in English,&nbsp;&nbsp; but if you dive into the original Scriptures, and&nbsp; you dive into the Tanakh and the Old Testament in&nbsp;&nbsp; the Hebrew Scriptures and the concepts that&nbsp; are there, if God’s people are elect, He&nbsp;&nbsp; moves them into captivity and a select&nbsp; elect – those that obey His voice and&nbsp;&nbsp; His commandments and His ordinances,&nbsp; so on and so forth – become the remnant&nbsp;&nbsp; that are predestined to come back. And&nbsp; that’s what we see in the New Testament.&nbsp;&nbsp; And I want to point out something else here&nbsp; that’s really interesting. Peter tells us that the&nbsp;&nbsp; scattered are throughout Pontus and what? These were the words which&nbsp; you shall speak to the children of Israel.” So,&nbsp;&nbsp; in the Bible, there’s a law called the Law of&nbsp; Hermeneutics – one of the Hermeneutical principles&nbsp;&nbsp; is the law of precedent, and it says that&nbsp; the first time that something is used tells&nbsp;&nbsp; you the definition. It tells you how it&nbsp; is used in the rest of the Scriptures.&nbsp;&nbsp; So, this is telling us that “the kingdom&nbsp; of priest and a holy nation” phrase,&nbsp;&nbsp; he is speaking to the children of&nbsp; Israel. Hermeneutically speaking,&nbsp;&nbsp; and seminary students will tell&nbsp; you this, you can’t change it.&nbsp;&nbsp; It’s illegal. You cannot change what God said.&nbsp; This is the definition, if God said that the&nbsp;&nbsp; children of Israel are likened unto blossoms in&nbsp; a garden, you cannot say, “the rocks.” You can’t&nbsp;&nbsp; do that. Whatever He says is what it’ll always&nbsp; be. So if He says that the children of Israel&nbsp;&nbsp; are a kingdom of priests and a holy nation, then&nbsp; anytime that you see the New Testament authors&nbsp;&nbsp; use that, you have to ask yourself, “Where did&nbsp; they get that from?” They didn’t make it up.&nbsp;&nbsp; They’re quoting Scripture because they are trying&nbsp; to make a case to the Jewish people that Jesus&nbsp;&nbsp; is the Jewish Messiah. Verse 24, “For if you were cut out of&nbsp; the olive tree which is wild by nature, and were&nbsp;&nbsp; grafted contrary to nature into a cultivated&nbsp; olive tree, how much more will these, who&nbsp;&nbsp; are natural branches, be grafted into their own&nbsp; olive tree?” So, we’ve got an olive tree. We’ve&nbsp;&nbsp; got a wild olive tree. We’ve got a cultivated&nbsp; olive tree. And we’ve got from the olive tree,&nbsp;&nbsp; we’ve got broken branches and other branches&nbsp; being grafted in. That’s what going on. We got&nbsp;&nbsp; an agricultural example going on here. So what&nbsp; is an olive tree? A good Bible student will say,&nbsp;&nbsp; “Where is Paul getting this concept from? Because if you&nbsp;&nbsp; go back to Jeremiah 11 it is the only place in the&nbsp; Bible which tells us the definition of an olive&nbsp;&nbsp; tree from God’s perspective. (Speaking about his&nbsp; people Israel) “The Lord called your name, Green&nbsp;&nbsp; Olive Tree. Lovely and of good fruit. This is the actual flag of Israel,&nbsp;&nbsp; if you will. The identity symbol of Israel,&nbsp; according to the Scriptures, is the menorah, which&nbsp;&nbsp; is the seven branched candlestick that stood in&nbsp; the holy place which represented the Word of God&nbsp;&nbsp; that is light for the two olive trees that are&nbsp; the two houses of Israel. We turn to Ezekiel 37&nbsp;&nbsp; to add a little bit of weight to this, Verse 16,&nbsp; “As for you, son of man, take a stick for yourself&nbsp;&nbsp; and write on it: For Judah and for the children&nbsp; of Israel, his companions. They should&nbsp; have put trees. Because it’s two olive trees.&nbsp;&nbsp; One with Judah, one with Joseph. The&nbsp; two come together and become one again.&nbsp;&nbsp; Verse 21, “Then say to them, thus says the Lord&nbsp; God: Surely I will take the children of Israel&nbsp;&nbsp; from among the nations…” Again, I am beating&nbsp; this into the ground because it’s so critical.&nbsp;&nbsp; “…And I will make them one nation in the land,&nbsp; and one king will be king over them all. "And I saw that&nbsp;&nbsp; for all the adulteries of faithless Israel, I had&nbsp; sent her away and given her a writ of divorce,&nbsp;&nbsp; yet her treacherous sister Judah did not fear;&nbsp; but she went and was a harlot also."
