But He says,&nbsp;&nbsp; "But you have played the harlot with many&nbsp; lovers; yet you return to me,” says the Lord.&nbsp;&nbsp; Big problem because Deuteronomy Chapter 24&nbsp; gives us the Torah commandment against this.&nbsp;&nbsp; "When a man takes a wife and marries her, and&nbsp; it happens that she finds no favor in his eyes&nbsp;&nbsp; because he has found some indecency in her,"&nbsp; talking about fortification, "…and he writes&nbsp;&nbsp; her a certificate of divorce and puts it in&nbsp; her hand and sends her out from his house,&nbsp;&nbsp; and she leaves his house and goes&nbsp; and becomes another man’s wife,&nbsp;&nbsp; and if the latter husband turns against her and&nbsp; writes her a certificate of divorce and puts it in&nbsp;&nbsp; her hand and sends her out of his house, or if the&nbsp; latter husband dies who took her to be his wife,&nbsp;&nbsp; then her former husband who sent her away is not&nbsp; allowed to take her back again to be his wife,&nbsp;&nbsp; since she has been defiled; for that&nbsp; is an abomination before the Lord.”&nbsp;&nbsp; So guys, picture yourself. You're&nbsp; Jewish in the 1st Century. You're&nbsp;&nbsp; Paul. You studied under Gamaliel. You&nbsp; have this scripture memorized. So when&nbsp;&nbsp; you come across all of the Scriptures and&nbsp; the prophets where God says, "I'm divorcing&nbsp;&nbsp; the Northern House of Israel," there's no way for&nbsp; her to come back. It’s not possible. It’s done,&nbsp;&nbsp; out the door, over the shoulder; don’t&nbsp; let the door hit you on the way out.&nbsp;&nbsp; This is why in the 1st Century, the Northern&nbsp; House of Israel, which Josephus says is by the&nbsp;&nbsp; multitudes across the Euphrates&nbsp; River, why you don’t hear about them.&nbsp;&nbsp; Why they're not allowed to be&nbsp; part of Israel. Because the Judah,&nbsp;&nbsp; the Jadeites, or the short formed, the Jews, which&nbsp; comprised of modern-day Israel of the 1st Century,&nbsp;&nbsp; won't let them because they know the Torah.&nbsp; You can't be Israel. You lost your identity.&nbsp;&nbsp; You guys blew it. We blew it. We went to&nbsp; Babylon. We paid our penalty, but we came&nbsp;&nbsp; back. You guys assimilated to the nations, and&nbsp; you never came back, and God divorced you for it.&nbsp;&nbsp; You can't come back. It doesn't matter how&nbsp; bad you want to come back. And by the way,&nbsp;&nbsp; I will let you come back, but you got&nbsp; to convert to the southern kingdom.&nbsp;&nbsp; How many do you think did that?&nbsp;&nbsp; No way. Why? Because it was Hatfields and McCoys.&nbsp; When you had the northern and southern kingdom&nbsp;&nbsp; that were in existence back after Solomon&nbsp; -- they were always fighting one another.&nbsp;&nbsp; No way was an Israelite from the&nbsp; northern kingdom going to convert&nbsp;&nbsp; to Judah. Not going to happen. They had&nbsp; their own tribal identity. Very few did&nbsp;&nbsp; this. More of them were pagan Gentiles that&nbsp; were not Israelites that actually converted&nbsp;&nbsp; to Judaism than ever did an Israelite from the&nbsp; northern kingdom. So, we have a major problem&nbsp;&nbsp; because this becomes the mystery that Paul&nbsp; talks about. There are several mysteries,&nbsp;&nbsp; but the large mystery is how does God solve&nbsp; this? Because He invites them to come back,&nbsp;&nbsp; which seem to be against His own&nbsp; law. So you come to Romans Chapter 7,&nbsp;&nbsp; one of the most incredible chapters of the entire&nbsp; Bible. You could not be saved&nbsp;&nbsp; in 1st Century Judaism unless you were&nbsp; circumcised. So, if&nbsp; you want to compel my converts to get circumcised,&nbsp;&nbsp; then it means nothing because you're telling them&nbsp; to do something for their salvation rather than&nbsp;&nbsp; to have faith. Works comes later. Circumcision is&nbsp; a general term referring to a segment of Judaism.&nbsp;&nbsp; So, when you go back to 1st Corinthians&nbsp; 7:18, this is what it really is saying:&nbsp;&nbsp; Was anyone called while Jewish? Let him&nbsp; not be a Gentile. Was anyone called while&nbsp;&nbsp; Gentile? Let him not become Jewish. (Acts 15:1) This is why Paul says,&nbsp;&nbsp; "I wish they would just emasculate themselves,"&nbsp; in the book of Galatians, because all they talk&nbsp;&nbsp; about is circumcision. - My Nephew Tried To List My Mountain Home As His Vacation Rental, So I Prepared A Lesson He'll Nev...: Kind: captions Language: en I built this house with 31 years of my life. That's not a metaphor. 31 years working as a structural engineer for a firm in Nashville, Tennessee. Early mornings, late nights, weekends spent reviewing load calculations while my colleagues were at their kids' soccer games. I didn't have kids. I had deadlines. I had projects. I had a retirement account I fed every single month without fail, the way some men tend a garden quietly, patiently, trusting that something good would eventually grow. When I finally walked out of that office at 63, I had a handshake from my department head, a card signed by 17 people whose names I knew well, and enough saved to do the one thing I had been planning for longer than most people plan anything. I bought a house in the Blue Ridge Mountains of Western North Carolina. Not a cabin, not a vacation property, a real house, three bedrooms, a wrap-around porch, 12 acres of mixed hardwood forest, and a creek that runs cold and clear along the eastern property line, even in August. I paid $387,000 for it. Cash. No mortgage. No monthly payment to anyone. The deed has my name on it, and only my name. I moved in on a Tuesday in October, and that first evening I sat on the porch with a cup of coffee and watched the fog settle into the valley below. The trees were turning. The air smelled like wood smoke from somewhere distant. There was no traffic, no conference calls, no one needing anything from me. I thought, "This is what I worked for, this exact moment." I should have known it wouldn't stay quiet. My sister's name is Patricia. She is 4 years younger than me, lives in Charlotte, and has spent most of her adult life operating on the assumption that whatever I have is, in some loosely defined way, also available to her. Not out of malice, I don't think. It's just how she's always been. When our mother passed and left me her china cabinet, Patricia was in my driveway within the week to ask if I really needed it. When I got a bonus at work 15 years ago, she called to ask if I might want to invest in her husband's landscaping expansion. I always said no. She always acted surprised. Patricia has a son. His name is Derek, and he is 34 years old. Derek is the kind of young man who has, in the years since college, launched four businesses. None of them survived long enough to require an accountant. There was the food truck, the app that was going to disrupt something or other, the drop-shipping venture, and most recently, something involving cryptocurrency that I never fully understood and wisely never asked to have explained a second time. Derek is charming in the way that some people mistake for competent. He has good teeth and an easy laugh and the ability to make you feel, while he's talking to you, like his idea is actually your idea. I've watched him do it to other people for years. I told myself I was immune. I was not immune. About 4 months after I moved into the North Carolina house, Derek called me. "Just to catch up," he said. How was I settling in? Was the mountain air treating me well? Had I found a good hardware store in town yet, because knowing the right hardware store was essential in a rural area? We talked for 20 minutes about nothing in particular. It was pleasant. I remember thinking it was nice that he'd called. Then he said casually, the way you might mention the weather, "You know, Uncle Gerald, I've been doing a lot of research into short-term rentals." I said that was interesting. He said the mountain region of North Carolina was one of the top vacation rental markets in the entire Southeast. He said properties like mine, private, wooded, with acreage, were commanding serious money on weekends and holidays. He threw out a number. He said a place like mine could generate somewhere around 50, maybe $55,000 a year if managed correctly. I said that was interesting, too, and I meant it. But I wasn't looking to manage a rental. He said I wouldn't have to manage anything. That was the whole point. He would handle everything. Listing, guest communication, cleaning, turnover, all of it. He had connections with a cleaning service. He knew how to price dynamically for peak weekends. All I would need to do, he said, was make myself available to relocate to the smaller guest room during bookings. I want to be precise about what he said, because I've replayed it many times since. He said I would need to make guest room in my own house that I paid for with my own money, that I worked 31 years to earn. I told him no. I told him calmly and without any ambiguity. I said I bought this house to live in it quietly, and that was what I intended to do. I said I wasn't interested in having strangers cycle through my home on weekends. I said I appreciated that he was thinking creatively, but the answer was no. He said he understood completely. He said he respected that. He said we'd talk soon. He called again 3 weeks later. This time he came with numbers. He'd apparently built out a spreadsheet. He told me that, based on comparable listings within a 20-mile radius, my property was conservatively undervalued for its potential. He said the acreage alone was a differentiator. He said the creek was a natural amenity that I was leaving money on the table by not leveraging. I told him the money on the table was fine where it was. He laughed. He said he knew I was joking, because what retired person wouldn't want supplemental income? What if something happened? What if I had a health situation? What if the roof needed replacing and my savings took a hit? He was just trying to help me think ahead. I told him I had thought ahead for 31 years. That was how I bought the house. The answer was still no. He said he understood. We hung up. I told Patricia about the calls the next time she and I talked. I wasn't angry. I just mentioned it the way you mention something you want someone to know. She laughed and said Derek was just enthusiastic. She said he had a good eye for opportunity. She said I shouldn't be so resistant to new ideas. I noted that she did not say she would tell him to stop. He called a third time in February. By this point, it had been about 5 months since I'd moved in. This call was different in tone. He wasn't pitching anymore. He was informing. He said he had done some additional research and had determined that the optimal way to structure the arrangement would be as a partnership. He would handle all operations. I would provide the property. We would split the revenue. He proposed 60% for him, 40 for me, on the basis that his labor and expertise were the primary value drivers. I sat with that for a moment. Then I asked him to repeat it. He repeated it. 60 for him, 40 for me, for the use of my property that I own. I said, "Derek, I've told you no three times now. This is the last time I'm going to say it. Not through you, not through anyone. Please do not bring this up again." He said he was sorry I felt that way. He said he thought I'd come around eventually. He said the offer stood whenever I was ready. I hung up. That should have been the end of it. In March, my neighbor, two parcels over, a retired postal worker named Roy, who walks his beagle past my fence line most mornings, mentioned to me that he'd seen a car parked at my place the previous weekend. He thought I should know in case I hadn't been expecting anyone. He described the car. It was Derek's car. I hadn't been expecting anyone. I had been visiting a college friend in Asheville for the weekend. I had been gone Friday afternoon through Sunday evening. My house had been sitting empty. I called Derek and asked him directly why his car had been at my property. He said he'd just driven up to take some photos, for his own planning purposes. Nothing serious. I asked what photos. He said exterior shots, mostly. The porch, the view from the yard, the creek. I told him that was trespassing. He said I was being dramatic. He said he was family. I said the two things were not mutually exclusive. He laughed again. He has this laugh he uses when he thinks you're being unreasonable. It's not a mean laugh. It's almost worse than that. It's an indulgent laugh, the kind you'd use with a child who's upset about something you've already decided isn't important. I did not laugh back. That weekend I made three phone calls. The first was to a locksmith in town. I had every lock on the property rekeyed and upgraded. The front door got a deadbolt that requires a physical key on both sides. The back door got the same. I ordered a lockbox for the breaker panel on the outside of the house, in case anyone thought cutting the power was a useful option for some reason. I wasn't sure it was necessary. I ordered it anyway. The second call was to a security company. I had cameras installed at every entry point, front door, back door, both sides of the driveway, and one covering the parking area near the barn. The feed goes to my phone. Motion triggers a notification. I can see everything that happens on my property in real time, from anywhere. The third call was to an attorney in Asheville who specializes in property law. I had found him through the county bar association's referral service. I explained the situation. He asked several questions. He told me to document everything going forward, dates, times, content of every conversation. He told me to send Derek a formal written notice that he was not permitted on the property without my explicit prior consent, and that any violation would be treated as trespassing and reported to the sheriff's department. He drafted the letter. I signed it. It went out certified mail. I did not tell Patricia any of this. In April, Derek listed my house on a vacation rental platform. I know the exact moment I found out, because I was sitting at my kitchen table with my second cup of coffee, reading, when my phone lit up with a notification from the security app. Motion in the driveway. I looked at the feed and saw a vehicle I didn't recognize pulling slowly down my gravel driveway. I walked out onto the porch. There were two people in the vehicle, a man and a woman. Both looked to be in their 30s, both looking at their phones. >> [snorts] >> When they saw me standing on the porch, they stopped and the man rolled down his window and said they were here for the check-in. I said, "I beg your pardon?" He said they had a reservation. He held up his phone. He showed me a listing, my house, my property address. Photos I recognized, the porch, the yard, the view of the ridge, the creek. Derek's photos. Three-night booking. $420 per night. Check-in that afternoon. I told the couple very calmly that there had been a serious error and that they had not made a reservation with me because I had never listed this property and that I was very sorry for the inconvenience, but they would not be able to stay here. I told them to contact the platform for a full refund and to tell the platform that the listing was fraudulent. They were confused and upset, which was entirely reasonable. I stood on my porch and watched them back out and leave. Then I called my attorney. He was not surprised. He said this was what he had been concerned about. He said we had good documentation. He said to sit tight and let him work. What happened over the following 6 weeks is something I will try to explain clearly because some of it is complicated, but all of it matters. First, the platform. My attorney contacted them directly with documentation proving I was the sole owner of the property and had never authorized any listing. The listing was taken down within 48 hours. The platform provided records of the account that had created the listing and the bank account receiving payments. Derek had already collected deposits on three future bookings in addition to the couple who'd shown up at my door. Second, the sheriff. I filed a formal trespassing complaint based on Roy's account of Derek's March visit. My own security camera footage from that day, the cameras had captured Derek on my property on two separate occasions I hadn't even known about and the fraudulent listing. The deputy who took my statement was thorough and professional. He said he'd been seeing more of this kind of situation lately. He did not seem surprised, either. Third, the civil matter. My attorney filed for an emergency injunction preventing Derek from representing himself as having any authority over my property in any commercial or personal capacity. This was granted quickly, in fact. And fourth, the Internal Revenue Service, which I mention only because my attorney brought it to my attention that Derek had apparently been collecting deposits and payments on a property he did not own without reporting this income and that this was not simply a civil problem. I did not pursue that last matter myself. Some things have a way of catching up to people on their own schedule. Derek called me after the injunction was served. This was not the charming, easy phone call of someone who thought he might bring me around. His voice was different this time, tight. He said I had overreacted. He said he had been trying to help me. He said I had embarrassed him and caused him real financial damage and that he didn't understand why I had gone to these extremes when we were family. I let him finish. Then I said, "I told you no. I told you clearly multiple times, starting with the first phone call, continuing through the second and the third and the conversation about trespassing. I sent you a certified letter. You signed for it. You listed my house on a commercial platform without my knowledge or consent while I was away. You photographed my property without permission. You collected money from strangers for the use of a house that is not yours. You had every opportunity to stop and you did not. So, I stopped you." He said he thought I would eventually agree. I said that hope was not authorization. He hung up. Patricia called me that evening. She did not sound like someone calling to check on her brother. She sounded like a woman who had just spent an hour on the phone with her son and was now calling me on his behalf. She told me Derek was devastated. She told me that what I had done was excessive. She told me that family was supposed to help each other, not lawyer up and file police reports and that I had humiliated her son over a business idea. I asked her if she had told Derek to stop when I told her about the calls in January. She was quiet for a moment. I said, "I told him no in October. I told him no in November. I told him no in February. I told you in January that he was pushing this and you told me he just had a good eye for opportunity. I sent him a certified letter telling him he was not permitted on my property. He came onto my property anyway. He photographed it anyway. He listed it anyway. He took money for it. I did not make this complicated. He made it complicated and you had a chance to make it stop and you chose not to." She said I could have handled it differently. I said I had handled it eight times before I called an attorney. I asked her how many times she thought I should have said no before it counted. She didn't have an answer for that. We haven't spoken since, which is her choice. I've left the door open. I'm not interested in permanently severing anything over this, but I'm also not going to apologize for protecting my own property. Roy asked me a few weeks later how things had settled down. I gave him the short version. He nodded slowly and said some people only understand a thing once it costs them something. He said it without any satisfaction, just as an observation. I thought it was about the most accurate thing I'd heard said about the whole situation. Derek, from what I understand through a mutual cousin, is now exploring a new venture. I don't know the details. I wish him, genuinely, a version of success that doesn't require the use of anything that belongs to someone else. It is June now. The creek is running high from the spring rains. The rhododendrons along the eastern fence line bloomed earlier this month, huge, heavy clusters of pink that I walked through on three separate mornings just because I could. I have a good relationship with the hardware store in town. I know the guys at the feed store by name even though I don't have livestock because they also carry good fencing supplies and we talk about the weather. I drive into Asheville twice a month for groceries and occasionally meet my friend David for lunch at a place near the river that does a good trout. I repainted the porch railing last weekend. It took all of Saturday and part of Sunday morning. And when I finished, I stood back and looked at it and felt the specific satisfaction of a thing done properly and at my own pace with no one waiting on me, no deliverable, no deadline. I bought this house to live in it. I live in it. The fog settles into the valley most evenings around 6:00. I pour a cup of coffee, decaf now, which I resisted for years and have since accepted as not the tragedy I believed it would be. And I sit in the chair I dragged to the east end of the porch where the creek is audible if the wind is right. Sometimes Roy walks by with his beagle and we wave. Sometimes I go inside and read. Sometimes I just sit there while the light changes and the ridgeline goes from gold to gray to the particular dark blue that mountains turn just before full dark. I worked 31 years for this porch and this light and this silence. Nobody is taking any of it, not a nephew with a spreadsheet and an unreasonable laugh. Not a sister who confused family loyalty with refusing to say a clear word to her son when it would have mattered. Not anyone. I built a fence once, professionally, around something worth protecting. I always said the most important part of any structure wasn't the materials. It was the decision made early to build it right. I still believe that.

We pick up properties from owners because we're 100% third party. We don't own any of the properties we manage. >> Um so we see a lot of owners who want us to deal with tenants that they've had in there for 5 or 10 years that they're scared to raise the rent on. Um an emotional decision is should I leave the rent the same as opposed to having to deal with a month of vacancy? Well, if you leave it the same, is that below market rate? Is the vacancy period going to be worth it? Is that going to give you enough time to do renovations that's going to help with ROI uh and get you that market rate that you want? >> So, I think making emotional decisions is one of the worst things that a landlord can do. Um, placing a tenant that might not meet the qualifications because they have a sad story. We we're by the book. If you meet our criteria, great. If you do not, unfortunately, this isn't for you. >> Yeah. I I've often seen uh property owners have emotional attachments to their home. So, they they lived in this home, they decorated this home, they painted that wall purple, and then, you know, when we're giving them advice, well, you know, we're we're giving tours and everyone's commenting, can we can we paint the purple wall? And then they get so in their feelings about, you know, how much they love their home and they love their decor, but you know, once it becomes an investment, it's not about what you like. - The Airbnb effect: why second homes have become so divisive: Kind: captions Language: en Second homes have long been an aspirational dream for middle-class families. Then in 2008, Airbnb arrived on the market. Through its online marketplace for homestays, the Silicon Valley-based company has had a huge impact on the travel industry, with self-catering accommodations now as popular as hotels. But it has also created what is called the Airbnb effect, when the growing number of short-term rentals in a location in high demand leads to a rise in both rental rates and house prices. And many people aren’t happy about it. We're a sort of young, newly married couple and looking to live around here,&nbsp;who are competing with people who basically buy up these properties to let out. There aren't many properties for sale. It means the local children, as they're growing up, they can't afford to buy houses. So therefore they have to move out&nbsp; of the area. It's killing the area.&nbsp;&nbsp; Second housing, short-term rentals, Airbnb. No. But how much of this crisis in the housing&nbsp;&nbsp; market is due to Airbnb and its competitors?&nbsp; And what’s being done to solve the problem? &nbsp; I totally understand that there is&nbsp; a housing crisis, and that people&nbsp;&nbsp; are struggling to get on the housing ladder. Amanda is one of many people earning an income through Airbnb. She started her holiday vacation rental business in the Cotswolds in 2018. There's a difference between holiday homes and holiday rentals, because holiday rentals drive tourism. Holiday homes or second homes, are quite different, because people only go and stay there occasionally. And the rest of the time, they're empty. So they're not contributing to the community. The properties that I chose, I'm not taking homes away from families, because they're not the kind of properties that families would be able to or want to live in long term. In a 2018 study, researchers said that Airbnb’s presence in a market shrinks supply of long-term rentals, which results in price increases. In the 100 largest U.S. cities, a 10% increase of short term rentals was responsible for 1/7 of the average annual increase in housing prices and about 1/5 of the average annual increase in rents. For a house in the centre of Los Angeles for example, where the median price for a house in 2016 was more than $500,000, nearly $75,000 would be attributed to the growth of short-term rentals. Second homes and short-term rentals started after World War 1, with the development of transport and better social conditions, giving workers the right to holiday in the 1930s. The expansion of the middle-class after World War II and the growth of commercial air travel in the 1960s led to more demand for such options. Homesharing has always existed, but it had a boarding house, sort of Skid Row connotation. Lily Hoffman is the co-author of the book Airbnb, Short-Term Rentals and the Future of Housing. And what Airbnb did was legitimate and glamorise it. Airbnb marketed it as cooler, a greener way to travel, to visitors. The Airbnb model was very interesting, very positive, because it was based on the real sharing philosophy. It was followed quickly by competitors like HomeStay, HomeAway and Vrbo, all having a share of the big short-term rental market. These companies, including Airbnb, did not respond to a request for comment. Barring the pandemic, Airbnb has seen consistant growth since 2008, generating $8.4 billion in revenue in 2022. The company reached 150 million users in 2019, with an average of 6.6 million listings worldwide. Airbnb would post its first annual profit in 2022. In the UK, hosts make on average more than $22,000 per year; in Australia, the figure rises $28,000 and in the U.S., hosts make on average more than $44,000 per year. In England, the number of privately rented homes has more than doubled between 2000 and 2022 to 4.6 million. I had a property — a very small buy-to-let in London. I'd bought it in my 20s. I didn't have a pension, I had young children. I decided to use the equity from the business, and very quickly grew that over the last five years to five holiday cottages. The first one was in Bourton-on-the-Water, in the Cotswolds. I'd always loved it, I'd gone there as a youngster. Four out of five of my properties, I've had to&nbsp;&nbsp; refurbish, and I used local builders. And then we have local housekeepers. A lot of cafes, and restaurants and pubs, they wouldn't be able to exist, if there wasn't tourism coming into that area. Turning second-homes into a fully-fledged business is becoming increasingly popular. A 2015 study looking at Airbnb's effect on hotel sales growth in many U.S. cities showed that multi-unit operators total nearly 40% of Airbnb's revenue. In the Italian city of Florence, property investors have hoarded apartments, to such an extent that a thousand rental properties are run by just 20 landlords. The city recorded 15 million overnight stays from tourists before the pandemic in 2019, or 20 times its population. In the big majority of apartments on rentals, it is practically a business. Dario Nardella is the mayor of Florence. We have more than 7,000 apartments involved in&nbsp; this kind of system of short-term tourist rentals.&nbsp;&nbsp; And now it's very difficult for a young guy or a&nbsp; new family to find an affordable house to rent. They are the fastest growing segment of the&nbsp;market. They need an adequate supply of housing at&nbsp;&nbsp; all times and to get more supply, if, for example,&nbsp; I'm a corporation and I own 20 apartments,&nbsp;&nbsp; and I make a deal with Airbnb, I can&nbsp; property manage those 20 apartments&nbsp;&nbsp; and put them into the Airbnb market. That's 20 easy ones rather than just finding one host. A lot of people have been penalised tax-wise on long-term rentals and so a lot of people are thinking 'Oh, it's fine, I'll just go into short-term rental.' But the problem with that is it leads to an oversupply. For the past 10 years, from Lisbon to Santa Monica, local governments have been trying to balance the benefits of tourism with a worsening housing crisis. New York City has been in a long battle with Airbnb. Hosts, who need to be registered&nbsp; with the city, can only accept two people&nbsp;&nbsp; at a time, and the latest restrictions&nbsp; announced in 2023 will force owners to&nbsp;&nbsp; prove they reside in the rented properties. In Paris, second homes need to first be registered before renting, then the city asks for compensation, where owners have to buy a commercial building of the same size before renovating it to be used for housing. And in Singapore, hosts are prevented from renting out all public housing, which is home to more than 80% of locals. Private properties, however, can be let out for a minimum of three consecutive months. We are introducing an urban rule which forbids in the UNESCO area, any kind of short tourist rentals. And a zero taxes for the house for three years if you move from a short-tourist rental contract to a resident contract. Enforcing the rules is difficult, costly, and frustrating because the cities do not have the exact data they need. But short-term rentals and Airbnb aren't solely responsible for the hike in house prices. Other factors include boom-bust cycles in the housing market, such as the subprime mortgage crisis of 2008, and the impact of climate change on properties. Together, these factors are predicted to impact 1.6 billion people by 2025, exacerbated by rural-to-urban migration, and shortages in labour and materials. As a result, housing that is available is being priced out of many people’s budgets. Between 2010 and 2022, property prices have increased considerably in most countries, espeically in emerging markets such as Turkey and India. The housing problem has been made worse by wealth inequality. In recent years, average house prices have risen much faster than average incomes, making housing less affordable. In New York City, a buyer will need 10 times the average yearly salary to afford the cost of the average house, in Paris almost 20 times and in Shanghai more than 50 times. These factors have led some to question the unintended consequences of second homes. Commercial hosts and investors are a direct response to incentivizing housing as an asset. There’s no way to say that Airbnb is the cause of the housing crisis, but we're talking about a contribution. And the rent gap between a short-term rental and long-term rental incentivizes the shift. But would banning Airbnb and short-term rentals be the solution? In Switzerland, for example, the government banned the construction of second homes, following a referendum in 2012. It depressed the house prices of new build properties but increased the house prices of the current stock, inadvertently benefitting existing second-home owners who saw the value of their properties go up. One of the solution for example is to invest more energy and money on affordable and public social housing. I feel that it's a government issue, that they have missed the house building targets, year after year after year. And that's a really huge issue. And something that they need to rectify is to build more homes. And if they're not doing that, then it's a really easy way for them to blame somebody else by saying, Oh, it's just because there's loads of short-term rentals around. As we've discussed, it has plenty of problems for renters for Airbnb owners, for the company itself, for cities. How do you see it evolving? If we can get our hands on enough data, and enough ways to control the corporatization of these rentals, I think we'll have done a good part of the job.

  12. @ktm9292: As someone who travels a bit for wildlife, particularly ocean wildlife since I scuba dive, there are some things to look for when trying to determine sustainability. 
1. Certifications obviously.
2. For any tours where you go in the water, the sustainable tours often mention sunscreen. Whilst the impact of sunscreen on coral reefs has been largely exaggerated- the concentrations required for the damage observed in lab studies is unrealistic and increased ocean temps and pollution runoff from nearby factories and mining are much bigger issues - a lot of the more sustainable tours will mention it, and I think there are some cases where they prohibit sunscreen because of it being an irritant to the animals. 
3. They might mention using a boat without a propeller.
4. A main page on their website dedicated to sustainability or at minimum a section on their about page. 
5. Mention that the tour includes educational content and mention of conservation education. Potentially partnerships with researchers. 

Red flags:
1. Guaranteed sightings. It is fine if they can say there's a 90% chance of a sightings or similar but if they have a money back guarantee, especially for sharks, this may indicate that they provide food or chum the water. This is not good as it may lead animals to associate boats with food --> increased shark attacks, increased risk of getting killed by commercial fishing vessels and shipping trawlers etc, or alter migration patterns.
2. The complete absence of the considerations above. 
3. If they encourage touching/ holding wild animals.

- The Elusive Impact of Pro Environmental Intention on Holiday On Pro Environmental Behaviour At Home: Kind: captions Language: en the elusive impact of pro-environmental intention on holiday on pro-environmental behavior at home as human activities are the root of many environmental concerns it has become crucial to encourage individuals to adopt a more environmentally responsible behavior with awareness being a key to shaping behavior studies on how one pro-environmental behavior pebs will influence adopting similar behaviors have researched pebs within the holiday context on site this study explores the impact of holiday experiences on an individual's peb at home off-site using the attitude behavior context theory longitudinal methods were utilized to investigate these commitments to environmental conservation on site and those following their visit the time lagged studies found that behaviours on holiday have limited impact on behaviors at home highlighting the importance of understanding the impact context has on individual actions this paper found it is economically beneficial for stakeholders to justify travel by balancing the severe environmental impacts of a short holiday with long-term positive behavior changes instilled in tourists when they return home learn more please follow this link for more information

This thing must have come to your mind. Will it work out or not? After all, how do they manage both of them ? What will be its consequence or future going forward? Today through this video we will understand what is the psychology behind it? Why do people feel a little left out in a long distance relationship ? What is it that they are craving for? What is it that they want out of a long distance relationship. Come, first of all today we will understand in this video what is a long distance relationship? A long-distance relationship is a relationship where two people are romantically involved but physically distant, yet emotionally very strong. According to researches in this era of digital world there are 14 million people who are in a long distance relationship and it is kind of working out for them. Sometimes, anticipation and longing increase a lot due to being in long distance relationships and because of this our relationship becomes more passionate and strong. We call this idealization bias. This means that we consider our partner so perfect and think that our and his/her life is so beautiful that we actually ignore the real fault. Not just ignoring them, we actually stop seeing them. When we don't see them, we don't consider them a flaw and we think that our relationship is absolutely perfect. Psychology says that out of sight is not out of mind. This means that if the emotional bond between you is very strong, you are very emotionally connected, then even if you are at a distance, the bond between you will remain very secure. But if there are trust issues, problems, compatibility issues between you. In that case, the bond between you will be weak and the long distance will make it even weaker. Psychology does n't just show the dreamy side of long-distance relationships. Psychology also suggests that long- distance relationships can cause a lot of emotional fatigue. Sometimes, due to time zone differences , lack of trust, communication issues, there is extreme emotional exhaustion. In a long distance relationship, you live in time zone differences. Sometimes one person is busy, sometimes the other person is busy. Because of this, taking out common time to talk, checking in on each other's day, putting in so much effort causes a lot of emotional fatigue and gradually a relationship reaches a situation of burnout. Research suggests that people with an anxious attachment style are more likely to be in long-distance relationships. And because they do not get proper validation in this. Their feelings cannot be validated. They continue to have trust issues towards others. One starts getting anxious over small things. Because of this they feel more emotionally fit. I feel exhausted. Now the question arises do these relationships work out or not? Then yes these relationships work out? But with some conditions? For example, in a relationship, the communication between you and your partner should be very strong. There should be open communication. You guys should share everything with each other. Whenever your partner is unavailable. You should have enough trust that your partner is actually busy or occupied somewhere. That's why I can't talk to you. Secondly, long distance relationships work out when your future goals are the same. Meaning, 10 years from now, 20 years from now or 30 years from now, both of you see yourselves on the same platform. You both want to grow together and get to that place. The third point to working out a long distance relationship is emotional regulation. When we understand our emotions , channelize them properly , do not blame our partner and rather accept our insecurities and communicate them properly to our partner. That is when a relationship workout happens. Research also suggests that long-distance relationships are more physically and emotionally connected when they find a future together. Generally, in couples therapy or counselling, I meet a lot of clients who have been in long distance relationships for 10-10 years and are now happily married. But at the same time, we also meet people who have been in long distance relationships for many years. But now when they are standing together or they are married. That is when they want to part ways because they feel that that level of connection is no longer there between them. And they were better together from a distance. Long Distance Relationship Is Never About Love. Love always exists in a relationship. It is more about testing your patience, your commitment, your loyalty towards one person. How emotionally available you can remain to another person even while being at a distance. And apart from that you cannot imagine any other person at that level. - How to Make a Long Distance Relationship Work (Psychological Truth Most Couples Ignore): Kind: captions Language: en Long-distance relationships don't fail because of miles. [music] They struggle because of unspoken fears, unmet needs, and nervous systems that don't feel safe in absence. When someone you love is far away, your body doesn't measure love in promises. It measures it in presence. And when presence is inconsistent [music] or uncertain, old attachment wounds can awaken. The anxiety, the overthinking, the sudden insecurity, these aren't signs that you're weak. They are signs that your nervous system is trying to protect you. The real question is not can love survive distance. The real question is can two people create emotional safety without physical proximity because love isn't sustained by geography. It's sustained by attunement. Distance has a way of amplifying everything. It magnifies silence. It magnifies misunderstandings. It magnifies insecurities you didn't even know were there. When you can't reach for someone's hand, your mind reaches for certainty. [music] And if certainty isn't there, your nervous system begins scanning for danger. A delayed reply feels like rejection. A shorter call feels like withdrawal. The body remembers every past moment where connection felt unstable, and it reacts as if it's happening again. Long distance relationships don't just test love. They test regulation [music] to make distance work. You must understand that your reactions are not flaws. They are adaptations. If you become anxious, it may be because closeness once felt unpredictable in your life. If your partner withdraws, it may be because vulnerability once felt unsafe for them. Distance exposes attachment patterns. [music] And that exposure is not a curse. It's an invitation. The key is this. Physical absence cannot become emotional absence. [music] If distance turns into emotional unavailability, resentment will grow. But if distance is met with intentional presence with reassurance, clarity [music] and emotional transparency, then something deeper can form trust that is not dependent [music] on proximity. Long-d distanceance love demands maturity. It asks, can you express needs without accusation? Can you offer reassurance without feeling controlled? Can you regulate your anxiety instead of making your partner responsible for it? Love across distance is not [music] sustained by grand gestures. It is sustained by small consistent acts of emotional reliability. Consistency is what calms the nervous system. Clarity is what prevents projection. Emotional honesty is what [music] keeps intimacy alive. And perhaps most importantly, both people must choose the relationship actively. Long [music] distance cannot survive on one person carrying the emotional weight. Mutual effort is not optional. It is oxygen. [music] Distance when handled consciously can can deepen love. It forces communication. It reveals intentions. It strips away physical distraction and asks, "Are we connected at the level of truth?" If the answer is yes, [music] distance becomes a bridge, not a barrier. Let's talk about some facts that how to make a long distance relationship work. First thing is prioritize emotional presence over constant contact. When people are separated by distance, [music] the instinct is often to increase contact. More messages, more calls, more updates. It feels logical. If we cannot be physically close, we will compensate with constant communication. [music] But the nervous system does not measure connection by quantity. It measures it by quality. You can exchange hundreds of messages and [music] still feel alone. You can talk for hours and never truly feel seen. Because emotional presence is not about being available every minute. It is about being attuned in the moments that matter. When someone checks in with you while [music] distracted, multitasking, half listening, your body registers that absence. It may not be conscious, [music] but it is felt. The tone, the pauses, the emotional flatness, these signals [music] communicate more than words. In long distance relationships, this kind of fragmented contact [music] can quietly increase insecurity rather than soothe it. What truly regulates the nervous system is felt [music] understanding, a meaningful check, and sounds different. It is slower. It has space. It includes curiosity. Instead of, "How was your day?" followed by quick replies, it becomes, "What felt heavy today?" or "Where did you feel [music] most yourself?" It allows vulnerability to emerge instead of just logistics. Emotional presence requires attention. And attention is an act of care. When you speak with someone [music] who is genuinely attuned, you feel it in your body. Your breathing softens, your shoulders drop, [music] you feel less alone. Not because you spoke for a long time, but because you were received. Many people confuse constant [music] texting with intimacy. But often constant contact is anxiety trying to self soothe. It is the part of us that fears disconnection and [music] tries to prevent it through control, checking, monitoring, keeping the line open. This is not wrong. It is protective. It may have roots in earlier experiences where connection felt unstable. But what heals insecurity is not surveillance. It is safety. Safety is built when both partners show up emotionally regulated, willing to [music] listen, willing to share honestly. Even a short conversation can be deeply [music] nourishing if it includes authenticity. 10 grounded minutes of true presence can calm more than hours of scattered exchange. In distance, [music] this becomes even more important. Because you do not have physical cues, a touch, a glance, shared silence. [music] You must intentionally create emotional depth. You must ask yourself, am I speaking to fill space or am [music] I speaking to connect? Prioritizing emotional presence also means tolerating [music] space. Healthy love allows breathing room. If you require constant updates to feel secure, it may be an invitation to explore [music] what absence represents inside you. Is it abandonment? Is it loss? Is it unpredictability? When both partners understand their own attachment patterns, they can approach communication consciously instead of reactively. They can say, "When I don't hear from you, I notice I feel anxious instead of accusing or demanding." That honesty creates closeness far more effectively [music] than constant contact ever could. In long-distance relationships, depth replaces proximity. It is not about how often you speak. It is about how fully you show up when [music] you do. Second thing is that establish predictable communication rhythms. When two people are separated by distance, uncertainty can quietly become the third presence in the relationship. You may not speak about it directly, but the body feels it. When will they call? Why haven't they replied? Are they pulling away? The mind begins to create stories in the absence of information. And those stories are rarely generous. They are protective. The nervous system is wired for safety. And one of the primary ingredients of safety is predictability. As [music] children, we learned to relax, not simply because someone loved us, but because we could rely on their presence. We knew when they would return, how they would respond, what to expect, expect. Consistency builds trust at a biological level. In a long distance relationship, that predictability must be intentional. It does not happen automatically. Without physical proximity, you lose many regulating cues. The familiar rhythm of someone entering a room, the casual good morning, the shared dinner. In their absence, irregular communication can feel like emotional instability. Even when no harm is intended, when communication is random or inconsistent, the body interprets it as potential threat. A missed call may trigger a disproportionate reaction. A delayed text can awaken old attachment wounds. It is not immaturity, it is memory. The body remembers times when closeness was unpredictable and it reacts accordingly. Creating predictable communication rhythms is not about control. It is about co- regulation. When two people agree, for [music] example, that they will speak each evening or have a long call every Sunday or send a voice note before bed, they are offering one another nervous system reassurance. [music] They are saying, "You do not have to guess where I stand. You do not have to chase connection. It will be here." This reduces anxiety dramatically. The mind no longer needs to scan for danger. The body does not have to brace for loss. There is a rhythm and rhythm is soothing. Predictability also fosters emotional maturity. It requires reliability. It asks both partners to take responsibility for showing up when they say they will. If plans change, clarity becomes essential. A simple message that says, "I won't be able to call at our usual time, but I will call tomorrow morning." prevents unnecessary spirals. [music] It replaces ambiguity with reassurance. Many conflicts in long distance relationships are not about love. They are about uncertainty. When connection feels erratic, insecurity grows. When communication has rhythm, trust grows. Of course, life cannot be perfectly structured. There will be disruptions, busy days, unexpected events. The goal is not rigidity. It is reliability. Even when [music] schedules shift, the underlying message remains consistent. You matter. I am here. Our connection is stable. Over time, this consistency [music] becomes internalized. You begin to carry the sense of the other person's presence within you. You do not panic when a few hours pass [music] without contact because your nervous system has learned that the bond is dependable. [music] Distance challenges attachment patterns, but it can also heal them. Predictable communication becomes more than logistics. It becomes a corrective emotional experience. It teaches the body that closeness does not have to be chaotic. That love can be steady. That connection can have a rhythm you can rest into. And when you can rest in the rhythm of someone's reliability, distance loses much of its power [music] to destabilize you. Another important factor is reassure without being asked. Distance doesn't just separate bodies. It separates signals. When two people are close, reassurance happens in a 100 quiet ways. A glance across the room, a hand on your back while you're speaking. The casual I'm here [music] that's communicated without words. In long distance, those regulating cues disappear and what remains is interpretation. And when the nervous system is left to interpret in uncertainty, [music] it often defaults to protection. That's why distance amplifies doubt. Not because love is weaker, but because the mind fills in what the heart can't confirm. [music] A delayed reply becomes a question mark. A shorter call becomes a threat. A busy day becomes a story. Maybe I'm not [music] important. Maybe they're losing interest. Maybe I'm being replaced. Most people don't realize this doubt is rarely about the present moment. It's usually about an older memory being reactivated. If someone had to fight for attention in childhood, if affection felt inconsistent, if closeness was followed by withdrawal, then distance becomes a perfect stage for those [music] old patterns to replay. The adult may understand logically that their partner is simply busy, but the body doesn't respond to logic. The body responds to emotional [music] history. Reassurance then is not a performance. It is a form of nervous system care. And the [music] most powerful reassurance is the kind that arrives before someone has to beg for it. Because when reassurance is only given after anxiety erupts, it can start to feel transactional like comfort must be earned through distress. The person learns [music] unconsciously I only get closeness when I'm upset. That pattern deepens insecurity rather than healing it. Reassuring without being asked sends a different message. [music] You don't have to chase me. You don't have to prove you deserve my attention. I'm thinking of you cuz you matter, not because you're panicking. This is what emotional reliability looks like. It doesn't require long speeches. [music] It requires consistency and warmth. A simple I miss you today. A voice note that says I'm proud of you. A message that names [music] commitment plainly, I'm in this with you. These aren't dramatic lines. They are stabilizing signals. Cuz in long distance love, silence is loud. The absence [music] of communication gets interpreted as the absence of care. Especially for someone with an anxious attachment system. And for someone with [music] an avoidant pattern, the instinct may be to stay quiet because vulnerability feels risky. But the avoidance that protects one partner often injures [music] the other. So reassurance becomes an act of courage. It asks you to show your feelings without being forced. To say the tender thing before fear makes it complicated to offer warmth not as a reaction but as a steady presence. And there's another layer here. Reassurance [music] isn't only for the anxious partner. Even the person who seems fine, maybe holding down quietly, not wanting to appear needy. Distance can make even secure people question themselves at [music] times. Reassurance creates a climate where needs don't become shameful. But reassurance must be sincere, not overdone, not manipulative, not empty. [music] If reassurance is used to avoid real conversations, if it becomes a bandage over unresolved issues, it won't land. The nervous system can detect when words are being used to pacify rather than connect. True reassurance is rooted in truth. It matches behavior. It matches effort. It matches consistency and it also includes transparency. Sometimes reassurance is as as simple as explaining your [music] absence. I'm going into a long meeting, but I'll call you tonight. I'm exhausted and quiet today. It's not about us. This kind of clarity protects the relationship from unnecessary storytelling. Distance will always create space. The question is what you put into that space. If you fill it with silence, the mind fills it with fear. If you fill it with steady signals of care, the relationship becomes emotionally close, even when physically far. Reassurance without being asked is not about dependency. It's about devotion expressed in a language the nervous system understands. Predictability, warmth, [music] and presence. And when love learns to speak that language, doubt has less room to grow. Another important this is address conflict quickly and directly. Distance has a way of intensifying what is left unspoken. When two people are physically together, small ruptures are often softened by proximity. A facial expression, a gentle touch, shared laughter. These can repair minor misunderstandings before they harden. But in long distance relationships, you do not have those regulating cues. What remains are words, tone, [music] and interpretation. And when conflict is avoided, the silence does not neutralize the tension. [music] It amplifies it. Unresolved tension appear. It settles into the body. You may tell yourself it's nothing. You may decide it's not worth bringing up. You may avoid it because you fear creating distance, but internally something shifts. Your tone becomes slightly colder. Your enthusiasm becomes restrained. You begin protecting yourself subtly. This is how resentment [music] begins. Not through explosive arguments, but through accumulated unadressed moments. [music] The nervous system is deeply sensitive to disconnection. When conflict occurs and is not repaired, the body registers [music] it as relational instability. Especially in long distance dynamics where connection already requires intentional effort, unresolved [music] issues can create a quiet but persistent anxiety. Are we okay? Is something wrong? Silence then becomes [music] heavy and the longer it lingers, the more stories the mind creates. Many [music] people avoid addressing conflict because they associate disagreement with rejection. Perhaps earlier in life, expressing discomfort led to withdrawal, punishment, or shame. So now, even as adults, conflict feels dangerous. It feels like risking the bond. But avoiding conflict does not preserve connection. It erodess it slowly. Addressing conflict quickly and directly does not mean reacting impulsively. It means acknowledging what is happening before it calcifies. [music] It means saying gently and honestly, something didn't uh sit well with me earlier. [music] Instead of letting the discomfort grow into quiet resentment, direct communication requires emotional regulation. If you approach conflict from [music] a triggered state, the conversation will likely escalate. But if you can pause, reflect, and then speak [music] from your experience rather than accusation, repair becomes possible. Instead of you don't care, it becomes when you canceled our call last minute, I felt unimportant. Can we talk about that? This kind of vulnerability invites connection rather than defensiveness. [music] In long-distance relationships especially, repair is more important than perfection. There will be misunderstandings. There will be missed cues. What determines the strength of the bond is not the absence of conflict. It is the willingness to return to one another [music] after rupture. When conflict is addressed promptly, the nervous system learns [music] something profound. Disagreement does not mean abandonment. This is deeply healing for people who grew up in environments where [music] conflict led to emotional disconnection. Repair restores safety. It allows both partners to recalibrate. It replaces assumption with clarity. It prevents small injuries from becoming relational narratives. Because once a narrative forms, they don't [music] prioritize me. They always withdraw. I can't express myself. It becomes harder to undo. Distance [music] demands intentional repair. If something feels off, name it before it turns into distance. [music] If you feel hurt, express it before it transforms into indifference. If there has been tension, return to it with openness instead of pride. Quick repair communicates commitment. It says, "This relationship matters more than my ego. Our connection is worth the discomfort of honesty." Silence may feel safer in the moment, but in the long run, silence builds walls. Direct, compassionate communication builds bridges [music] even across miles. And in last, I would like to remind you that have a shared vision of the future. [music] Distance can be endured for a season. It can even deepen appreciation, but distance without direction slowly erodess emotional security. The human [music] nervous system does not relax into indefinite ambiguity. It needs orientation. It needs to know where is this going? What are we moving toward? Without that sense of direction, [music] even strong love begins to feel fragile. When two people are separated geographically, the present moment often feels incomplete. [music] There are missed experiences, missed milestones, missed daily rhythms. The bond is sustained not only by current connection, but by the promise of eventual reunion. If that promise is vague or unspoken, uncertainty fills the [music] gap, and uncertainty over time becomes exhausting. Love cannot live indefinitely in uncertainty because uncertainty activates vigilance. The mind starts [music] scanning. Are we serious? Are we temporary? Am I investing in something that has no destination? Even if these questions are not voiced, they influence [music] behavior. One partner may hesitate to fully open. The other may feel anxious and push for clarity. Tension emerges not because of lack [music] of affection, but because of lack of shared vision. A shared vision of the future does not require uh rigid timelines or perfect plans. Life is complex. Circumstances shift. But what it does require is alignment and intention. It requires both partners to say clearly and sincerely, "I see a future with you." It requires conversations about closing the distance, not as [music] fantasy, but as possibility. It requires exploring practical steps, even if they are long-term. Without these discussions, the relationship can begin to feel suspended, [music] alive, but not grounded. When a relationship has no defined direction, the nervous system cannot fully [music] settle. Commitment feels partial. Effort feels risky. One may unconsciously hold back to protect against potential [music] loss. But when there is a shared vision, something changes internally. The sacrifices feel purposeful and the waiting feels meaningful. Even difficult days are held within a larger narrative. We are building towards something. This sense of shared direction [music] strengthens resilience. It transforms distance from a barrier into a transitional phase. It becomes something you are navigating together rather than something that is happening to you. Having a shared vision also requires honesty about compatibility. Are both of you willing to relocate, adjust careers, or shift circumstances if needed? Are your long-term values aligned? Love alone cannot sustain a relationship if your visions of life diverge fundamentally. Distance forces these questions to surface sooner, which can actually be a gift. Avoiding future conversations may feel easier in the short term. It protects [music] against disappointment. But avoidance creates silent anxiety. Clarity, even if uncomfortable, creates stability. When two people consciously choose one another, not just emotionally, but practically, love matures. It becomes intentional rather than romanticized. A shared vision is not about fantasy. It is about commitment expressed through direction. Without direction, distance feels endless. With direction, [music] distance becomes temporary. And when both partners can look ahead and say, "We are moving toward each other. Love finds the grounding it needs to endure." A long-distance relationship [music] works not because two people endure separation, but because they choose emotional closeness despite it. Distance will surface your wounds. It will challenge your patience. It will ask you to grow. But when two people meet that challenge with awareness instead of blame, with consistency instead of [music] chaos, and with courage instead of fear, love doesn't shrink. It matures. And sometimes love that survives distance becomes [music] the most grounded love of all because it was built not on convenience, but on conscious [music] choice.

  19. @kimsnader8777: I've moved multiple times over the years; from larger to smaller spaces and bedroom count just as frequently as the reverse.  I invest more in a versatile small piece with refinish/reupholster potential than a large "filler" for this reason. I often shop in antique malls and better thrift stores for this reason.  Pieces that have traveled with me thru multiple homes and layouts: 
* Upholstered storage bench (similar to a piano bench) which can double as a coffee table in a small or narrow room or a .
* solid wood drop-leaf tables (whether dining/desk height or endtable/nightstand height). One even have a small drawer!
* a pair of solid wood children's lowboy dressers (refinished and legs replaced with decorative ball casters)
* nesting tables, stools and plant stands (stack in smaller spaces, spread out to fill a larger home/more rooms without new purchases)
* solid wood folding/stacking bookcases (occupy lass space on a moving truck than stacking cube storage units, and offer the flexibility to go vertical or horizontal based on the wall you're trying to fill)
* stackable, sturdy decorative wood or woven storage boxes (handy when stacked as end table substitutes but can be displayed as decor in a larger space).
* folding luggage stool with butlers tray

- 10 Small Space Mistakes Making Your Room Feel Tiny: Kind: captions Language: en Your room might look smaller than it really is, not because of square footage, but because of simple design mistakes that shrink your space without you even realizing it. And trust me, I know the pain. I've lived in and designed plenty of tiny apartments where every choice counted. So today, I'm breaking down the top mistakes to avoid and the simple swaps that make any room feel instantly bigger. If your room feels a little cramped, but you can't figure out why, the problem might not be your floor space. It is how you're using your walls. Most people decorate only at eye level and below, filling the room with short and wide furniture and leaving the top half of the walls completely empty. It is not intentional, but it visually compresses the space and makes the ceilings feel lower than they really are. The fix is to use the full height of your walls. So let's say you need some storage. Instead of a bulky credenza or sideboard, opt for a tall shelving unit or cabinet that reaches higher up the wall. And don't stop at furniture. You can decorate vertically, too. Hang a mirror or artwork above a console or sideboard. Layer it with a tall table lamp or vase, or add wall sconces or a pendant instead of cluttering surfaces with bulky lamps. These shifts pull the eye upward and stretch the room visually. Lighting is another easy win. Tall floor lamps, pendants, or sconces mounted on the wall help brighten dark corners and keep your eye moving around the space. And if got a sad, empty corner, a tall plant will do wonders for lifting the room. Finally, windows are your biggest cheat code. They're the main source of natural light, but heavy curtains or rods mounted just above the frame will weigh everything down. They block light, shorten the wall visually, and box in the room. Instead, go high and wide. Mount the rod close to the ceiling and extend it past the window, so the curtains frame the glass rather than covering it. And if curtains aren't your thing, roller blinds are great for small spaces as they let you control light without adding bulk. I have a whole video on Bigger Sky to choosing window treatments if you want to explore the various options available. Now, once the height and light is sorted, the next silent space shrinker is color, especially when there's too many competing shades in one small room. Most people think the biggest mistake in a small space is going with a dark color scheme. But the truth is, dark colors aren't necessarily bad. In fact, they can create that lovely cocooning effect and a cozy warm vibe that so many people want. The real mistake is having too many colors fighting for attention. That is what chops up your room visually and makes it feel a bit chaotic, especially if your walls, trims, doors, and ceilings are all painted in different shades. Your eye stops at every edge and the whole space feels jarring. The same thing happens with flooring. If every room has a different material, floorboards in one, random tiles in another, carpet in the next, it can feel choppy and disconnected. It can work well in a really big home, but it's counterproductive in smaller homes. The fix is simple. You want to create a seamless, cohesive look. The easiest way to do this is to paint everything in the same colors, walls, woodwork, skirtings, everything. When all the edges blur together, the walls seem to recede and the space feels calmer and more expansive. Similarly with floors, using the same materials throughout makes it flow better. And if that material doesn't have a busy pattern or heavy grain, it feels even cleaner and more continuous. To keep my workflow just as clean and continuous, I've been using Plot Note, a pocket-sized AI note taker, who's the sponsor of today's video. It's thin and light enough to live in my wallet, as thin as your typical dollar coin, and it conveniently snaps to the back of my phone using the provided magnetic holder. A quick 2-second press of a button starts recording, so I no longer need to open my phone and hunt for an app, and my phone stays free for photos, texts, or quick research. There's also a simple switch between two modes, call for phone calls and note for in-person meetings. The microphone is noticeably clearer than my phone, so it still picks up softer voices even in multi-person meetings, which makes the notes way more usable, especially in person. When I finish a call, the notes are already done. A clean transcript with speaker labels, an AI summary, and even a mind map of the key points. You can also use Ask Plot to search anything across your audio files to surface key points or forgotten moments. If I need custom automation, I can build one directly in the app and even integrate with other apps out there. It's like having a personal assistant by your side, taking notes of your meetings. The battery lasts up to 30 hours of continuous recording and around 60 days on standby, so I'm not thinking about charging between meetings or events. If you want the same seamless workflow from notes to action, check out Plot Note via the link in the description below. Once you've sorted your colors, the next culprit is furniture, especially when it's bulky, oversized, and swallows half the floor. Another classic mistake I see time and time again is furniture that is simply the wrong scale for the space. Usually, it's oversized sofas or clunky dressers that sit directly on the floor. Not only are these pieces themselves often too big, but they also hide one of your greatest assets, the floor. And the less floor you can see, the smaller your room will feel. There's a design concept called visual weight, which demonstrates this perfectly. See this dresser that sits flat on the ground? Now compare it to this one with legs. Which one feels visually lighter to you? The one with legs feels lighter, right? The same with these two black sofas. One feels significantly lighter than the other due to its legs and slimmer profile. So if you want your space to feel larger, choose low-profile furniture with visible legs. When you can see the floor underneath, it instantly creates a sense of openness. And the more streamlined design, the more light and air can flow around it, which exaggerates that feeling of space. Mid-century modern pieces are especially good for this, since most of them have tapered legs and clean lines. You can also get creative with wall-mounted furniture. Floating nightstands, for example, free up floor space and make the whole room feel lighter compared to bulky pieces that sit directly on the ground. Of course, when you don't have much square footage to play with, it is not just about picking lighter furniture. It's about making every piece work harder. That is where multifunctionality comes in. When you're in a small home, you've got limited square footage, but you still need all the same pieces of furniture, a sofa, storage, bed, table. It adds up fast. And if you approach it like a big space, buying one piece for every single function, you'll end up with a space that feels cramped. The smarter approach is to make every piece pull double duty. Pretty much, if you can get something multifunctional, consider it. Ottomans with hidden storage, extendable dining tables, bookcases that fold out into desk, modular sofas, all of these give you flexibility without stealing more floor space. Most multifunctional furniture also comes with bonus storage, which is priceless when you're short on space. Because let's be honest, the problem usually isn't that we own too little, it's that we own way too much. If you've watched my videos before, you'll know this mistake always makes the list, because it's the biggest one I see all the time. Most people get excited about colors and furniture, but barely think about lighting until the end. That is why so many homes end up relying only on ceiling lights, whether it's a single bulb or a whole row of downlights, and the result is always the same, flat, harsh, and uninspiring. The problem is ceiling lights only push light downward. That leaves the corners in shadow and creates harsh contrast, which makes the space feel smaller, darker, and a lot less inviting. The fix is to always layer your lighting. Think of it in three parts, ambient, task, accent. Ambient sets the overall mood with a soft glow. Task lighting is functional, like a lamp on your desk or pendants over a dining table. Accent lighting highlights features or adds atmosphere, like a wall sconce or an LED strip under a shelf. When you spread your light sources throughout the room, corners get softly illuminated, the harsh shadows disappear, and your eye is led around the whole space. That is what makes a room feel open, warm, and inviting instead of flat and dingy. And while lighting can change how you see your space, mirrors can actually double what you see, which makes them one of the best tools for opening up a Another mistake I often see is treating mirrors as nothing more than a place to check your outfit before heading out the door. In reality, they're one of the most powerful tools for making a small room feel bigger, brighter, and more open. Mirrors reflect both light and views, which tricks the eye into thinking there's more depth than there really is. Place one opposite a window, and you don't just bounce more daylight into the room, you also double the view outside, which instantly makes the space feel larger. But it's not just about placement. The shape and scale of your mirror matter, too. A tall, slender mirror can draw the eye upward, while a big, wide mirror can stretch a room visually. So stop thinking of mirror as purely functional. Use them strategically to reflect light, to extend views, or to exaggerate height and width. Done well, even the smallest, most awkward rooms can feel airy and spacious. But even with mirrors working their magic, nothing will save a space that is drowning in visual clutter. If every inch of your room is cramped with stuff, it is going to feel small. When you're not intentional about decor and storage, it's easy for surfaces to get buried under knickknacks. The easiest way clutter sneaks in is through open storage. Open shelving looks great in photos, but in real life, it quickly turns into a dumping ground for mismatched mugs, random books, and knickknacks. That constant visual noise makes a space feel smaller. Closed storage is a much better option in tight rooms. A cabinet with doors instantly look calmer and hides the mess. And when it comes to decor, less on display almost always feels better. Instead of spreading little objects across every surface, group few favorites together and put the rest away. And if you're not sure where to start, I've made it easier for you. I put together a free decluttering guide and checklist with over 100 specific items you can clear out, plus practical tips to keep the clutter from creeping back. And while clutter is one of the easiest mistakes to spot, one of the sneakiest space shrinkers hides in plain sight, your doors. Traditional hinge doors swing open into the room, and that arc eats up valuable floor space. In a very small bedroom, the swing of a door might block where you'd ideally put a dresser, a chair, or even just a clear walkway. If you own your own home and can make changes, consider space-saving options. Pocket doors are a great choice as they slide right into the wall cavity. When they're open, you gain back the whole corner of the room. If a pocket door isn't an option, folding doors can work beautifully. They open wide without swinging fully into the room, which means you can reclaim the area behind them for furniture or storage. And then there're the clever details. A jib or hidden door can disguise messy storage, while a glazed or glass panel door lets natural light pass through, making adjoining rooms feel brighter and more connected. Even painting a door the same color as your walls reduces visual clutter and helps the whole space feel calmer and bigger. Take a look around most rooms and you'll notice a theme. Everything is boxy. Rectangular dining tables, square coffee tables, boxy sideboards. Bringing in curves break up those angles and helps the room flow better. A round dining table is perfect for small spaces because it's easier to walk around. Even a rectangular table with curved edges makes a big difference. Similarly, curved lamps, mirrors, or accessories add movement without eating up extra floor space. These rounded shapes make your room feel more inviting and relaxed. When every piece has sharp corners, the vibe can feel stiff and boxy. Adding a few curves and suddenly the whole space feels lighter and easier to live in. But sometimes it is hard to picture how a new piece will actually look in your space. That is exactly why I built room visualizer AI. You upload a photo of your room and instantly see a completely new look styled to your taste. It's like getting a designer's perspective for your own space. It is in beta right now and invite only, but you can join the waitlist via the link below. And once you've nailed furniture shapes, the last mistake to avoid is stopping here. Because all of these details only work if the entire room feels cohesive. [Music] Now, you can avoid every mistake on this list, but if your room doesn't feel cohesive, it still won't look right. Cohesion is what makes a space feel pulled together instead of random. Without it, the room will feel busy and disconnected. The fix is to create a common thread. That might be repeating a color across different elements, carrying the same wood tone through a few pieces, or using one metal finish consistently. These small things give the room flow, so your eye moves smoothly around the space instead of getting stuck on every little change. Cohesion doesn't mean everything has to match. In fact, too much matching makes the room look flat. It is about balance. Enough similarity to feel intentional and enough variety to keep it interesting. When you get that right, the room feels calm, spacious, and complete. And that is really the secret to small space design. It is not about square footage. It is about making every choice intentional. If you enjoyed this video, check out this video on eight trendy design that makes your home look cheap, or 21 living room design mistakes you might be making. Otherwise, thanks for watching and I'll see you in the next one.
