"Get furniture on legs that you can see underneath! BUT ALSO, get furniture like beds and sofas and ottomans with hidden storage!" YOU CANNOT HAVE BOTH. Beneath-furniture storage almost always descends to the floor, because... where else do you think that storage space is coming from? Begging interior design YouTubers to think critically for a moment.

The next hack is to put all of your furniture on casters. Um, besides maybe your sofa, anything that can move around, we want it to move around. Um, and just make sure they're casters that have an associated break because this will allow you to easily move furniture out of the way to make your small space work for you. The next hack is to get outlet extenders. So, these are really great. They can extend your outlet upwards, to the left, to the right. They really can. I think it's like 270° worth of tilt. But this is great because I feel like a lot of people limit their furniture placement to where they can access outlets. So, they want to have a TV, so they put their furniture in like the worst possible arrangement. But that's fair. You want to be able to watch How to Train Your Dragon. That's what I watched last night. The new version is very good. And this will allow you to extend an outlet without having to place your furniture in weird places so that you can actually choose the layout that is the most aesthetically pleasing, but also keep your space super functional. Another great hack is to attach miscellaneous items to the back of picture frames. So if you have like a deep picture frame, I'm talking like 3 in 4 in, you can use command strips to attach things to it. So that could be your remote. It could be some wires, some cords, something like that. You can use cord management strips on the back. You can use command strips and attach things like that to the back of those picture frames so that you don't have tabletop clutter. Um, and so those things are really beautifully hidden without having to add boxes or something to that effect. And the last hack is only one that you should do if you need it, but if you need like a rolling cart to roll things from room to room, maybe you're breastfeeding, maybe you like to do your skin care in the living room, even though those things are stored in the bathroom, or maybe you just want to have one table and move it all throughout your home. Get a rolling cart, one of those IKEA rolling carts that are really, really popular. Then I want you to add skirting. They now have one from IKEA that has a lid. You can use peel and stick wallpaper on it. You can use contact paper on it. You can paint it. You can stain it. I don't care. And then you can add some type of skirting on it so that it looks like a normal table, but in fact it is like one of those rolling carts. No one has to know because you've done this beautiful skirting. So it looks lux, but it's also super duper functional for your small space and can work in so many different spaces. That's it for today's video. Those are some small space hacks that I know you're thinking, "Oh my god, Ke, I got to run to the store right now, get these items so that I can apply these hacks because they're oh so good."   12. @fionajet312: My daughter has a two bedroom house with no storage there’s literally no place to put anything clutter everywhere x
  13. @chris...9497: The hack for bookshelves works for kitchen cabinets as well, especially the wall cabinets.
Warning: Some cabinets make it very difficult to remove shelves, usually due to the type of clips that hold the shelves in place.
Be prepared to have to remove the cabinet door to slide the shelf out horizontally, because the clip holds the shelf on both bottom and top, so it will not angle up for adjustment or removal. Unscrew the hinge not from the door, but form the cabinet interior wall.

I bought 2 gridded shelving units, something like a small Kallax unit for my livingroom. Each is about 4ft square and is about ten inches deep. It's divided into square spaces four tall and four wide.
I use this as a kind of console table between my sofa and the wall, so I can use the top surface for necessities like a lamp, clock, reading matter, decor, or to rest a drink or snack. I use the lower two levels of spaces for long-term storage and the top two levels for frequent use items like office supplies, art supplies, medicines, and recordkeeping.

I have a magnetic knife holder attached to the inside of the wall cabinet that site directly above where I do my food prep.
It's a strong, quality magnet that came with an inset metal late for attaching to wall or door, however the inset depth matched Alien tape exactly. Alien tape is that double-sided silicon tape that holds very strong, eels off easily without damaging what it sticks to, and can be washed and rinsed and reused with no loss of adhesion.
All you need to do is situate the magnetic bar so the knife handles don't obstruct the shelf edges.
This leaves me clearer countertops and emptier drawers in my kitchen.

I have two doors on my sinkbase cabinet. The right side is where I access cleaning tools and supplies, including a hanging shelf that allows quick grab of scouring pads, sponges, dish soap, and scouring powder. The left side is where I've tucked an Ikea trash container. It rolls out for easy filling or emptying, and has a lid to keep in odors and cradle rolls of garbage bags. The sinkbase is immediately adjacent my prep area, making it quick and easy to clear away offcuts. Also, being inside the cabinet, it frees up my very limited floor space.

I would also recommend some of the expanding width rolling shelves available on Amazon.
The depth of many base cabinets are a challenge to use for frequent use storage, so having a top and a bottom shelf that rolls out for easy access makes life much easier.
Just remember to measure the width of the interior, the width of the cabinet opening, and the depth of the cabinet from back to interior of the face frame, so you can order the correct product.
For renters, yes, you will need to drill a few screw holes, but take the rolling shelves with you when you move and plug the holes with some white toothpaste. Landlords will never look inside a base cabinet, and the damage is negligible.

I was watching you demonstrate use of the outlet extender and noticed you needed two hands, one to steady the movement of the extender.
I would suggest attaching a Command velcro strip between the wall and outlet extender box, to lock it in place.

If you have an accessible flat area, like a sofa support or a coffee table or side table, consider attaching a velcro Command strip to the remote control and flat surface. You wont have to hunt for it if it has a place in reach to stick it.

Do you ever misplace or forget your keys? Try putting a small Command hook above the doorknob of your default entry and exit door.
Place it high enough that you can open the door, but low enough that it brushes against your hand, so it reminds you to grab them.
This is especially useful if your door automatically locks when closed.

I am elderly and live alone in a 320 sqft studio apartment. I did pick up one of those Ikea rolling carts and a wooden lid, and I use it as a side table, a condiment storage, and a food prep area. It's practically hidden at the far end of the livingroom, next to the sofa arm but rolls to the front of the sofa, so it's nearly invisible while still being so handy!

that in the description box of this video okay so day number one you want to inspect the space and secure resources now you may be familiar with the space or you may be entering it for the first time you want to do a complete walkthrough including sheds basements crawl spaces attics garage find out what you see there are there large collections and bulky items like cars or antiques that will need special handling are there cabinets filled with papers photos and small items that are going to be tedious to sort are there categories like clothing linens kitchen items that may not have monetary clear out the four keys so one of the most valuable things I learned from working with the state sellers is that other than the four p's with the exception of trash everything else in the home can be sold so what are the four p's they are photos paperwork perishables and prescriptions so this really changed the way I looked at downsizing homes with enough hired help even the largest estates could be downsized quickly now this does not mean you have to sell everything in the home you know via and a state auction service but it gives you the option and a starting point two of the four PS which are photos and paperwork contain things of such great value that are so tedious to sort that I recommend in an extreme time crunch that you pack these items up into photo boxes and banker boxes and move them to another location or a climate-controlled storage unit when packed properly a small 5x7 storage unit will hold tens of thousands of these documents now you may need to locate certain legal and financial documents and the downsizing process provide a list of these items to your helpers so everyone can search for these items as you pack away the papers to sort when you have more time clearing the other Pease perishables and prescriptions do not typically take as long and can be easily discarded quickly okay let's take a look at day three today is all about picking favorites if you or your loved one is downsizing because of a move now is the time to pick the furniture and other large items that will be accompany them to the new space if the downsizing is due to a passing this is different colors like blue green or yellow I don't recommend putting stickers or regular tape to or using those to mark items because the residue can damage your valuables for large complicated estates I recommend creating a spreadsheet to list all the items and collections including where the item will go eventually and the means by which it will get to its new home if you are downsizing to a smaller home it's helpful to have a floor plan handy and take measurements to assure that everything will fit into the new space ok day for today is about packing you want to bag up the remaining trash and recyclables pack additional items to move to another home or to storage and by the end of the day the only things left in the home should be items to sell or to donate and you want to label boxes clearly like storage unit or Bob's house in addition to notes about the contents don't pack liquids or chemicals as they could spill and damage other items and do not pack in really large boxes unless you're hiring professional movers is easier to store and safer to move smaller boxes if you want it box it up seal it up label it and stack it up now be really selective you only want to pack the things that you will love and use you do not want to create more clutter and overwhelm in another location okay day five today is about moving today you want to have secured charities haulers an auction house to eliminate the remaining items ideally you want to move items that you are keeping out first and this ensures that there's no confusion and that a treasured item it does not get sold or end up at Goodwill some charities offer pickup in my experience they are very selective and have narrow schedules hiring a hauling service ensures that all items are removed on time and you can request that haulers take items to a specific charity or to multiple stops or you can hire a moving company to take one little boxes to storage and the other load to a charity if you are working with an online auction house like Mack sold you can leave items out for the agents to photograph now you may wish to do some sorting and inventory for your own records but do not worry if you run at a time unsold items and a final cleanup you may also wish to have a professional Kru come in after the auction so that the house is ready to go on the market if you are selling the property the important three hundred thousand dollars on our home but are considering selling our home due to the amount of equity we would have this would allow us to downsize from our over three thousand square foot home and invest some of the money as well if we stay in our home there are a lot of major repairs that would need to be done which would total close to one hundred thousand dollars those repairs eat into our monthly savings my husband works from home now and will continue to and i am a stay-at-home mom to our three-year-old we could move anywhere really thanks again for your help and all of your great button subscribe to the channel and then commenting something nice down below thank you very much for considering it okay let's start with pro number one of downsizing less housework as a homeowner housework fills many hours of my day and my week and my month and my year cleaning vacuuming yard work raking weeding bagging lawn stuff all that stuff now there's no dodging this housework when you downsize but there may be a lot less of it for you to do recently my parents downsized from a 3 200 square foot house to a 1300 square foot house the yard work and the housework have gone down significantly for them they spend less time keeping the house and landscaping tidy and more time relaxing exercising volunteering and spending time with their family con number one of downsizing less space now this could really be a pro or a con depending on what your goals are but one thing my parents did say is that they would prefer a little bit more space for hosting at the house so that begs the question then how much square footage is the right amount for a family of three in your situation well the range that i found online is anywhere from 500 to 700 square feet per person might feel comfortable so depending on your lifestyle and your needs and the fact that your husband works from home you might want to find a house in the 1500 to 2100 square foot range now depending on where you are in the country that could seem ridiculous or appropriate if another child is in your future and you become a family of four consider something in the 2000 to 2800 square foot range knowing that that top range is not too far away from your current square footage samantha perhaps the lower side makes more sense for you we have a family of four and our house is around 2 700 square feet right now it feels like the right size for our family in this current stage of life let's recap the home square footage suggested per person just for a fun exercise for somebody obviously this is hotly debated i posted on twitter some people thought it was great some people thought it was ridiculous okay let's do it anyway family of two 1 to 1400 square feet family of three 1500 to 2100 square feet family of four 2000 to 2800 square feet family five 2500 to 3500 square feet and a family of six three thousand to forty two hundred square feet the interesting thing about home ownership just like personal finance is that it's all personal people so these square feet ranges are all well and good but if it doesn't fit your situation then forget these numbers pro number two of downsizing access to equity getting access to hundreds of thousands of dollars is a huge upside to downsizing your home you can use this extra money for so many things like investing for the future paying off debt giving back going on family vacations or even making your new house more comfortable these options can be so plentiful that i'd recommend taking time to sit down and specifically write down what you do with the money and then ask your partner to do the same thing while this potential windfall might feel like a blessing if you two aren't on the same page then it could cause some unintentional marital fights it's funny when we get a lot of money we fight and when we don't have a lot of money we fight right con number two of downsizing selling a house is expensive while on the surface the quick math problem looks like the sale price seven hundred thousand dollars minus the current home mortgage principal three hundred thousand dollars would net you four hundred thousand dollars right well unfortunately that won't be the final amount you'll walk away with you'll want to crunch the numbers on some important costs that go along with selling your house shout out to bankrate.com for these categories and numbers real estate commissions up to six percent of the sale of your home would be 42 000 title insurance which is typically a percentage of the purchase price in this case we'll use one percent that would be 7 000 repairs to your house to get it to sell now this all depends on how much work is needed but even you samantha said it might be a hundred thousand dollars to get it in a good spot that you'd want to live there so i don't know that could be a lot of money right then you got escrow fees you got property taxes you've got things like moving costs think about that attorney fees transfer taxes home staging seller concessions pre-listing home inspection and the list unfortunately goes on and on and on so at the end of the day consider that potentially 100 000 or more of this 400 might go away with just the general costs of selling a home pro number three of downsizing owning a smaller home is less expensive while i've almost talked myself out of this downsizing idea because of the cost to sell the thing it's important to note one of your major complaints samantha it's expensive to maintain this larger 3 000 square foot home right when you own a smaller home you'll potentially have fewer things to fix fewer upgrades to make and fewer costs to incur this all depends on the home you would downsize to but the goal is with a smaller home you'll get smaller problems right to put some math around this idea a general rule of thumb is to save up one to four percent of the value of your home for home maintenance each year for that seven hundred thousand dollar home that's seven thousand dollars to twenty eight thousand dollars per year a few years of not updating your home just like our family has done over the past few years will make that number creep up to 100k real fast let's say you found a three hundred thousand to four hundred thousand dollar home that you were able to buy outright with no mortgage then you'd be able to look at - The Unstoppable Power of Letting Go | Jill Sherer Murray | TEDxWilmingtonWomen: Kind: captions Language: en Translator: Phuong Cao Reviewer: Peter van de Ven Letting go can make you unstoppable. I know because I let go of a relationship and reclaimed my life. And I know that letting go can create the best of change for each and everyone of you. Let me tell you a story. When I was 41, the death of a relationship showed me how to truly let go of what wasn't working. See, up until that time, I didn't really think about the future. I kind of lived my life like a dog - moment to moment. I chased balls, I ate whatever I could find on the ground. (Laughter) And life was good. I had a great job, great friends, a great apartment, a great actual dog, and a great boyfriend. Well, sort of. See, Hector didn't have any skin in the game, and I felt that. He danced around the very idea of marriage, and after 12 years, we didn't even live together. Still, he gave me hope. Well, sometimes. More like living in a situation that had no hope just felt normal. Don't get me wrong. I stayed because Hector was a good looking, smart, reliable, and sensitive guy who cared, and while our relationship wasn't perfect, it worked in theory. Until a wake-up call from a friend changed everything. So my realtor friend called to tell me about a condo coming up for sale in my Chicago neighborhood. She knew that I was looking for Hector to commit and thought, ''Well, maybe this could inspire a little forward motion.'' Still, my first inclination was to say, "We're not ready. Not yet." Haha, "Not yet." That was Hector's favorite catchphrase. I would say, ''I want to get married.'' He'd say, ''Not yet.'' I'd say, ''Let's live together.'' "Not yet." ''Not yet'' was a hair I couldn't get out of my eye and a bad song I couldn't get out of my head. So you could imagine my surprise when he said "Sure'' to meeting me at the condo at noon. Now, I arrived early and eager. But Hector? Haha, well that was another story. 12:15 came. 12:30. 12:45. 1:00 pm. No Hector. Eventually, he called, something had come up. So we agreed to reconvene at three, but Hector was a no-show again. It was in that moment that I decided, after 12 years, it was time to let go. See, I had to let go of Hector and of the idea of marrying him or anybody because at 41, my options were scary. I could either stay with a man who couldn't commit, but was great on all the holidays and birthdays, or I could break up with him and be alone. Not that letting go of a good man I truly loved was easy. No, no. I had to survive the consequences of my epiphany, and that's when the pain stage kicked in. "You and Hector won't be together forever. You won't be his person. In fact, he'll probably meet somebody else fast, marry her, and she'll be his person. And then you're going to have to live with the fact that you made a mistake." I ugly cried, ate a lot of pizza, listened to a lot of Joni Mitchell. And then, when I couldn't rub my eyes anymore without hitting bone or imagine Hector in a wedding photo with another woman, probably in a size-6 dress, I brushed myself off. See, I let go of the fear that I would grow old and die alone, that my friends would use me as a cautionary tale, that it was too late for me. No, no. In that moment, I had to finally admit what I really wanted, which was more. You see, Hector not showing up, that was a gift, and that it gave me freedom because let's face it, I'd been chasing that ball for 12 years. No, no. It was time to move on, even if I risked rejection. So I made a plan, one that got clearer with every step. Of course, Hector had an excuse for his no-show. But by then, it didn't matter. I told him it was over. I quit my job, I hugged my friends, I sold my beautiful condo in the same neighborhood that delivered me that life-changing epiphany. I let go of everything to start a whole new life in New Hope, Pennsylvania. To which he said, ''Don't go, we'll get married.'' To which I said, ''You had twelve years.'' To which he said, ''I'll come visit.'' To which I said, ''Not yet.'' Was it hard? Ha ha, you bet. Was it worth it? Within a year of leaving, I met my husband Dan online. I knew when he showed up for our first date, in the most wrinkled shirt I have ever seen, with a rain hat to keep me dry walking from the restaurant to the car, that this was my guy. You see, umbrellas were for people who wanted distance. Too heavy a wind, you know, turned them inside out, and even the best of them only lasted so long. But a rain hat, you know, the kind with a string, and you tie it under your chin, Now, that's personal. And after four dates, it turned to love. Finally, I understood why I had to wait so long. Dan was handsome and wise and soulful and kind, and he made me feel like I could do and be anything. We could. And we got married a year later. (Sigh) When I turned 50, Hector died of cancer. And as you can imagine, I grieved for a very long time. But his death reaffirmed for me the promise I made to myself when I was 41: that I would never take time for granted again. Instead, I would use it to let go, to create space for the things I really wanted and for what mattered most. Here are five ways to let go I know work because I still use them every single day. One. Let go of taking things personally. I spent a lot of time wondering why Hector didn't love me enough to marry me until I realized that his inability to commit had less to do with me and more to do with his duty to his family. Now, I'm not saying that was not a hard pill to swallow, but there was a lot of peace in knowing that it was his issue and not some defect in me. If people aren't giving you what you want, or if they're just behaving badly, most times, that's their problem, not yours. Two. Let go of what other people think. So after my husband and I dated for a couple of months, I took him home to meet my parents. ''He's very good-looking,'' my mother said. ''You know, Ted Bundy is very good looking.'' Now, I could've let this influence my actions, could have let my imagination run wild with thoughts of my new beau stabbing me while I slept. But instead, I just chalked it up to my mother. There is a rule in business that states: Whenever you are putting something out there, 10% of people will hate it, 80% will be indifferent, and 10% will be your raving fans. And raving fans are awesome, but if somebody's not a raving fan, let that be okay too. Three. Let go of trying to be something you're not. Now, I have this crazy big personality that I actually call "the Big." Some people really like the Big. Some people are fascinated by it, kind of the way they are fascinated by jugglers. And others just run away. But it’s who I am. I have tried to turn down the current on the Big, but hard as I try, there it is. There are some things we just can't change about ourselves, and that's a good thing. Four. Let go of the need to be perfect. Many years ago, I wrote a column for Shape Magazine, and I got a lot of mail from readers, including a very sad letter from this teenage girl asking for my advice on how to improve herself after her absolutely horrid boyfriend had her strip down so he could critique her body. This is a true story. I said, ''Dump him immediately, and never let anybody make you feel bad about yourself again." But we all know that feeling the need for perfection is not just about our weight. It's also about keeping the house clean and the dogs groomed and the kids healthy and the bosses happy and all the balls in the air. It's even about keeping our youth intact. And yet, who wants to be friends with someone who's perfect? Think about that. And lastly, five - my favorite! Let go of ''Not yet.'' You know, when I left Chicago, my life was pretty good, it just wasn't good enough. If there is something you want to do, make a plan and act, but don't wait. I still grieve for Hector, you know, it just comes in waves now. But it’s the phone call I can't make that reminds me to make every day count. And I encourage you all to do the same. Whatever that is, I say, ''Let go for it.'' Thank you. (Applause)

- How to Let Go of People and Situations _ Stoicism & Psychology for Inner Peace: Kind: captions Language: en Letting Go it sounds simple but in practice it's one of the hardest things we can do whether it's people past experiences or overwhelming situations the act of releasing something we're deeply attached to can feel like losing a part of ourselves but here's the truth holding on too tightly often causes more suffering than Letting Go ever will learning to let go isn't about detach M from life it's about freeing yourself from the emotional chains that keep you stuck in this episode we're diving into how to let go of people and situations using insights from stoic philosophy and modern psychology you'll learn how to unlock the power of Detachment when to step back for mental Clarity and how to set healthy emotional boundaries Letting Go doesn't mean giving up it means making space for what truly Matters by letting go you don't lose control you gain the freedom to focus on what truly Matters by the end of this episode you'll have the tools to release what no longer serves you and move forward with a lighter heart greater peace of mind and A Renewed sense of purpose let's begin lesson one unlocking the power of Detachment when most people hear the word detachment they assume it means apathy or indifference caring less feeling less or withdrawing from life but in truth Detachment is not about removing emotions it's about freeing yourself from the need to control things you can't control it's about maintaining your inner peace regardless of what happens around you attachment often comes from fear fear of losing people situations or even vers of ourselves But the irony is the tighter we hold on to something the more it controls us when we become Overly Attached we tie our happiness to external factors other people's opinions the outcome of a situation or material possessions and when those things change or slip away as they inevitably do we suffer stoicism teaches that true Freedom comes not from controlling everything around us but from controlling how we respond epicus a stoic philosopher famously said wealth consists not in having great possessions but in having few wants Detachment in this sense doesn't mean you stop caring it means you stop clinging you stop expecting life to conform perfectly to your desires and instead you learn to adapt to flow with change rather than resist it imagine holding a handful of sand if you grip it too tightly most of it slips through your fingers but if you hold it gently it stays in your palm this is the essence of Detachment it's not about pushing things away it's about holding them lightly without letting them Define your inner state to unlock the power of Detachment start by asking yourself what am I clinging to that's causing me stress or anxiety it could be a relationship where you feel overly responsible for the other person's happiness a situation at work where you're trying to control every outcome or even a past event that you can't seem to let go of recognizing what you're attached to is the first step toward releasing its hold on you next practice mindful acceptance this doesn't mean passively accepting everything that happens it means acknowledging what's outside your control and choosing how to respond for example if someone's behavior is upsetting you instead of trying to change them focus on setting boundaries and managing your own emotions you can care deeply about someone while still maintaining a healthy level of emotional Detachment here's something practical you can do each day take five minutes to reflect on one thing you're attached to that's causing you stress ask yourself is this something I can control or is it something I need to let go of if it's outside your control practice mentally releasing it by saying I choose to let go of what I cannot control this simple exercise helps train your mind to focus on what matters and let go of unnecessary burdens ask yourself am I holding on because I truly care or because I'm afraid of what will happen if I Let Go fear based attachment only leads to suffering while healthy Detachment creates space for growth freedom and peace remember Detachment isn't about abandoning people or responsibilities it's about protecting your inner World from being thrown into chaos by external events it's about showing up fully but without clinging to outcomes when you unlock the power of Detachment you begin to experience life with greater Clarity balance and emotional resilience you free yourself from the weight of things you cannot control and in doing so you find a lighter more peaceful way to live lesson two stepping back for mental Clarity when life feels overwhelming when emotions are high and your mind is clouded it's easy to react impulsively make poor decisions or let stress take over in these moments one of the most powerful things you can do is step back taking a step back isn't about avoidance it's about creating space between your emotions and your actions so you can gain mental Clarity when you're caught up in the intensity of a situation Everything feels urgent your mind races with thoughts your emotions escalate and it becomes harder to think clearly but by pausing and stepping back whether physically mentally or emotionally you give yourself the opportunity to see the situation from a different perspective this practice helps you respond thoughtfully rather than react impulsively imagine a painter standing too close to their canvas up close all they see are individual brush strokes and small imperfections making it difficult to understand the full picture but when they take a few steps back they gain a clearer view of the entire painting the same principle applies to your mind when you step back from overwhelming situations you gain perspective you can see the problem for what it really is rather rather than through the distorted lens of heightened emotions stepping back can be as simple as taking a deep breath going for a short walk or even journaling your thoughts these small actions create mental distance allowing your emotions to settle and your mind to regain its balance once you've created that distance you're in a better position to reflect on what's truly happening and decide how to respond in a way that aligns with your values stoicism teaches us that Clarity comes from calmness Marcus Aurelius wrote you have power over your mind not outside events realize this and you will find strength by stepping back you take control of your mind refusing to let external chaos dictate your internal State this is where real strength lies not in control controlling every situation but in controlling how you perceive and respond to it here's something practical you can do the next time you feel overwhelmed by a situation practice the pause technique before reacting pause for 10 seconds take a deep breath count to 10 or simply close your eyes for a moment in that brief pause ask yourself what's the best response here not the quickest or easiest but the one that aligns with who I want to be this small practice can drastically improve your ability to make thoughtful clear decisions another method is perspective journaling when you're feeling stuck or emotionally charged Take 5 to 10 minutes to write down your thoughts as if you were giving advice to a friend in the same situation this practice helps you step outside your emotions and view the problem from a more objective angle ask yourself am I too close to this situation to see it clearly if the answer is yes step back give yourself the time and space to reflect not every situation requires an immediate reaction and often the wisest actions come after moments of calm reflection remember stepping back isn't a sign of weakness it's a sign of wisdom it's a deliberate Act of choosing Clarity over chaos reflection over reaction by making it a habit to step back when things feel overwhelming you cultivate a calmer more thoughtful approach to life you learn to see challenges for what they are temporary obstacles not permanent problems and in doing so you regain control over your mind and your emotions when you master the art of stepping back you not only make better decisions you also create a life where Peace of Mind becomes your default State no matter what storms life throws your way lesson three deciding when to distance yourself one of the hardest decisions we face in Life Is Knowing When to distance ourselves from people or situations whether it's a draining relationship a toxic work environment or even a personal habit that no longer serves you holding on too long can take a toll on your emotional health but how do you know when it's time to create that distance and more importantly how can you do it in a way that aligns with your values and preserves your peace the first step in making this decision is self-awareness ask yourself is this relationship or situation helping me grow or is it holding me back growth doesn't always feel comfortable so it's important to distinguish between temporary discomfort that leads to Improvement and persistent negativity that drains your energy if something consistently leaves you feeling anxious unappreciated or diminished it might be time to consider creating distance many people struggle with distancing themselves because of guilt you may feel responsible for other people people's feelings or worry that creating distance makes you a bad person but the truth is setting boundaries isn't selfish it's necessary for maintaining your well-being stoicism teaches us that we can't control other people only our own actions and choices epicus said we cannot choose our external circumstances but we can always choose how we respond to them distance ing yourself is a response a deliberate Act of protecting your emotional health it's also helpful to reflect on your values does staying in this situation or relationship align with who you want to be sometimes we stay in unhealthy Dynamics out of habit or fear of change but if staying means compromising your values or well-being it's worth considering a different path remember creating distance doesn't mean you're abandoning someone or giving up it means you're choosing to prioritize your peace and personal growth once you've decided it's time to create distance the next step is to do it quietly and respectfully there's no need for Grand declarations or confrontations unless they're absolutely necessary in many cases simply spending less time with a person or gradually disengaging in from a situation is enough this quiet approach allows you to protect your energy without adding unnecessary drama here's something practical you can do if you're unsure whether it's time to distance yourself try a trial separation give yourself a set period say 2 weeks where you intentionally limit your engagement with the person or situation during this time pay attention to how you feel feel are you calmer more focused do you miss the interaction or do you feel relieved without it this trial period can provide valuable insight into whether creating permanent distance is the right choice ask yourself am I staying out of obligation or because this truly adds value to my life if the answer leans toward obligation or guilt it might be time to take a step back remember you can care about someone and still choose to protect your boundaries distance doesn't erase love or respect it preserves your well-being another key factor to consider is communication while quiet distancing Works in many cases some situations may require an honest conversation if the relationship is worth preserving but needs adjustment consider expressing your feelings and setting clear boundaries boundaries sometimes creating distance isn't about cutting ties it's about redefining the relationship in a way that works for both parties remember deciding when to distance yourself is about choosing what serves your growth and well-being it's not about blame or resentment it's about creating space for clarity peace and personal development when you let go of what no longer serves you you make room for healthier relation ships better opportunities and a life that aligns with your values ultimately life is too short to stay stuck in situations that drain your energy by learning when and how to distance yourself you take an important step toward living with greater purpose peace and self-respect Lesson Four building trust through meaningful conversations when life feels overwhelming one of the most powerful ways to find Clarity and emotional relief is by engaging in deep meaningful conversations with trusted individuals human connection isn't just a source of comfort it's a path to Insight growth and emotional healing talking through your feelings with someone you trust can help you gain New Perspectives challenge negative thought patterns and uncover Solutions you may not have considered on your own but here's the key not every conversation builds trust surface level small talk or exchanges filled with judgment don't lead to real connection trust is built when conversations are honest vulnerable and supportive when both parties feel safe enough to share without fear of being misunderstood or criticized it's about opening up but also about listening deeply and without judgment if in stoic philosophy trust and open dialogue were seen as essential for personal growth the stoics valued conversations that challenge their thinking in a respectful and thoughtful way senica once wrote associate with those who will make a better man of you welcome Those whom you yourself can improve in other words seek out people who encourage your growth not just those who Echo your thought meaningful conversations aren't just about venting they're about learning reflecting and evolving to build trust through conversations it's important to create an environment of mutual respect and empathy this means listening without interrupting validating the other person's feelings and offering support rather than unsolicited advice often people don't need solutions they they need to feel heard and understood simply saying I understand how you feel or I'm here for you can go a long way in strengthening trust if you're seeking clarity about a difficult situation don't hesitate to reach out to someone you trust this could be a close friend a mentor or even a professional counselor the act of speaking your thoughts out loud helps you organize them and hearing an outside perspective can offer insights you may have missed just make sure the person you're opening up to is someone who respects your boundaries and offers a safe space for sharing here's something practical you can do identify one or two people in your life whom you trust deeply the next time you feel stuck or overwhelmed reach out to them for a meaningful conversation start by saying something like I've been feeling Str confused overwhelmed about something and I'd really value your perspective or just someone to talk to framing the conversation this way invites them to listen without feeling pressured to solve the problem at the same time be open to offering support when someone comes to you building trust is a two-way street it's about giving as much as receiving practice being present listening actively and offering EMP empathy when someone shares their struggles with you this reciprocity deepens the connection and creates a foundation of trust that benefits both of you ask yourself do I have trusted individuals in my life with whom I can have meaningful conversations if the answer is yes nurture those relationships if not consider seeking out new connections through supportive communities groups or even professional networks where trust and open dialogue are valued remember trust isn't built overnight it's cultivated through repeated acts of vulnerability empathy and understanding by engaging in meaningful conversations you not only gain Clarity and emotional relief but you also strengthen the bonds that support you through life's ups and downs in a world filled with surface level interactions meaningful conversations are a rare and valuable gift they remind you that you're not alone help you see things from A New Perspective and provide the emotional support needed to navigate challenging Times by building trust and fostering open dialogue you create a network of connection and strength that sustains you through life's inevitable uncertainties lesson five mastering emotional boundaries have you ever found yourself a overwhelmed by someone else's emotions absorbing their stress anxiety or negativity as if it were your own if so you're not alone many people struggle with emotional boundaries the invisible lines that separate your emotions from those of others without these boundaries it's easy to feel drained lose your sense of self and become entangled in problems that aren't yours to solve mastering emotional boundaries doesn't mean shutting people out or becoming indifferent it means learning to protect your emotional well-being while still offering empathy and support when you set healthy emotional boundaries you create a balance between caring for others and caring for yourself you can be compassionate without sacrificing your peace of mind why is this important without boundaries you risk taking on emotional burdens that aren't yours this can lead to emotional exhaustion resentment and even strained relationships stoic philosophy reminds us that while we can care for others we must remain in control of our own inner World epicus said it is not what happens to you but how you react to it that matters this wisdom applies not only to external events but also to the emotions of those around us you can't control how others feel but you can control how much you let their emotions affect you the first step in mastering emotional boundaries is awareness start by noticing when you're taking on emotions that aren't yours ask yourself am I feeling this way because of my own situation or because I've absorbed someone else's emotions recognizing when you're crossing your emotional boundaries is key to regaining control next practice assertive communication setting boundaries isn't about confrontation it's about Clarity for example if a friend frequently unloads their problems on you in a way that leaves you feeling drained you might say I care about you and I want to support you but I also need to take care of my own well-being let's talk but can we keep it brief this approach shows empathy while clearly stating your limits another helpful strategy is to use visualization techniques when you're in a situation where emotions are running High imagine a protective barrier around yourself something that allows you to empathize without absorbing the emotional weight this mental practice helps reinforce the idea that while you can care you don't have to carry here's something practical you can do the next time you feel overwhelmed by someone else's emotions pause and take a deep breath mentally separate your feelings from theirs by saying to yourself their emotions are theirs I can care without carrying then focus on grounding yourself whether through breathing exercises journaling or taking a brief walk ask yourself am I sacrif iing my emotional well-being to support others if the answer is yes remind yourself that setting boundaries isn't selfish it's necessary when you protect your emotional energy you're in a better position to help others without burning out it's also important to remember that boundaries may need reinforcement over time some people might not respect your boundaries right away but that doesn't mean they aren't valid stay consistent the more you practice setting and maintaining boundaries the more others will learn to respect them and the more you'll respect yourself remember mastering emotional boundaries is an act of self-respect and self-care it allows you to show up fully for the people you care about without losing yourself in the process you can be kind empathetic and supportive while still protecting your own peace when you set clear emotional boundaries you free yourself from the weight of emotions that aren't yours to Bear allowing you to live with greater Clarity balance and Inner Strength lesson six weighing the outcomes of Separation there are moments in life when distancing yourself from certain people or situations becomes necessary for your well-being but making that decision isn't easy easy it comes with doubts emotions and concerns about the potential consequences weighing the outcomes of separation is a crucial step in ensuring that you're making the right choice not out of impulse or frustration but with Clarity and purpose distancing yourself can take many forms it might mean ending a toxic relationship leaving a draining job or stepping away from a long-term friendship that no longer aligns with your values whatever the case it's essential to carefully evaluate how this separation will impact not only your life but also the lives of those around you the goal isn't to escape discomfort it's to prioritize your growth well-being and peace of mind stoic philosophy teaches us the importance of intentional decision making before taking action the stoics would reflect on whether a decision aligned with their values and long-term well-being sener advised we suffer more often in imagination than in reality this means that much of the fear around separation is rooted in imagined worst case scenarios by weighing the real outcomes instead of fearing hypothetical ones you can make more grounded thoughtful choices here's how to approach weighing the outcomes one evaluate the emotional impact ask yourself how does staying in this situation Make Me Feel on a daily basis if the answer is consistently negative stress anxiety or a sense of being stuck that's a strong indicator that creating distance might be beneficial two consider your long-term well-being reflect on whether this situation or relationship is helping helping you grow or holding you back will staying here support your future goals and happiness or will it continue to drain your energy three acknowledge potential losses every separation comes with some form of loss it could be emotional social or even Financial be honest with yourself about what you might lose but also ask is this loss worth the peace and Clarity I'll gain sometimes short-term discomfort is necessary for long-term well-being it's also important to remember that separation doesn't have to be absolute in some cases creating distance rather than completely cutting ties may be enough for example if a relationship is draining but not entirely toxic you might choose to spend less time with that person or engage in fewer emotionally charged interactions this allows you to protect your well-being without severing the relationship entirely here's something practical you can do when faced with the decision to create distance try a pros and cons reflection take a piece of paper and divide it into two columns on one side list the benefits of staying in the situation or relationship on the other list the benefits of creating distance seeing these factors laid out visually can help clarify your decision ask yourself am I staying because I genuinely want to or because I'm afraid of the consequences of leaving if fear is the primary reason it's worth reconsidering often the fear of change is worse than the change itself once you take the step you might find that what you feared wasn't as overwhelming as you imagined remember weighing the outcomes of Separation isn't about finding a perfect solution it's about choosing the path that best supports your growth and well-being no decision is without its challenges but by taking the time to reflect and evaluate you ensure that your choice is intentional and aligned with your values letting go is rarely easy but it's often necessary when you carefully weigh the outcomes and make make a conscious decision to create distance you open the door to a lighter Freer life you make room for New Opportunities healthier relationships and a version of yourself that thrives unburdened by what no longer serves you lesson seven detoxing from social media in an age where social media has become an integral part of daily life it's easy to fall into the Trap of constant scrolling comparing and consuming while social media connects us to people and information it also introduces a significant amount of digital noise distractions unrealistic comparisons and even anxiety from endless updates detoxing from social media doesn't mean abandoning it altogether it means creating space for clarity focus and genuine connection by limiting its grip on your life the problem with excessive social media use is that it keeps your mind in a constant state of stimulation every notification post or like triggers a small dopamine hit conditioning you to seek more over time this can create a cycle of dependence where moments of silence or Stillness feel uncomfortable but true peace of mind comes not from external stimulation it comes from learning to be present reflective and content with yourself stoicism emphasizes the value of focusing on what truly matters Marcus Aurelius wrote you must reclaim yourself for yourself the world distracts you with many things focus on a few that matter and let the rest go social media can be a valuable tool but without mindful use it distracts you from what's meaningful real relationships inner peace and personal growth detoxing from social media can take many forms for some it might mean taking a complete break for a few days or weeks for others it could be a simple as setting time limits on daily usage or unfollowing accounts that don't add value to their lives the goal isn't to eliminate social media entirely it's to regain control over how you you engage with it here's how to start a social media detox one identify your triggers notice when and why you turn to social media is it out of boredom stress or habit understanding your triggers helps you find healthier Alternatives like going for a walk reading or journaling that provide genuine relaxation or stimulation two set boundaries create specific times during the day when you'll check social media rather than constantly reaching for your phone for example you might decide to check it only after lunch or in the evening limiting your exposure to digital noise three curate your feed unfollow accounts that make you feel inadequate anxious or drained instead follow those that Inspire uplift or educate you your digital environment should reflect your values not undermine them here's something practical you can do try a 24-hour social media detox for one full day avoid all social media platforms use that time to engage in offline activities spend time with loved ones work on a hobby or simply enjoy The Quiet Moments at the end of the day reflect on how you feel did you miss anything important or did you feel more present less stressed and more focused ask yourself am I using social media intentionally or is it using me if you find that it's consuming more of your time and energy than you'd like commit to creating healthier boundaries remember you don't have to give it up entirely you just need to ensure that it serves you rather than controls you remember detox boxing from social media isn't about isolation it's about reconnection by reducing digital noise you create space for deeper conversations richer experiences and greater peace of mind you learn to be present in your own life rather than constantly watching the highlight reels of others when you take control of how you engage with social media you reclaim your time your focus and your inner calm you free yourself from the constant comparison and distraction allowing room for what truly matters real connection meaningful growth and the quiet powerful moments that shape who you are lesson eight prioritizing your core needs in the rush of everyday life it's easy to lose sight of what truly matters between work obligations social commitments and endless distractions your core needs those things that nourish your emotional mental and spiritual well-being often get pushed aside but here's the truth if you don't prioritize your core needs no one else will learning to identify and prioritize what truly sustains you is essential for building a balanced and fulfilling life many people confuse wants with needs while wants are temporary desires like entertainment validation or material possessions your core needs are deeper they're the things that keep you grounded fulfilled and energized these could be things like meaningful relationships time for self-reflection Creative expression or even simple activities like getting enough sleep and spending time in nature stoicism teaches us to focus on what is essential and and to let go of what is Superfluous epicus said wealth consists not in having great possessions but in having few wants this wisdom reminds us that true fulfillment doesn't come from chasing more but from nurturing the essentials that sustain our inner well-being to prioritize your core needs start by asking yourself what are the things that genuinely make me feel alive and at peace these are often the things that when neglected leave you feeling drained or disconnected for example if quality time with loved ones is a core need for you constantly prioritizing work over relationships will eventually lead to feelings of isolation and dissatisfaction once you've identified your core needs the next step is to make time for them intentionally this might mean setting aside time each day for self-care saying no to commitments that don't align with your values or creating boundaries around your work and personal life remember prioritizing your needs isn't selfish it's necessary when your needs are met you're in a better position to show up fully for the people and responsibilities in your life here's a simple exercise make a list of your top five core needs next to each one write down one small action you can take this week to prioritize that need for example if one of your core needs is rest your action might be committing to going to bed 30 minutes earlier each night if another need is creativity your action might be setting aside 1 hour to work on a personal project the key is to start small and be consistent ask yourself am I living in a way that honors my core needs or am I by constantly sacrificing them for external demands if it's the latter remind yourself that no achievement or external validation can replace the sense of wholeness that comes from meeting your true needs it's also important to let go of guilt when prioritizing your needs many people feel guilty when they take time for themselves as if they're neglecting their responsibilities but taking care of yourself isn't neglect it's what allows you to fulfill your responsibilities without burning out when you prioritize your well-being you not only improve your own life you also show up better for those around you remember life isn't about doing more it's about doing what matters by prioritizing your core needs you create a foundation of balance strength and inner peace you stop chasing after things that don't fulfill you and start living in a way that nourishes your body mind and soul when you make time for what truly matters you don't just improve your quality of life you become more present more resilient and more connected to your purpose and that's when life becomes not just something you manage but something you truly enjoy lesson N9 embracing gratitude and the present moment in a world that constantly pushes us to want more more success more possessions more recognition it's easy to fall into the Trap of focusing on What's Missing rather than what's already present this endless Pursuit often leads to feelings of dissatisfaction anxiety and restlessness but there's a simple yet profound antidote to this gratitude when you learn to embrace gratitude and anchor yourself in the present moment you shift your perspective from lack to abundance from striving to being gratitude isn't about ignoring life's challenges or pretending everything is perfect it's about recognizing the good that already exists even amidst difficulty it's about appreciating the small moments a kind word from a friend a peaceful morning or the fact that you have another day to grow and experience life when you focus on what you have rather than what you lack you cultivate a mindset of abundance and peace stoicism places great emphasis on appreciating the present Marcus aelius wrote do not dwell on the past or dream of the future but concentrate the mind on the present moment the stoics believed that true contentment comes not from what you achieve or acquire but from fully experiencing the Here and Now gratitude is a way of grounding yourself in the present it reminds you that despite life's imperfections there's always something to be thankful for practicing gratitude doesn't have to be complicated one powerful method is to start or end your day by reflecting on three things you're grateful for they don't have to be grand sometimes the simplest things bring the greatest sense of Peace over time this practice trains your mind to notice the good even when life feels overwhelming Beyond gratitude embracing the present moment means letting go of past regrets and future worries the past can't be changed and the future is uncertain what you have control over is the present the choices you make the actions you take and the way you experience this very moment when you let go of dwelling on what was or obsessing over what might be you free yourself to fully live in the now here's something practical you can do the next time you feel stressed or overwhelmed pause for a moment take a deep breath look around you and find something anything you can appreciate in that moment it could be the warmth of sunlight the taste of your coffee or even the the strength you've shown in handling a difficult situation this simple Act of mindful gratitude helps ground you and brings a sense of calm to your mind ask yourself am I focusing more on What's Missing or what's already present if you find that your mind often leans toward lack make a conscious effort to shift toward gratitude it's not about ignoring your goals or desires it's about balancing them with appreciation for what you already have another way to stay present is by practicing mindfulness whether through meditation deep breathing or simply being fully engaged in what you're doing mindfulness helps you slow down and Savor life's moments it's about noticing the details how the air feels on your skin the sound of the wind the texture of the food you're eating these small acts of presence bring richness and depth to Everyday Life remember life isn't lived in the past or the future it's lived in the present by embracing gratitude and staying anchored in the moment you free yourself from the constant Chase for more and find peace in what is you realize that while life may never be perfect it can still be beautiful when you take the time to notice and appreciate it in the end gratitude and presence aren't just practices they're mindsets when you cultivate them you create a life filled with more joy less anxiety and a deeper connection to what truly matters so take a moment breathe deeply and ask yourself what can I appreciate right now you might be surprised by how much there is to be thankful for lesson 10 cultivating flexibility and adaptability life is unpredictable no matter how carefully you plan or how hard you try to control outcomes unexpected changes will always arise it's in these moments when things don't go according to plan that your ability to adapt determines whether you thrive or suffer cultivating flexibility isn't about giving up on your goals or lowering your standards s it's about learning to adjust your approach stay calm in the face of uncertainty and find growth in every challenge many people resist change because it feels uncomfortable we like stability predictability and control but rigidity in the face of change creates unnecessary suffering when you refuse to adapt you cling to what was instead of embracing what is this resistance often leads to frustration anxiety and a sense of helplessness on the other hand when you cultivate flexibility you learn to flow with life's changes finding New Paths forward even when things don't go as planned stoicism offers Timeless wisdom on this topic epicus taught it's not what happens to you but how you react to it that matters this idea hi highlights the importance of flexibility not in avoiding change but in mastering your response to it when you accept that change is inevitable and learn to adapt with Grace you maintain your inner peace and Clarity no matter what life throws your way flexibility starts with shifting your mindset instead of seeing change as a threat view it as an opportunity for growth every unexpected event every setback holds a lesson if you're willing to look for it ask yourself what can I learn from this situation how can I grow through this challenge this shift in perspective helps you stay open to new possibilities rather than getting stuck in frustration over what didn't go according to plan another key to cultivating flexibility is developing emotional res resilience life's changes often trigger strong emotions fear frustration or disappointment instead of letting these emotions take over practice pausing acknowledging how you feel and then choosing a response that aligns with values emotional resilience doesn't mean ignoring your feelings it means processing them in a healthy way while staying grounded and clear-headed when faced with an unexpected change practice the 3 A's accept assess adapt one accept that the change has happened resisting it won't undo it two assess the new situation what are your options and what can you control three adapt by choosing the best course of action based on what's in your control this simple framework helps you respond to change thoughtfully rather than reacting impulsively out of fear or frustration ask yourself am I clinging to something that no longer serves me or am I willing to adapt and move forward if you find yourself resisting change remind yourself that flexibility doesn't mean giving up it means finding a new way to keep going life is const constantly evolving and your ability to evolve with it is what leads to Lasting success and peace of mind cultivating flexibility also means being open to New Perspectives and ideas sometimes we get stuck because we insist on doing things a certain way or believing that only one path will lead to success but when you're willing to listen learn and try new approaches you open doors to opportunities you might never have considered remember flexibility isn't weakness it's strength it's the ability to bend without breaking to adjust without losing your sense of purpose just like trees that Sway in the wind are less likely to snap during a storm those who cultivate flexibility are better equipped to handle life's inevitable ups and downs in the end life is a ser series of changes by cultivating flexibility and adaptability you learn to navigate those changes with confidence and Grace you stop fighting against what you can't control and start focusing on what you can your mindset your actions and your ability to keep moving forward and in doing so you create a life of resilience growth and Lasting inner peace lesson 11 fostering empathy and generosity in a fastpaced world where everyone seems to be focused on personal goals and individual success it's easy to forget the profound impact of empathy and generosity yet these two qualities have the power to not only transform your relationships but also bring greater meaning and fulfillment to your life fostering empathy and practicing generosity aren't signs of weakness they're expressions of inner strength self-respect empathy is the ability to understand and share the feelings of others it goes beyond simply acknowledging someone's experience it involves truly listening without judgment and offering genuine support when you Foster empathy you strengthen your connections with those around you people feel seen heard and valued which Fosters trust and deeper relationships stoicism often emphasizes personal resilience but it also highlights the importance of contributing to the well-being of others Marcus Aurelius wrote what benefits the whole benefits the individual in other words by acting in ways that uplift others you also uplift yourself empathy isn't just about helping others it's about creating a world where mutual respect and compassion flourish which benefits everyone including you generosity on the other hand isn't just about giving material things it's about giving your time attention kindness and wisdom true generosity comes from a place of abundance not obligation when you give freely without expecting anything in return you cultivate a mindset of abundance and reduce feelings of scarcity or lack here's something to reflect on when was the last time I showed empathy or practiced generosity without expecting anything in return these small acts whether it's listening to a friend helping a colleague or simply offering a kind word to someone in need create ripples that extend far beyond the moment they uplift not only the person receiving but also the one giving but fostering empathy and generosity doesn't mean neglecting your own well-being in fact empathy begins with yourself if you're constantly giving without taking care of your own needs you'll eventually feel drained and resentful that's why it's important to practice self-compassion being kind to yourself when you're struggling forgiving yourself when you make mistakes and treating yourself with the same empathy you offer others another powerful aspect of generosity is that it helps shift your focus outward when you're caught up in your own worries giving to others whether it's through volunteering mentoring or simply being present can help reduce anxiety and increase your sense of purpose generosity creates a cycle of positivity the more you give the more connected and fulfilled you feel here's something practical you can do each day look for one opportunity to practice a small Act of empathy or generosity it could be as simple as listening to someone without interrupting offering help when you see someone struggling or even just sending a thoughtful message to someone you care about these small acts done consistently build a habit of empathy and generosity that strengthens your character and enriches your relationships ask yourself am I approaching my interactions with empathy or am I focused solely on my own perspective if you find yourself slipping into self-centered thinking pause and try to see things from the other person's point of view this simple shift can transform the way you relate to others and Foster deeper more meaningful connections remember fostering empathy and practicing generosity isn't about grand gestures it's about showing up with kindness and understanding in everyday moments when you make empathy and generosity a core part of your life you don't just improve your relationships you cultivate a sense of purpose fulfillment and inner peace in the end what truly matters isn't how much you accumulate but how much you contribute by fostering empathy and practicing generosity you create a life of connection meaning and lasting impact you don't just live for yourself you become a source of light and strength for those around you and in doing so you discover a deeper sense of happiness and [Music] fulfillment Lesson 12 nourishing your mind and body in a world that constantly demands your attention energy and effort it's easy to neglect the most important foundation of a fulfilling life peace of mind slips away nourishing your mind and body is isn't a luxury it's a necessity for living a balanced resilient and meaningful life when you take care of your body you're not just enhancing how you feel think and respond to Life's challenges senica once said A sound mind in a sound body is a short but full description of a happy state in this world the stoics believed that a disciplined approach to caring for your body was essential for cultivating a strong mind nourishing your mind goes beyond simply consuming information it's about feeding your mind with thoughts ideas and practices that promote growth and peace this means being intentional about what you read watch and listen to it means engaging in practices like mindfulness meditation and reflection that help calm time these small acts compound creating a lifestyle that supports your well-being ask yourself am I treating my mind and body with the respect they deserve if not start today remember you can't pour from an empty cup taking care of yourself isn't selfish it's what allows you to show up fully in life for both yourself and those you care about in the end your mind and body are your most valuable and get the laundry done before the next guest comes at three o'clock and it was a disaster i do not recommend you doing it that way i feel like it was a huge mistake of ours once i realized after a place clean and spotless for the next guest and you wouldn't believe the amount of laundry there is this farmhouse is four bedrooms guys you got all the duvets all the comforters all the pillowcases the top sheet the fitted sheet you got the bath mats you got all the bath towels the face towels the hand towels you got the the linens in the kitchen the tea towels all of that stuff has to get laundered in between every guest now we have doubles of everything so that you can just quickly set up the rooms but you still need a place to do the laundry we have our laundry facility in the basement but it still takes hours and hours to get the laundry done i tell you that so definitely give yourself a two night minimum the second lesson i learned guys you need to focus on reviews people they search your listing and they want to check out the reviews if you got a bunch of bad reviews or you have very few reviews that may affect your chances what i do now when i'm interacting with guests because we have the benefit of being a farm stay so when we have guests i'm basically here every day i chit chat with the guests every day it's not a hands-off operation at all i'm here every day and i'm very very hands-on but i do have the benefit of chit-chatting with the guests every single visit so i actually asked them now how was your stay guys you have a really good time is there anything that we could improve on what do you think you know what do you think about giving us a good review there so you know it there's there's some there's somewhat of an expectation now because i've had that little 10 second dialogue with them hey this guy needs some reviews you need the reviews so that you'll show up higher in the search when someone's searching your town and your number of bedrooms and they're searching to list things you want to show up high in the search gonna help you and the higher the average review number the better so we're pretty close to five right here on the farm because my number one priority is to ensure that the guests have the most amazing stay and recharge themselves at the farm another thing i learned is instant booking make sure you turn that on instant booking basically guarantees a great user experience by the guest they can search your listing and if they want a book all they do is they book now they have to have a verified account which makes it easier for you because you're not just booking these weird strangers you're actually instantly booking verified guests and that's important but what it does for you is it gives you bookings instantly there's none of this back and forth they don't have to ask questions they don't have to try to negotiate because everything's listed in your listing everything boom instantly book and that's the way to go because if you start getting all these back and forth from lots of different guests it's going to take a lot of time off your hands you don't want that instant booking is the way to go the fourth lesson that i learned guys is that you have to keep updating your calendar and when i say calendar i mean the entire listing put a bunch of new photos on there change the actual name you know we're the hidden spring farm but i don't put the hidden spring farm in the listing i might put something like uh you know super private tranquil farm stay because you really want to attract people who are looking for private tranquil getaways it helps keep your listing fresh and it helps push the listing it helps keep you up on top of the search and that's what you want i learned this the hard way because i went months and months and months after i first uploaded our photos and i created the listing it just basically went stagnant we were getting bookings here and there but not as much as you do if you keep refreshing your calendar so i kind of feel like that's another lesson learned and it's very important for anybody who's new to this short-term vacation rental stuff a fifth and final lesson that i've learned since starting this vacation rental is not to chance out on the cleaning supplies so here's the quick story what happened with us within our first six guests of renting out this farmhouse we lost a bottle an entire big jug of hand sanitizer it was like a 25 bottle one of the guests stole it i mean you got your shampoos you got your conditioners you got your soaps you got your dish soap you got your dishwasher stuff you got your laundry stuff so basically we had jugs of it out and what it did for us is it walked and it was a lesson learned so what we ended up doing after that experience was that we locked everything up all of the cleaning supplies got locked up and that created an even bigger problem because customers started asking and we don't want that we want to be able to provide everything that they could possibly need during their stay to make it seamless and to have a great experience plan that we came up with was for them to have a little bit of every single cleaning supply you could think of but only in small quantities so we basically learned keep a little bit out and give the quantity of whatever the cleaning supply is based on the length of the booking and another example is in our early days of running this business we had a cupboard full of paper towel you know just rolls a paper towel okay well there was like one guest and like it was gone there was like half a roll left so during their three night stay they used six rolls of paper towel you know and and what i learned after the fact from that guest after confronting them about it is that they used all the paper towel to start a fire in the fire pit is it my responsibility to leave some kind of fire starter or paper or something for them to get a fire going or should they be bringing all that so i decided that it's my responsibility because i want them to be able to function like they're living in the house so again lesson learned so now all the guests have access to a little bit of cleaning supplies we've had some longer term guests too and i give them access to a mop i give them access to a dust pan and a broom i give them access to a vacuum because if they're staying here for a month which is great i love to have a month booking and if the guest wants to clean by all means i don't want to stop you because cleaning is the most expensive part of running a short-term rental so if the guest wants to actually get some cleaning done that's amazing give them the tools to do it i'm going to give you a little bonus tip here too guys our first couple of guests we had a key lock just like an old school deadbolt walk and after the guests misplaced a key i realized time to upgrade so what we have is this little digital keypad thing here and they just type in the four digit code it's wi-fi enabled i can change the code whenever i want in between guests and it's really handy and all they need is a call they don't there's no keys you don't want to be fumbling with keys so i guess that's six lessons learned i mean there's a whole lot of lessons that i learned starting up this farm stay but the reality is we could be here for 40 minutes talking about all the lessons that i've learned if anybody's thinking about starting a short-term rental whether it's in your apartment whether it's in your house whether it's on your farm it can be done and do a little bit of research don't be like me and learn the hard way but i'm loving what we're doing here at the farm and it's been really fun and i'm going to continue doing
